The rhetorical question follows: 'What great nation has statutes as righteous as all this law?' (v. 8). The implied answer positions Israel as a model society that draws the nations to inquiry about YHWH.
4.7: ki mi-goy gadol asher-lo Elohim qerovim elav kaYHWH Eloheinu bekhol-qor'enu elav: 'For what great nation has a god as close to it as the LORD our God is to us whenever we call upon him?' Qerovim elav — near to it/him. YHWH's accessibility is one of two distinguishing marks of Israel's God (the other being the righteous Torah, v. 8). Accessibility on demand (bekhol-qor'enu elav, in all our calling to him) distinguishes YHWH from the distant, unpredictable gods of Mesopotamia. The NT applies the same accessibility to Christ as the mediator who gives believers direct access to the Father (Heb 4:16: 'approach the throne of grace with confidence'; Rom 8:15-16: Abba — Father).
4.8: umi goy gadol asher-lo huqqim umishpatim tzaddiqim kekhol-hatorah hazot asher anokhi noten lifneikhem hayom: 'What great nation has statutes and rules as righteous as this entire law I am setting before you today?' The comparative claim for Torah's righteousness (tzaddiqim) is bold: no other legal corpus matches the ethical quality of YHWH's covenant law. In Paul's framing (Rom 7:12: 'the law is holy, righteous, and good') this uniqueness of Torah is affirmed even as Paul argues for the law's limited ability to produce the righteousness it demands — a limitation that Christ's spirit fulfills (Rom 8:4).
4.9: raq hishamer lekha ushemor nafshekha me'od pen-tishkah et-hadevarim asher-ra'u einekha: 'Only be careful, and guard yourselves diligently, lest you forget the things your eyes have seen.' The double imperative — hishamer (guard yourself) and shemor nafshekha (guard your soul/life) — and the modifier me'od (very, diligently) signal the seriousness of memory as a covenant obligation. Pen-tishkah (lest you forget) — the danger of forgetting is not merely cognitive but covenantal: to forget YHWH's acts is to lose the basis for covenant loyalty. Deuteronomy's entire structure as a repeated recitation of history serves this anti-forgetting purpose.
4.10: yom asher amadta lifnei YHWH Eloheicha beHorev be'amor YHWH elai haqhel-li et-ha'am ve'ashmi'em et-devarai: 'Tell them about the day you stood before the LORD your God at Horeb, when the LORD said to me: Gather the people to me and I will let them hear my words.' The Horeb assembly (yom asher amadta lifnei YHWH) is the constitutive event for all subsequent generations. Haqhel-li et-ha'am (gather the people to me) — the assembly before YHWH is a paradigmatic act that the NT picks up: Jesus gathers his disciples to deliver the greater Torah in the Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5:1-2), explicitly evoking the Sinai/Horeb gathering.
4.11: vatiqrevu vata'amdu tahat hahar vehahar bo'er va'esh ad-lev hashamayim choshekh anan va'arafel: 'And you came near and stood at the foot of the mountain while the mountain burned with fire up to the heart of heaven — darkness, cloud, and thick darkness.' The triple darkness vocabulary — choshekh (darkness), anan (cloud), arafel (thick cloud/darkness) — marks the Horeb theophany as both terrifying and bounded. YHWH is present but hidden; audible but not visible. The same combination (cloud + fire + voice) structures the NT transfiguration (Matt 17:5: 'a bright cloud overshadowed them, and a voice from the cloud said...') — the Son's identity disclosed where Moses's identity was established.
4.13: vayagged lachem et-berito asher tzivah etchem la'asot aseret hadevarim vayikhtevem al-shney luhot avanim: 'He declared to you his covenant that he commanded you to keep — the Ten Commandments — and he wrote them on two stone tablets.' Aseret hadevarim (the ten words) — not 'commandments' but 'words' (devarim), the same term for Deuteronomy's title (the 'words' of Moses). The Decalogue is the covenant treaty-document written by YHWH himself on stone (luhot avanim): both the durable medium and the divine author distinguish it from all other law. Paul's contrast in 2 Cor 3:3 (not on stone tablets but on tablets of human hearts) invokes this distinction: the new covenant internalization of Torah surpasses the externally written Decalogue.
4.14: ve'oti tzivvah YHWH baat hahi lelammed etchem huqqim umishpatim: 'And the LORD commanded me at that time to teach you statutes and rules for you to carry out in the land you are entering to possess.' Moses's teaching role is here explicitly commanded, not self-appointed. Lelammed (to teach) — the Piel of lamad, the intensive teaching verb from which Talmud derives. Moses is the first Torah-teacher, and his model establishes the Deuteronomic-prophetic tradition of instruction that culminates in the Teacher who is greater than Moses (Matt 23:10: 'you have one teacher, the Christ').
4.15: venishmartem me'od lenafshoteichem ki lo re'item kol temunah beyom dibber YHWH aleichem beHorev mittokh ha'esh: 'Therefore watch yourselves very carefully. Since you saw no form on the day the LORD spoke to you at Horeb out of the midst of the fire.' The explicit grounding of the second commandment (no images, vv. 16-18) in the invisibility of Horeb is Deuteronomy's unique contribution to Israelite anti-idol theology. Lo re'item kol temunah (you saw no form at all) — the totalizing negation (kol) rules out any partial or symbolic representation. YHWH's hiddenness is permanent, not temporary; the command against images is therefore permanent.
4.16: pen-tashkitun va'asitem lachem pesel temunat kol-semel tavnit zakhar o neqevah: 'Do not act corruptly by making for yourselves a carved image in the form of any figure — the likeness of male or female.' Tashkitun — the root shahat (to destroy, corrupt) — making an image is destroying one's relationship with YHWH. The enumeration of prohibited image-types (human, animal, bird, fish, heavenly bodies — vv. 16-19) is exhaustive: every category of created being that ancient Near Eastern cultures worshipped is explicitly forbidden. The prohibition is not aesthetic but covenantal: to represent YHWH as any creature is to misidentify him fundamentally.
4.17: tavnit kol-behemah asher baaretz tavnit kol-tzippor kanaf asher ta'uf bashamayim: 'The likeness of any animal on the earth, the likeness of any winged bird that flies in the sky.' The anti-image catalog continues. Egypt's theriomorphic gods — bull-headed, falcon-headed, crocodile-headed deities — are precisely the image-forms prohibited here. Israel came out of a culture saturated with animal-deity iconography; the second commandment is a counter-cultural demand rooted in YHWH's transcendence. The golden calf incident (Exod 32) — occurring immediately after the Decalogue — shows how deep the pull toward animal imagery ran in Israel.
4.18: tavnit kol-romes baaadamah tavnit kol-dagah asher-bamayim mitahat laaretz: 'The likeness of anything that creeps on the ground, the likeness of any fish in the water beneath the earth.' The four-zone enumeration (human/animal, bird, creeping thing, fish) corresponds to the Genesis 1 creation account's four creature categories. The anti-image prohibition thus maps onto creation itself: every domain of the created order has been worshipped by humans; none represents YHWH. The comprehensive prohibition is implicitly a confession of creation theology: YHWH is the Creator of all categories and is therefore represented by none of them.
4.19: upen-tissa einekha hashamayima uva'ita et-hashemesh ve'et-hayareaah ve'et-hakokhavim kol-tzva hashamayim venidahta vehishtahavita lahem uvedita: 'Beware lest you lift your eyes to heaven and see the sun and moon and stars — all the host of heaven — and be drawn away to bow down and serve them.' Tzva hashamayim (host of heaven) — the astral pantheon of Babylonian and Canaanite religion, which Israel repeatedly adopted (2 Kgs 17:16; 21:3; Jer 19:13). Venidahta (and be drawn away) — the root nadah means to be thrust aside, led astray by attraction. The danger is visual: the beauty of celestial objects becomes a spiritual enticement. Deuteronomy's warning predicts precisely the apostasy of the divided monarchy.
4.20: ve'etchem laqah YHWH vayotzeh etchem mikkur habarzel miMitzrayim: 'But the LORD has taken you and brought you out of the iron furnace, out of Egypt.' Kur habarzel (iron furnace) — the smelting furnace is an image of intense, refining affliction. Egypt as iron furnace appears also in 1 Kgs 8:51 (Solomon's prayer) and Jer 11:4. The metallurgical image implies that Israel's suffering in Egypt was not accidental but purposeful: YHWH was forging a people. La'am nachala (to be a people of inheritance) — Israel is YHWH's nachala (inheritance, treasured possession), the very term used in Ps 2:8 of the nations given to the Messiah as inheritance.
4.21: veYHWH hit'anef-bi al-divreichem vayishava'a asher-lo e'evor et-haYarden: 'Furthermore, the LORD was angry with me on your account and swore that I would not cross the Jordan.' Hit'anef — the Hithpael of anaph (to be angry) means to become intensely angry against oneself's interlocutor. The use of the reflexive-intensive form intensifies the anger. Vayishava (and he swore) — YHWH's anger becomes an oath: Moses's exclusion is now irrevocable, sealed by divine vow. The phrase al-divreichem (on account of your words/matters) explicitly places the blame on Israel's corporate sin — Moses is punished representatively, bearing consequences for the people's faithlessness.
4.22: ki anokhi met baaretz hazot eini over et-haYarden ve'atem overim virishatem et-ha'aretz hatovah hazot: 'I must die in this land; I am not to cross the Jordan. But you shall cross over and possess that good land.' The contrast is stark and deliberate: anokhi met (I die) / ve'atem overim virishatem (you shall cross and possess). Moses's death enables Israel's inheritance — a pattern that the NT applies to the death of Christ: 'unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone, but if it dies, it bears much fruit' (John 12:24). The leader who cannot enter makes room for the one who can bring them in.
4.23: hishamru lachem pen-tishkehu et-berit YHWH Eloheichem asher karat imachem: 'Be careful not to forget the covenant of the LORD your God that he made with you.' The covenant (berit) is the object of potential forgetting — not merely a law or a command, but a relationship. Pen-tishkehu (lest you forget) — the very thing that makes idolatry possible is covenant amnesia: when YHWH's acts and character slip from memory, the vacuum is filled by attractive false alternatives. The entire rhetoric of Deuteronomy is designed to prevent this forgetting through repeated recitation, ceremony, and written inscription.
4.25: ki tolid banim uvnei vanim venoshantem baaretz vehishkatem va'asitem pesel temunat kol va'asitem hara be'einei YHWH Eloheicha lehakh'iso: 'When you have children and grandchildren and have grown old in the land, and if you act corruptly by making a carved image in the form of anything — doing what is evil in the eyes of the LORD your God so as to provoke him.' The prediction of future apostasy frames it as a covenantal inevitability rooted in human nature: prosperity and generational distance from founding events breed forgetfulness and corruption. Lehakh'iso (to provoke him to anger) — the root ka'as (to be angry, to be provoked) is the covenantal term for Israel's persistent rebellion as a domestic provocation against YHWH.
4.26: ha'idoti vachem hayom et-hashamayim ve'et-ha'aretz ki avod tovdun maher me'al haaretz asher atem overim et-haYarden shammah lerishta: 'I call heaven and earth as witnesses against you today that you will quickly perish from the land.' Ha'idoti vachem et-hashamayim ve'et-haaretz — the treaty-curse invocation of cosmic witnesses is standard suzerainty-treaty form: heaven and earth are summoned to testify to the covenant's terms and to observe any violation. The same invocation opens the covenant litigation in Deut 30:19 and Isa 1:2, where YHWH sues Israel before the cosmic court. The witnesses of heaven and earth give the covenant a universal, public character: Israel's faithlessness is not a private matter but a cosmic event.
4.27: vehefitz etchem YHWH baamim venishartem met'ei mispar baggoyim asher yeneheg YHWH etchem shammah: 'The LORD will scatter you among the peoples, and you will be left few in number among the nations where the LORD drives you.' Vehefitz etchem (he will scatter you) — the root putz (to scatter, disperse) is the exile vocabulary. Moses's prediction of the Diaspora anticipates the Assyrian and Babylonian deportations (2 Kgs 17:6; 25:11) with precision. Gibbur YHWH — YHWH himself as the agent of exile. This is the covenant's own sanction: YHWH does not abandon his people when they are exiled; he is the one who exiles them, retaining sovereignty even in judgment.
4.28: va'avadtem sham elohim ma'asei yedei adam etz va'even asher lo-yir'un velo yishme'un velo yokhelu velo yerihun: 'And there you will serve gods made by human hands — wood and stone — that can neither see, nor hear, nor eat, nor smell.' The idol-polemic (wood-and-stone gods that lack all sense and life) is a characteristic prophetic refrain (Ps 115:4-7; 135:15-17; Isa 44:9-20; Jer 10:3-5). Deuteronomy's irony is sharp: Israel, who heard YHWH speak out of fire (v. 12), will end up worshipping objects that cannot even smell. The contrast between the living, speaking, hearing YHWH and the senseless idols is Deuteronomy's ultimate anti-idolatry argument.
4.29: uviqashtem misham et-YHWH Eloheicha umatzata ki tidreshenu bekhol-levavcha uvkhol-nafshecha: 'But from there you will seek the LORD your God and you will find him, if you search for him with all your heart and with all your soul.' The restoration promise embedded in the exile prediction: uviqashtem... umatzata (you will seek and you will find). The condition — bekhol-levavcha uvkhol-nafshecha (with all your heart and all your soul) — is the positive formulation of the Shema's love command (6:5). YHWH is findable (matzata) precisely in exile, from the scattered condition — the locus of finding is not the temple or the land but the posture of whole-heart seeking. James 4:8 ('draw near to God and he will draw near to you') is a NT application of this promise.
4.30: batzar lekha umtza'uha kol-hadevarim ha'eleh be'aharit hayamim veshavta ad-YHWH Eloheicha: 'When you are in distress and all these things have come upon you, in the latter days you will return to the LORD your God.' Be'aharit hayamim (in the latter days) — a temporal phrase that in the prophets takes on an eschatological dimension: the 'latter days' are the time of YHWH's definitive act of restoration (Isa 2:2; Mic 4:1; Jer 23:20; 30:24). Moses here already anticipates not merely a historical return from exile but the eschatological return to YHWH that the prophets unfold. The NT presents this eschatological return as inaugurated in Christ's person and mission.
4.31: ki El Rachum YHWH Eloheicha lo yarpekha velo yashkhitekha velo yishkah et-berit avoteicha asher nishba lahem: 'For the LORD your God is a merciful God. He will not abandon you or destroy you or forget the covenant with your fathers that he swore to them.' El Rachum — YHWH as the compassionate/merciful God (rachum from rechem, womb) is the self-disclosure of Exod 34:6-7, the great divine name-proclamation. The restoration after exile is grounded not in Israel's merit but in YHWH's covenantal faithfulness and compassion. The triple negation — lo yarpekha, lo yashkhitekha, lo yishkah (will not abandon, will not destroy, will not forget) — emphasizes the irreversibility of YHWH's covenantal commitment.
4.32: ki sh'al-na liyamim rishonim asher hayu lefanekha lemi'an hayom asher bara Elohim adam al-haaretz: 'For ask now about the former days, before you, since the day God created mankind on the earth.' The appeal to universal history — from creation to the present — as evidence for YHWH's uniqueness. Hayom asher bara Elohim adam (the day God created mankind) — the creation is the starting point for historical inquiry. Moses challenges his audience to survey all of human history and find a parallel to Horeb: has any other nation's god spoken from fire, or taken one nation out of another? The implied answer grounds Israelite monotheism not in abstract philosophy but in historical uniqueness.
4.33: heshama am qol Elohim medabber mitokh ha'esh ka'asher-shamata attah vayehi: 'Did any people ever hear the voice of God speaking out of the midst of fire, as you have heard, and survive?' Vayehi (and survive/live) — the astonishing fact is not just that Israel heard the divine voice but that they survived it. In the ancient Near East, direct encounter with the divine was universally assumed to be lethal; the survivor of a theophany is exceptional (cf. Gideon's fear in Judg 6:22-23; Isaiah's terror in Isa 6:5). Israel's corporate survival of Horeb is therefore evidence of YHWH's grace, not just his power.
4.34: o hanisoah Elohim lavo laqahat lo goy miqerev goy bemassot be'otot uvemoftim uvemilhamah ubeyyad hazaqah uv'izzroa netuyah uvmora'im gedolim kekhol asher-asah lachem YHWH Eloheichem beMitzrayim le'einekha: 'Or has any god ever attempted to go and take one nation out of the midst of another nation?' The Exodus is cited as the unique redemptive act in all of history: seven categories of divine action are listed (tests, signs, wonders, war, mighty hand, outstretched arm, great terrors) — the same combination of terms that becomes liturgical in the Passover Haggadah. The redemption of Israel from Egypt is the OT's defining act of salvation; the NT presents the cross-and-resurrection as the new Exodus that this event typified.
4.36: min-hashamayim hishmi'echa et-qolo leyasserecha uve'al haaretz her'echa et-isho haggedolah: 'Out of heaven he let you hear his voice to discipline you, and on earth he showed you his great fire.' The two modes of revelation — voice from heaven (min-hashamayim) and fire on earth (ue'al haaretz) — are distinguished: the voice disciplines (leyasserecha), the fire demonstrates holiness. Yasar (to discipline, instruct) is the word for fatherly moral education (Prov 3:11-12; 19:18) — YHWH's voice at Horeb was not mere command but formative, parental instruction. Heb 12:18-21 uses the Horeb fire and voice as the counterpoint to the better access believers have through Christ.
4.37: vetahat ki ahav et-avotekha vayivchar bezaro aharav vayotziakha lefanav bekoho hagadol miMitzrayim: 'And because he loved your fathers and chose their descendants after them, he brought you out of Egypt with his great power.' The theological sequence is decisive: election precedes exodus. Ki ahav et-avotekha (because he loved your fathers) — the Exodus is a consequence of prior covenantal love, not a new initiative. Vayivchar bezaro aharav (and he chose his descendants after them) — Israel's election is carried in the bloodline of the patriarchs as a matter of covenantal continuity. Paul's treatment of election in Romans 9-11 engages directly with this Deuteronomic framework.
4.38: lehoresh goyim gedolim va'atzumim mimekha milfanekha lehavi'akha latet lekha et-artzam nachala kayom hazeh: 'To drive out before you nations greater and mightier than you, to bring you in and give you their land as an inheritance, as it is today.' Nachala (inheritance) — the land is not conquered property but covenantal inheritance, a gift that YHWH gives from his sovereign ownership. The phrase kayom hazeh (as it is today) grounds the theological claim in observable political reality: the land-gift is not a future hope but a present fact. The NT uses the same inheritance language for the spiritual blessings of the new covenant (Eph 1:11,14,18: 'the riches of his glorious inheritance').
4.39: veyadata hayom vahashevota el-levavecha ki YHWH hu ha-Elohim bashamayim mima'al ve'al-ha'aretz mitahat ein od: 'Know therefore today, and take it to heart, that the LORD is God in heaven above and on the earth beneath; there is no other.' The formula YHWH hu ha-Elohim (YHWH — he is God) appears most dramatically at Mount Carmel, where the people respond to Elijah's victory over Baal's prophets with the same confession (1 Kgs 18:39). Bashamayim mima'al ve'al-ha'aretz mitahat (in heaven above and on earth beneath) — the comprehensive sovereignty covers the entire cosmological vertical axis. The double-directional claim rules out any local or partial deity: YHWH is God of the entire created cosmos.
4.40: veshomarta et-huqqav ve'et-mitzvotav asher anokhi metzavkha hayom asher yitav lekha velvanekha aharekha: 'Therefore keep his statutes and commandments that I am giving you today, so that it may go well with you and your children after you.' The concluding exhortation of the theological section (vv. 1-40) brings the argument full circle: from imperatives to history to theology and back to imperatives. Asher yitav lekha (that it may go well with you) — the covenant's functional logic: obedience generates flourishing, not as mechanical cause-and-effect but as covenantal alignment between a people and the God who designed them. The phrase recurs throughout Deuteronomy as the motivational signature of the covenant.
4.41: az yavdil Moshe shalosh arim be'ever haYarden mizrahah shamesh: 'Then Moses set apart three cities in the east beyond the Jordan.' The legal provision for cities of refuge (Numbers 35; Deut 19) is here implemented for the Transjordanian territory. Yavdil (set apart) — the same verb used for separating the sacred from the profane (havdil in Lev 10:10; the Havdalah ceremony). The cities of refuge are a covenantal institution that protects the inadvertent killer from blood vengeance. Their theological function is to preserve the purity of the land by distinguishing murder from manslaughter.
4.42: lanus shammah rotzeah asher yirzah et-re'ahu bildi da'at: 'So that a manslayer could flee there — anyone who kills his neighbor unintentionally without prior enmity.' The legal definition — bildi da'at (without knowledge/intent) — distinguishes accidental homicide from premeditated murder. The cities of refuge embody a principle of legal mercy: intentionality matters in YHWH's justice. This principle runs through the NT (the parable of the talents presupposes degrees of culpability; Jesus's distinction in John 9:41 — 'if you were blind you would have no sin') and shapes Christian ethics' treatment of motive alongside act.
4.43: Betzer bamidbar bearetz hamishor laRe'uveni veRamot beGil'ad lagGadi veGolan baBashan laMenuasshi: 'Bezer in the wilderness on the tableland for the Reubenites; Ramoth in Gilead for the Gadites; and Golan in Bashan for the Manassites.' The three named cities correspond to the three Transjordanian tribal territories. Bezer, Ramoth, and Golan form a geographic distribution that ensures no Transjordanian manslayer is too far from refuge. Ramoth-Gilead becomes significant in Israel's later history (1 Kgs 22; 2 Kgs 8-9) as a contested border city with Aram — its role as a city of refuge makes its later military loss particularly symbolic.
4.44: vezot haTorah asher-sam Moshe lifnei benei Yisra'el: 'This is the law that Moses set before the people of Israel.' The colophon-formula introduces what follows (vv. 44-49) as the superscription to the main Torah speeches of chs. 5-26. Zot haTorah (this is the Torah) — the demonstrative zot (this) points forward to the entire body of instruction. The formula is liturgical: the same zot haTorah is used in the synagogue when the Torah is lifted after reading. The written Torah as a defined, demarcated body of instruction is Deuteronomy's great contribution to Israelite religion.
4.45: eleh ha'edot vehahuqqim vehamishpatim asher dibber Moshe el-benei Yisra'el betzetam miMitzrayim: 'These are the testimonies, the statutes, and the rules that Moses spoke to the Israelites after they came out of Egypt.' The three-term typology — edot (testimonies, covenant ordinances), huqqim (fixed statutes), mishpatim (case-law rules) — is Deuteronomy's standard legal taxonomy, also found in Ps 119 where the three terms appear throughout the alphabetic meditation on Torah. The geographic tag (betzetam miMitzrayim, after their coming out of Egypt) situates all of Torah in the Exodus context: the law is given to a redeemed people, not as a mechanism of earning redemption.
4.46: be'ever haYarden beghai mul Beit-Pe'or be'eretz Sihon melekh haEmori asher yoshev beHeshbon: 'East of the Jordan in the valley opposite Beth-peor, in the land of Sihon king of the Amorites, who lived at Heshbon.' The geographic specification grounds the entire legal corpus in a historical moment: Torah was given in a specific place (Beth-peor valley), in a specific political context (the just-conquered land of Sihon). Law is not abstract; it is given to a particular people in a particular historical situation that shapes its application.
4.47: vayirshu et-artzo ve'et-aretz Og melekh-haBashan shney malkhei haEmori asher be'ever haYarden mizrah-shemesh: 'They had taken possession of his land and the land of Og king of Bashan — the two Amorite kings east of the Jordan.' The paired conquest — Sihon and Og — is cited again as the territorial context for the following Torah speeches. The repetition is not careless; it serves the document's legal function of establishing the standing of the recipient people: they are a people who have received YHWH's promises, received YHWH's word, and already experienced YHWH's faithfulness in battle.
4.48: me'Aro'er asher al-sfat nahal Arnon ve'ad-Har Siyyon hu Hermon: 'From Aroer on the rim of the Valley of the Arnon as far as Mount Sirion — that is, Hermon.' The parenthetical identification — hu Hermon (that is, Hermon) — clarifies that the Amorite name Sirion (3:9) refers to the same peak as Hermon. The geographic precision defines the full extent of Transjordanian Israel from the Arnon (south) to Hermon (north) — the entire territory that sets the stage for the covenant-giving speeches.
4.49: vekhol-ha'Aravah ever haYarden mizrahah ve'ad yam ha'Aravah tahat ashdot haPisga: 'Along with all the Arabah east of the Jordan as far as the Sea of the Arabah, under the slopes of Pisgah.' The Arabah, the Jordan Rift Valley, and Pisgah frame the setting of Deuteronomy's speeches. Pisgah is the site from which Moses will view the promised land before his death (34:1) — by mentioning it in the geographic frame of ch. 4, the narrator foreshadows Moses's end. The entire legal section of Deuteronomy (chs. 5-30+) is thus delivered in the shadow of Moses's impending death — a last testament given from the edge of the promised land.

5.1: שְׁמַע יִשְׂרָאֵל (shĕmaʿ yiśrāʾēl) — "Hear, O Israel." Moses opens with the same summons that frames the great commandment (6:4) and Israel's foundational confession. Shema (H8085) is more than hearing; it implies attentive obedience — "hear and do." The call to all of Israel (kol-yiśrāʾēl) stresses the covenant community as a whole, not merely its leaders.
5.2: כָּרַת (kārat, H3772) — "cut a covenant": the standard Hebrew idiom for covenant-making, rooted in the sacrificial rite of cutting animals (Gen 15:10). The covenant was made at Horeb (the Deuteronomic name for Sinai), tying the legal content of chs. 5–26 to the historical event of the Exodus generation's encounter with YHWH.
5.3: The claim "not with our fathers but with us" resists covenant-as-inheritance in a passive sense. Every generation stands directly before YHWH as the covenant party. Anachnu (we) is emphatic: the covenant is not a legal artifact passed down but a living relation each generation must own. This logic drives Moses's renewal-preaching in Deuteronomy as a whole.
5.4: פָּנִים בְּפָנִים (pānîm bĕpānîm) — "face to face": the most direct possible mode of divine communication. The same phrase describes Moses's unique prophetic access (34:10; Num 12:8). The people's experience at Sinai/Horeb — hearing the divine voice from fire — was not mediated by human intermediary at that moment; the mediation (v5) came because of their fear afterward.
5.5: עָמַד בֵּין (ʿāmad bên) — "I stood between": Moses's intercessory position is both spatial (between the people and the divine fire) and covenantal (mediating word). The same verb root ʿmd describes the priest standing before YHWH to minister. Moses's role combines prophetic (declaring the word), priestly (standing between), and quasi-judicial (bringing YHWH's verdict to the people) functions.
5.6: The preamble to the Decalogue — "I am YHWH your God who brought you out" — precedes all commands. This is the standard suzerainty-treaty order: the great king identifies himself and cites his past benefactions before issuing stipulations. The commands that follow are responses to prior grace, not conditions for earning it. Bêt ʿăbādîm ("house of slavery") is Israel's pre-covenant identity; the commands define who the redeemed are now.
5.7: עַל פָּנַי (ʿal pānāy) — traditionally "before me" but literally "against my face" or "to my face." The idiom may mean "in my presence" (no other gods where I am present, i.e., anywhere) or "in addition to me" (no gods as rivals). The spatial sense reinforces that the prohibition is not merely theoretical monotheism but relational exclusivity: the God who redeemed Israel will not share the covenant relationship.
5.8: פֶּסֶל (pesel, H6459) — a graven/carved image; תְּמוּנָה (tĕmûnāh) — a likeness or form. The prohibition covers both making and possessing images. The scope is comprehensive: heaven above, earth beneath, waters under earth — the three tiers of the ancient cosmology. The concern is not merely idolatry toward foreign gods but any visual representation of YHWH himself (Deut 4:15-16: "you saw no form").
5.9: אֵל קַנָּא (ʾēl qannāʾ) — "a jealous God." Qannāʾ (H7067) is used exclusively of YHWH in the OT and denotes the exclusive claim of the covenant relationship — not petty envy but the righteous intolerance of covenant violation. The "visiting iniquity of fathers on children" clause reflects corporate solidarity: covenant faithfulness (or unfaithfulness) has generational consequences. The contrast in v10 — "steadfast love to thousands" — shows the asymmetry: punishment extends to 3-4 generations; ḥesed extends to thousands.
5.10: חֶסֶד (ḥesed, H2617) — steadfast love, covenantal loyalty. The contrast between the third-to-fourth generation of v9 and "thousands" here is deliberately asymmetric: divine judgment is limited and proportional; divine ḥesed is extensive and generous. The recipients are those who "love me and keep my commandments" — the covenant-loyal community. Ḥesed is not unconditional sentimentality but the faithfulness of a covenant partner who rewards the faithful.
5.11: לַשָּׁוְא (laššāwĕʾ, H7723) — "for emptiness/worthlessness/falsehood." The prohibition covers not only profanity (using YHWH's name as a curse or oath-word) but invoking the divine name for empty, false, or deceptive purposes — swearing falsely, invoking YHWH to guarantee a lie, or treating the divine name as a magical formula. Shāwĕʾ has the semantic range of "vain," "worthless," "false" — the name of the living God is not to be wielded for worthless ends.
5.12: שָׁמוֹר (shāmôr) — "observe, guard." In Exod 20:8, the command is zākor ("remember") the Sabbath. Deuteronomy substitutes "observe/guard" (shāmôr) — not a contradiction but a complementary nuance: Exodus ties Sabbath to creation-memory (God rested); Deuteronomy ties it to liberation-memory and the ethic of rest for all, including slaves (v14-15).
5.13: Six days of labor (ʿāsîtā kol-mĕlākĕtekā) are affirmed, not merely permitted: work is part of the created order and covenant faithfulness. The Sabbath is the seventh-day cessation from the six-day norm, not a replacement for it.
5.14: The Deuteronomic Sabbath extends rest explicitly to the ben-amatkā (your male slave) and bat-amatkā (female slave), and to the sojourner (gēr) within the gates. Exodus 20 lists the same beneficiaries but in a more compressed form. The humanitarian motivation — "so that your slave may rest as you do" — is characteristically Deuteronomic, grounding law in empathy rooted in Israel's own experience of servitude.
5.15: זָכַרְתָּ כִּי עֶבֶד הָיִיתָ (zākarttā kî ʿebed hāyîtā) — "remember that you were a slave." The Sabbath in Deuteronomy is grounded in the Exodus, not creation (Exod 20:11). Memory of servitude becomes the ethical foundation for extending rest to those who serve. The "mighty hand and outstretched arm" (yād ḥăzāqāh ûzĕrôaʿ nĕṭûyāh) is a fixed formulaic pair for the Exodus event — it appears frequently in Deuteronomy as the experiential basis for covenant obligation.
5.16: כַּבֵּד אֶת אָבִיךָ וְאֶת אִמֶּךָ (kabbēd ʾet-ʾābîkā wĕʾet-ʾimmekā): "Honor your father and your mother." Kabbēd (H3513, piel of כָּבַד) means to make heavy/weighty, i.e., to treat as significant and authoritative — not merely affection but social recognition of parental authority. The attached promise — "that your days may be long and that it may go well with you" — is the first commandment with a promise (Eph 6:2-3), and Paul cites it as still applicable to NT believers under a new framework.
5.17: לֹא תִרְצָח (lōʾ tirṣaḥ): The verb rāṣaḥ (H7523) is not the general term for killing (hārag or mût) but the specific term for unlawful homicide — murder, manslaughter requiring legal process (Num 35:16-28). It does not prohibit all killing (capital punishment and holy war are commanded elsewhere); it prohibits the taking of human life outside the covenant community's authorized frameworks.
5.18: לֹא תִנְאָף (lōʾ tinʾāp): nāʾap (H5003) — sexual betrayal of the covenant. The sexual act here is covenantally framed: marriage is the covenant between husband and wife; adultery violates both the human covenant and Israel's self-understanding as YHWH's covenant-spouse. The prophets use adultery as the primary image for Israel's idolatry (Hosea, Ezekiel) — the two transgressions share the same covenantal structure.
5.19: לֹא תִגְנֹב (lōʾ tignōb): gānab (H1589) — to steal, but also to deceive or act covertly. Rabbinic tradition reads this commandment as prohibiting kidnapping (carrying a person off to slavery) rather than property theft, since property theft has its own specific legislation. On either reading, it protects the physical and social integrity of the covenant neighbor.
5.20: עֵד שָׁוְא (ʿēd shāwĕʾ) — "a worthless/false witness." Deuteronomy uses shāwĕʾ (worthless/false) in place of Exodus 20:16's šeqer (lying). The distinction may be slight, but shāwĕʾ adds the nuance of testimony that is empty or groundless, not just deliberately false. Legal testimony in the covenant community carries divine weight; groundless accusation corrupts justice at its foundation.
5.21: Deuteronomy uses two different verbs in v21 where Exodus 20:17 uses one. For coveting a neighbor's wife: חָמַד (ḥāmad, H2530) — to delight in, to desire with pleasure. For coveting a neighbor's house, field, etc.: תִּתְאַוֶּה (titʾawweh, from אָוָה H183) — to wish for, to crave. The choice of two different verbs may distinguish relational desire (for a person) from acquisitive desire (for property), or may simply be stylistic variation. In either case, both forms of covetousness are prohibited as violations of the neighbor's whole life and estate.
5.22: קוֹל גָּדוֹל (qôl gādôl) — "a great voice": the divine voice at Sinai/Horeb is a sustained, overwhelming auditory experience. Welōʾ yāsāp — "he did not add more," implying these ten words are the complete, sealed direct divine speech. The Decalogue is uniquely distinguished: written on stone by God, spoken directly to the whole assembly without human mediation. Everything else in the law came through Moses.
5.23: The elders and tribal heads (rāʾšê šibṭêkem) approach Moses — the leaders acting for the people as a whole. The "darkness" (ḥōšek) and "cloud" (ʿānān) are the typical theophanic veil: YHWH's presence manifests in phenomena that conceal as they reveal, protecting the people from the unmediated divine glory.
5.24: כְּבֹדוֹ וְאֶת גָּדְלוֹ (kĕbōdô wĕʾet-godlô) — "his glory and greatness." Kābôd (H3519) is the weightiness, radiance, manifest importance of YHWH's presence; gādal (H1431) is the extensiveness of his power and works. The people's admission — "we have heard his voice from the fire" — confirms the unique standing of the Sinaitic theophany in Israel's history.
5.25: The people's fear of death if they continue to hear YHWH's voice directly becomes the occasion for Mosaic mediation. "This great fire will consume us" — the divine holiness is lethal to those without adequate standing before it. This is not pusillanimity but accurate anthropological assessment: the creature cannot sustain direct confrontation with uncreated holiness without a mediating structure.
5.26: אֱלֹהִים חַיִּים (ʾĕlōhîm ḥayyîm) — "the living God": this title distinguishes YHWH from dead idols that cannot speak. The question "who among all flesh has ever heard the voice of the living God speaking from fire and lived?" marks the Sinai theophany as existentially unique. The answer is: only Israel, and they survived only through YHWH's mercy.
5.27: דַּבֵּר אַתָּה (dabbēr ʾattāh) — "you speak to us." The people's delegation of hearing to Moses is the foundation of the prophetic institution. Moses as the covenant mediator becomes the pattern for all subsequent prophets, and ultimately for the prophet-like-Moses still to come (18:15). The people's request is honored: YHWH approves it (v28) and establishes Moses as the permanent mouthpiece.
5.28: YHWH's response — "they are right in all they have spoken" — validates the people's instinct. Their fear is not faithlessness; their delegation to Moses is appropriate. The divine approval of the mediatorial structure is significant: YHWH himself institutes the pattern of indirect revelation through a human spokesperson, which becomes the constitutive structure of the prophetic office.
5.29: מִי יִתֵּן (mî yittēn) — the idiom of wishing: "O that they had such a heart as this always." The divine wish expresses genuine pathos: YHWH desires the people's sustained covenantal fidelity but recognizes they do not have the heart for it. This verse is the OT's sharpest pre-Jeremiah expression of the new covenant problem — external law without transformed heart is structurally insufficient. Jeremiah 31:33 and Ezekiel 36:26 will promise the solution: a new heart.
5.30: The command "return to your tents" disperses the assembly back to domestic life after the theophanic encounter. The juxtaposition with v31 ("but as for you, stand here with me") underscores Moses's unique station: Israel returns to ordinary life; Moses remains in the divine presence to receive the full law.
5.31: אֶת כָּל הַמִּצְוָה (ʾet kol-hammitsĕwāh) — "the whole commandment." The singular mitswāh followed by the plural ḥuqqîm ûmishpāṭîm (statutes and rules) reflects Deuteronomy's structure: the one commandment (covenant loyalty) expressed in many stipulations. The law is a unity with a variegated expression.
5.32: לֹא תְסוּרוּ יָמִין וּשְׂמֹאול (lōʾ tāsûrû yāmîn ûśmōʾl) — "do not turn aside to the right or to the left." This formula for covenant faithfulness — walking the exact path without deviation — recurs throughout Deuteronomy and the Deuteronomistic History. It becomes the criterion for evaluating the kings of Israel: did they walk in the ways of David, or did they turn aside?
5.33: The goal of covenant obedience is life: lĕmaʿan tiḥyûn ("so that you may live"). Deuteronomy is consistently shaped by the life-or-death stakes of covenant keeping. The choice will be formalized in 30:15-20 as the covenant's climax: life and good, or death and evil. Obedience is not the condition for earning life but the path within which covenant life is sustained.

7.1: The list of seven nations — Hittites, Girgashites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, Jebusites — is the standard Deuteronomic enumeration of Canaan's pre-Israelite inhabitants. The number seven suggests completeness; these are all the peoples of the promised land. Waḥărēm taḥărîm ʾōtām ("you must completely destroy them") introduces the ḥerem (H2764) principle — consecrated/devoted destruction — which is theologically and historically one of the most demanding elements of Deuteronomy.
7.2: חֵרֶם תַּחֲרִים (ḥērem taḥărîm) — the infinitive absolute intensifying: "you shall utterly devote to destruction." Ḥērem designates something removed from ordinary use and dedicated to YHWH, whether by destruction or consecration. In warfare, it means the complete removal of the enemy from the land without taking spoil, because the land itself belongs to YHWH and the inhabitants' corruption is total. "You must not make a covenant (lōʾ tikrōt lāhem bĕrît) with them" — covenant-making is the opposite of ḥērem; it would assimilate Israel to the very idolatry it must purge.
7.3: The intermarriage prohibition is grounded in v4: the danger is not ethnic contamination but theological assimilation. "They will turn your sons away from following me to serve other gods" — the household becomes the entry point for covenant unfaithfulness. The prohibition is directional: the problem is not the foreign person but the foreign worship they would introduce.
7.4: The causal chain: intermarriage → turning your sons → serving other gods → YHWH's anger → swift destruction. The speed of the sanction (mĕhēr, quickly) reflects Deuteronomy's concern that covenant apostasy does not have a long runway — once the heart has turned, the consequences follow.
7.5: The commanded destruction of Canaanite religious infrastructure — altars (mizbĕḥōtāyhem), sacred pillars (maṣṣĕbōtām), Asherah poles (ʾăshērāyw), and carved images (pĕsîlêhem) — is a systematic elimination of the material culture of Canaanite religion. These objects represent the rival system that would compete for Israel's covenantal allegiance. The positive command — "burn the carved images with fire" (v25) — makes clear that the destruction is itself a liturgical act of covenantal purification.
7.6: סְגֻלָּה (sĕgullāh, H5459) — "treasured possession." The root suggests wealth closely guarded; it appears in Mal 3:17 ("they shall be mine, says the LORD of Hosts, my special possession"), Ps 135:4, and 1 Pet 2:9 (where Peter applies it to the NT church: "a people for his own possession," laos eis peripoiēsin). Sĕgullāh is Israel's most intimate covenantal title — not merely God's people but his personally treasured possession, set apart from all peoples of the earth.
7.7: לֹא מֵרֻבְּכֶם (lōʾ mērubbĕkem) — "not because you were more numerous." The negative election theology here is rare in world religions: Israel is chosen not because of size, strength, or virtue but despite being the least of peoples (haqqāṭōn mikkol hāʿammîm). This undercuts all ethnic pride and establishes election as pure divine initiative.
7.8: מֵאַהֲבַת יְהוָה אֶתְכֶם (mēʾahăbat Yhwh ʾetkem) — "because the LORD loved you." The reason for election is YHWH's own love (ʾahăbāh, H160) — a circular, irreducible reason: YHWH loves Israel because he loves Israel (cf. Deut 10:15). The second reason — keeping the oath sworn to the fathers — grounds the love in covenant faithfulness across generations. Both reasons are internal to YHWH's character, not external to Israel's merit.
7.9: הָאֵל הַנֶּאֱמָן שֹׁמֵר הַבְּרִית וְהַחֶסֶד (hāʾēl hanneʾĕmān shōmēr habbĕrît wĕhaḥesed) — "the faithful God who keeps covenant and steadfast love." Three terms define YHWH's covenantal character: neʾĕmān (faithful, trustworthy, H539), bĕrît (covenant, H1285), ḥesed (steadfast love/loyalty, H2617). Together they describe a God whose commitment to the covenant relationship is structurally reliable — not capriciously revoked by Israel's failures but faithfully maintained. "To a thousand generations" (lĕʾelep dôr) is the eschatological horizon of covenant faithfulness.
7.10: The balancing clause: YHWH "repays those who hate him to their face" (mĕšallēm lĕśōnĕʾāyw ʾel-pānāyw lĕhaʾăbîdô). The divine faithfulness in v9 is matched by a corresponding divine righteousness — YHWH does not ignore those who hate him. The covenant has two faces: blessing for the faithful, sanction for the hostile. "He will not delay" (lōʾ yĕʾaḥēr) — the divine patience has limits with those who actively oppose the covenant.
7.11: The conclusion of the "faithful God" section (vv7-11): "therefore keep the commandments, statutes, and rules." The indicative of divine election and faithfulness (vv7-10) grounds the imperative of human obedience (v11). The logic is always this order: who YHWH is and what he has done → what Israel is to do. Command follows grace.
7.12: עֵקֶב (ʿēqeb, H6118) — "because of, as a result of." The term can mean "heel" (the track of the heel, hence the consequence of walking in a certain path). The covenant blessings follow Israel's obedience as the track follows the foot — not because obedience earns blessing but because walking the covenant path leads through covenant blessing. Shāmar (to keep) + ḥāsēd (YHWH's loyal love) — the correspondence of human keeping and divine keeping is the covenant's relational structure.
7.13: וְאֵהֵבְךָ וּבֵרַכְךָ וְהִרְבֶּךָ (wĕʾēhēbĕkā ûbēraḵĕkā wĕhirbĕkā) — "he will love you, bless you, and multiply you." Three verbs of divine action flowing from covenant faithfulness: love (prior commitment renewed in blessing), bless (material and social flourishing), multiply (generational continuity). The blessings listed — womb, ground, grain, wine, oil, herd, flock — are the fundamental fertility goods of agricultural life.
7.14: בָּרוּךְ תִּהְיֶה מִכָּל הָעַמִּים (bārûk tihyeh mikkol hāʿammîm) — "blessed more than all peoples." The comparative "more than" (mikkol) situates Israel's covenant blessing in the context of the nations. This is not ethnocentrism but covenant distinctiveness: the people chosen to bear the divine name will be visibly blessed as a sign of YHWH's faithfulness — which is itself the means of the nations eventually coming to know him.
7.15: מַחֲלֶה מִצְרַיִם (maḥăleh miṣrāyim) — "the diseases of Egypt." The covenant guarantee includes freedom from the diseases that afflicted Egypt — likely including the plagues but also the endemic diseases of the Nile region. YHWH's healing protection is the positive counterpart to the military protection of holy war.
7.16: וְלֹא תַחוֹס עֵינְךָ עֲלֵיהֶם (wĕlōʾ tāḥôs ʿênĕkā ʿălêhem) — "do not look on them with pity." Ḥûs (H2347) means to have regard for, to spare emotionally. The prohibition against emotional pity toward the nations targeted for ḥērem is not cruelty but the recognition that sentiment-driven compromise would undermine the very purpose of the conquest: not expelling the false worship from the land. "And do not serve their gods — for that would be a snare to you": the warning is ultimately self-protective, not triumphalist.
7.17: The rhetorical question "these nations are more powerful than I" is addressed before the emotion arises. Deuteronomy consistently anticipates the fear and doubt that will arise when Israel faces the militarily superior nations of Canaan and provides the corrective before the crisis comes.
7.18: זָכֹר תִּזְכֹּר (zākōr tizzĕkōr) — infinitive absolute + finite verb: "remember carefully/diligently." Memory of what YHWH did to Pharaoh and Egypt is the antidote to fear of the Canaanite nations. The Exodus is not ancient history but the ongoing credential of the God who fights for Israel.
7.19: הַמַּסֹּת הַגְּדֹלֹת אֲשֶׁר רָאוּ עֵינֶיךָ (hammassōt haggĕdōlōt ʾăsher rāʾû ʿênêkā) — "the great ordeals your eyes saw." The appeal is to eyewitness testimony: the signs, wonders, mighty hand, and outstretched arm are not mythology but narrated history to which the living generation can testify. This empirical grounding of faith is characteristic of Deuteronomy's rhetorical strategy.
7.20: הַצִּרְעָה (haṣṣirʿāh) — "the hornet." The divine hornet sent among the survivors is a mysterious reference — possibly the Egyptian military campaigns that weakened the Canaanites before Israel's arrival, or a literal insect agent (as in Exod 23:28; Josh 24:12), or a metaphor for divine panic-inducing judgment. Whatever the referent, it represents YHWH's own initiative supplementing Israel's military effort: the conquest is jointly divine-human.
7.21: אֵל גָּדוֹל וְנוֹרָא (ʾēl gādôl wĕnôrāʾ) — "a great and awesome God." Nôrāʾ (H3372, the Niphal participle of yārēʾ, to fear) means fear-inducing, awesome in the sense of evoking reverent dread. The phrase recurs in Neh 1:5; 9:32 and Dan 9:4 — a liturgical formula for the God who is terrifying in power and faithful in covenant.
7.22: The "little by little" (mĕʿaṭ mĕʿaṭ) conquest strategy reflects ecological wisdom: complete and immediate depopulation would leave the land to wild animals. The divine timing is not weakness but prudent management of a complex transition. This pragmatic note within the theology of holy war is characteristic of Deuteronomy's realistic ethical vision.
7.23: מְהוּמָה גְדֹלָה (mĕhûmāh gĕdōlāh) — "great panic/confusion." Mĕhûmāh (H4103) is the divinely-sent panic that immobilizes the enemy, making military defeat possible. The same term appears in the description of divine warfare throughout the OT (1 Sam 14:15; Zech 14:13). It is YHWH's weapon before Israel strikes: the conquest is YHWH's victory first, Israel's second.
7.24: מָחָה אֶת שְׁמָם (māḥāh ʾet šĕmām) — "erase their name." The erasure of a name from "under heaven" is the reversal of covenant existence: to have a name is to have continuing presence and legacy; to have one's name erased is complete covenant annihilation. This is the ultimate ḥērem sanction.
7.25: פְּסִילֵי אֱלֹהֵיהֶם תִּשְׂרְפוּן בָּאֵשׁ (pĕsîlê ʾĕlōhêhem tisrĕpûn bāʾēsh) — "burn the carved images of their gods with fire." The fire-destruction of idols is the inverse of YHWH's theophanic fire: the divine fire that spoke from Horeb now destroys the images that would rival YHWH. Tôʿēbāh lYhwh ("a detestable thing to the LORD") — the same term (tôʿēbāh, H8441, abomination) used for Israel's dietary prohibitions and sexual violations: idols belong to the same category of covenant-contaminating objects.
7.26: חֵרֶם תִּהְיֶה כָּמֹהוּ (ḥērem tihyeh kāmōhû) — "you will be devoted to destruction along with it." The principle of contamination-by-association: bringing a ḥērem-object into the household transfers the ḥērem status to the household. This is not magic but covenantal reality: the object that belongs to YHWH for destruction cannot be assimilated to the covenant community without destroying that community. The story of Achan (Josh 7) is the narrative demonstration of this principle.

14.2: ki am qadosh atah l'YHWH Eloheicha uvcha bachar YHWH lihyot lo l'am segulah mikol ha'amim asher al-pnei ha'adamah (kî ʿam qādôš ʾattāh laYhwh ʾĕlōhêkā ûbĕkā bāḥar Yhwh lihyôt lô lĕʿam sĕgullāh mikkōl hāʿammîm ʾăšer ʿal-pĕnê hāʾădāmāh): 'For you are a people holy to the LORD your God, and the LORD has chosen you to be a people for his treasured possession, out of all the peoples who are on the face of the earth.' Am qadosh (holy people) and am segulah (treasured possession/people of special property) are Deuteronomy's twin covenant-identity terms. Segulah (also Exod 19:5; Deut 7:6; 26:18; Mal 3:17) derives from a root meaning private or personal property that one keeps close — it describes the intimate ownership-affection that distinguishes this people from all others. Peter applies both terms directly to the church (1 Pet 2:9-10): a holy nation, a people for YHWH's own possession. The dietary laws that follow in 14:3-21 are grounded in this identity: what Israel eats is shaped by who they are — a people set apart for YHWH.
14.21: lo-tevashel gedi bachalev imo (lōʾ tĕbaššēl gĕdî baḥălēb ʾimmô): 'You shall not boil a young goat in its mother's milk.' This brief prohibition (repeated verbatim in Exod 23:19; 34:26) is the ancient scriptural basis for the rabbinic separation of meat and dairy foods (basar bechalav). Its precise rationale is debated: (1) a Canaanite fertility-ritual prohibition (though no such Ugaritic ritual is clearly attested); (2) a humanitarian principle against mixing life-food (milk) with death-food (meat) especially from the same animal; (3) a holiness-distinction principle consistent with Deuteronomy's separation-categories. The threefold repetition across Torah gives it canonical weight beyond its brevity. The NT never addresses it directly, but Paul's principle in 1 Cor 8-10 (idol food) applies similar logic: covenant identity shapes eating practices, and the offense to conscience may matter more than the intrinsic act.
14.1: banim atem laYHWH Eloheichem: 'You are children (bānîm) of the LORD your God.' The familial designation opens the purity laws: the food restrictions are grounded not in hygiene or arbitrary rules but in Israel's identity as YHWH's children (bānîm). The father-son relationship with YHWH is a Deuteronomic theme (1:31; 8:5; 32:6) that the NT applies to believers — 'you are all sons of God through faith' (Gal 3:26). The mourning-rites prohibited (titgodedu, gashing yourselves; shim tusumu qorcha, shaving the front of the head for the dead) are Egyptian and Canaanite funerary practices that blurred the boundary between Israel and surrounding nations. The prohibition grounds: if you are YHWH's children, you do not mourn as those who have no covenant hope.
14.3: lo-tokhal kol-toeva: 'You shall not eat any detestable thing (tôʿēḇāh).' The single summary verse before the purity-list uses tôʿēḇāh (abomination, detestable thing) — the strongest category of violation in Deuteronomy's ethical vocabulary. The same word covers idolatry (7:25-26; 13:15), sexual prohibitions (Lev 18:22,26-30), and unjust business practices (25:16). By leading the food-law section with this term, Deuteronomy frames dietary practice not as mere etiquette but as a matter of covenantal integrity: eating the wrong foods is categorically as serious as other covenant violations. The NT's Peter-Cornelius vision (Acts 10:14-15) directly addresses this tôʿēḇāh framework: 'What God has made clean, do not call common.'
14.4: zot habehemah asher tokhelon: 'These are the animals you may eat: the ox, the sheep, and the goat.' The permitted animal list begins with the three domestic herd/flock animals — shor (ox), seh kesavim (sheep), seh izzim (goat) — the primary sacrificial and food animals. Their permissibility is not incidental; these are the very animals used in sacrifice (Lev 1-5), establishing a structural connection between what Israel eats and what Israel offers: the animals that may be eaten are the animals that may be offered. The dietary laws and the sacrificial laws share the same list.
14.5: ha'ayyel vehatzvi vehayyachmur veha'aqqo vehadishon uteho vezamer: 'the deer, the gazelle, the roe deer, the wild goat, the ibex, the antelope, and the mountain sheep.' The list of wild game expands the permitted category to seven named species. Several of these are hapax legomena whose precise identification is uncertain (cf. the variety in English translations): yaḥmûr (roe deer or fallow deer), ʾaqqô (ibex?), dišôn (antelope?), tᵉô (wild ox?), zemer (mountain sheep? chamois?). The naming of seven species (a complete-number list) suggests the permission is comprehensive: all split-hooved, cud-chewing wild game is clean. The LXX's identification of these animals follows Hellenistic zoological categories rather than precise species correspondence.
14.6: vekhol behemah maphresat parsa shosa sheti ferasot maalat gerah: 'You may eat any animal that has completely split hooves and chews the cud.' The two-criteria system for clean land animals — pārāsāh (hoof) that is both maphresat (split, cleft) and šôsaʿat (completely split into two halves) plus maʿălat gērāh (brings up the cud, ruminates) — must both be present for an animal to be clean. The dual requirement prevents misidentification by single-criterion matching: the camel chews the cud but lacks the split hoof; the pig has the hoof but does not chew the cud. The Levitical parallel (Lev 11:2-8) is slightly fuller; Deuteronomy's summary form assumes the reader knows the Levitical tradition.
14.7: akh et-zeh lo tokhelon mimaalei hagerah umimmiphrisei haperisah hashesuyah: 'However, among those that chew the cud or have split hooves, these you shall not eat: the camel, the hare, and the rock badger.' The three borderline cases — gamal (camel), arnevet (hare), shaphān (rock badger/hyrax) — are animals that appear to meet one criterion but not the other. ki maʿaleh gerah hemmah ufarsah lo hiprisu (for they chew the cud but do not divide the hoof): the hare and rock badger are not true ruminants in modern taxonomy, but their chewing motion (grinding molars, caecotrophy in rabbits) presented as cud-chewing to ancient observers. The classification is phenomenological — based on observable behavior — rather than anatomical.
14.8: ve'et-haḥazir ki-maphris parsa hu velo yigar gerah tame hu lachem: 'And the pig — though it has split hooves, it does not chew the cud. It is unclean for you.' The pig (ḥăzîr) is mentioned last and with extra emphasis, perhaps because of its particular status in Canaanite and later Hellenistic culture. The pig's single-criterion status (hooves yes, cud no) makes it the paradigmatic unclean animal. In the Maccabean period (1 Macc 1:47-63; 2 Macc 6:18-7:42), forced pork consumption became the test-case for apostasy: refusing to eat pork was the defining act of Jewish identity maintenance under Antiochus IV. The pig-prohibition thus carried historical-martyrdom weight beyond its textual origin.
14.9: et-zeh tokhelon mikol asher bamayim kol-asher lo senapir vesquamah tokhelon: 'Of all creatures living in the water, you may eat those that have fins and scales.' The two criteria for clean aquatic life — sᵉnappîr (fins) and qasqeset (scales) — are simpler than the land-animal criteria. The positive formulation first ('you may eat whatever has fins and scales') before the negative prohibition makes the clean category the baseline. The criteria effectively permit fish and some shellfish while excluding others — but the precise scope is debated because ancient Israelite taxonomic knowledge of aquatic life was less developed than their knowledge of land animals.
14.10: vekhol asher ein lo senapir vesquamah lo tokhelon tame hu lachem: 'But whatever lacks fins or scales you shall not eat; it is unclean for you.' The negation of both criteria prohibits shellfish, crustaceans, eels, and catfish. ṭāmēʾ hûʾ lākem (it is unclean for you) — the personal pronoun lākem (for you) is significant: the uncleanness is relational, not absolute. The animal is not intrinsically corrupt; it is unsuitable for Israel because of Israel's covenant status as a holy people. The NT's 'clean all foods' declaration (Mark 7:19; Acts 10:15; Rom 14:14) removes the lākem restriction by extending the covenant community beyond ethnic Israel.
14.11: kol-tzippor tehorah tokhelon: 'You may eat any clean bird.' The positive statement for birds is brief because the list immediately follows with the prohibited species (vv. 12-18). The clean-bird category is defined by exclusion rather than positive criteria. The absence of anatomical criteria for birds (unlike the hoof/cud system for land animals) suggests that identification relied on traditional knowledge and specific prohibitory lists rather than a systematic rule. The Mishnaic tractate Hullin develops a four-sign system for birds (crops, extra toe, peelable gizzard, egg-catching behavior), but this is later rabbinic development.
14.12: ve'elleh asher lo tokhelon mehem hannesher vehaperes veha'ozniyyah: 'These are the birds you shall not eat: the eagle, the bearded vulture (peres), and the black vulture (ʾozniyyāh).' The prohibited bird list begins with the large raptors — nesher (eagle or griffon vulture), peres (bearded vulture/lammergeier), ʾozniyyāh (black vulture or osprey). These are all carrion-feeding or large predatory birds. The ecological principle seems to be that birds of prey that tear and consume blood are unsuitable — consistent with the blood-prohibition that underlies much of the kashrut system.
14.13: vehara'ah ve'et-ha'ayya vehadayyah leminam: 'the kite and any kind of falcon.' rāʾāh (kite), ʾayyāh (falcon or kite species), dayyāh (kite or falcon, Lev 11:14) — the phrase lemînāh (according to its kind) extends each prohibition to all species of the genus. The same extension-phrase (lemîn) is used in Genesis 1's creation account for the multiplication of living creatures according to their kinds — Deuteronomy's food laws use creation-vocabulary to organize the animal world.
14.14: ve'et kol-orev lemino: 'every kind of raven.' The raven (ʿōrēḇ) is prohibited with its entire genus (lᵉmînô). Ravens and crows are omnivores that feed on carrion — consistent with the predator/carrion-feeder pattern of the prohibited bird list. The raven appears in biblical narrative at significant moments: sent out by Noah from the ark (Gen 8:7) and feeding Elijah at the brook Cherith (1 Kgs 17:6) — a divine use of an unclean bird for a clean purpose, demonstrating YHWH's sovereign freedom over the categories he has established.
14.15: ve'et-ha'astityah vehat-tes vehashahal ve'et-hanets leminehu: 'the ostrich, the nighthawk, the sea gull, and every kind of hawk.' The bat-yaʿănāh (ostrich, literally 'daughter of howling' or 'daughter of greed') appears in Lev 11:16 as well. The ostrich's inclusion in the prohibited list is consistent with its behavior as a scavenger and its consumption of various non-food materials. The lemîn extension again covers all species of each type, making the prohibitory list comprehensive across genera.
14.16: ha-kos vehashalakh vehayanshup: 'the little owl, the screech owl, and the barn owl.' The three owl species (precise identifications uncertain — kôs, šālaḵ, yansûp) are nocturnal predators. The owl's association with desolation and ruins in the prophets (Isa 34:11,13-15; Jer 50:39; Zeph 2:14) may reflect its status as an unclean bird dwelling in destroyed places — a habitat that symbolizes divine judgment. The banned birds tend to be associated with death (carrion-feeders), predatory violence (raptors), or desolation (owls).
14.17: vehaqa'at ve'et-harakham ve'et-hashalaḵ: 'the pelican, the carrion vulture (rāhām), and the cormorant (šālaḵ).' qāʾāt (pelican or owl species), rāhām (osprey or Egyptian vulture — related to raḥam, compassion/love, an ironic bird-name), šālaḵ (gannet or cormorant). These are aquatic birds that pursue fish rather than live on land — adding a dimension to the prohibited birds: not only carrion-feeders and raptors but fish-eating water-birds are also excluded. The fish-catching birds stand at the intersection of clean aquatic prey and unclean bird predation.
14.18: vehaḥasidah veha'anafah lemina vehadukiphath vehaat'alleph: 'the stork, any kind of heron, the hoopoe, and the bat.' ḥăsîdāh (stork) — the name derives from ḥesed (steadfast love/kindness), perhaps because storks were observed caring attentively for their young. The irony that the ḥăsîdāh (kindness-bird) is unclean was noticed in rabbinic literature (b. Hullin 63a). ʿăṭallēp (bat) — the bat is technically a mammal but classified with the birds in the ancient taxonomy (as in Lev 11:19); it concludes the bird list with the most anomalous creature, one that flies but has no feathers.
14.19: vekhol-shretz ha'of tame hu lachem lo ye'akhelu: 'All winged insects (šereṣ hāʿôp) are unclean for you; they shall not be eaten.' The šereṣ category (swarming creatures) covers insects and small flying creatures generally. Leviticus 11:20-23 qualifies this: winged insects that go on all fours are unclean, but those with jointed legs for leaping (locusts, crickets, grasshoppers) are clean. Deuteronomy's summary form does not include the locust exception, which is assumed from the Levitical tradition. John the Baptist's diet of locusts and wild honey (Matt 3:4; Mark 1:6) was fully within the permitted-insect exception, placing him squarely within Israel's kashrut framework.
14.20: kol-of tahor tokhelon: 'But all clean birds you may eat.' The brief summary permission (echoing v. 11) closes the prohibited-bird section by restating the positive category. The repetition brackets the prohibition list (vv. 11 → prohibited list → v. 20) with positive permission statements — Deuteronomy's rhetorical pattern of sandwiching prohibitions within affirmations of what is allowed, reflecting the covenant's positive framework: the restrictions exist within a broader grant of freedom.
14.22: aser teaser et kol-tevuat zarecha hayotzeh hasadeh shana shana: 'You shall tithe (ʿaśśēr tᵉʿaśśēr) all the yield of your seed that comes from the field each year.' The doubled verb ʿaśśēr tᵉʿaśśēr (tithe you shall tithe) — the Piel infinitive absolute + finite verb — is the Hebrew emphasis construction, indicating the strongest possible obligation: you must certainly tithe. māʿăśēr (tithe, ten-percent) — the practice of giving a tenth to religious institutions has ancient Near Eastern precedent (tribute to temples, to kings). Deuteronomy's tithe is specifically designated for the sanctuary feast at the chosen place, with provision for the Levite (v. 27) and the poor every third year (v. 28-29). The system distributes the agricultural surplus through worship and social solidarity.
14.23: ve'akhalita lifnei YHWH Eloheicha bemakom asher yivchar leshakem shemo sham: 'And in the presence of YHWH your God, at the place he will choose to make his name dwell, you shall eat the tithe of your grain, wine, and oil, and the firstborn of your herd and flock.' The tithe is to be eaten in YHWH's presence (lipnê YHWH) — a communal feast before YHWH at the central sanctuary, not merely a tax payment. lᵉmaʿan tilmad lᵉyārᵉʾāh ʾet-YHWH Eloheycha kol-hayyamim (so that you may learn to fear the LORD your God always) — the feast's purpose is formative: annual eating of the tithe at the sanctuary is an annual education in YHWH-fear, a structured liturgical pedagogy that sustains covenant orientation through repeated practice.
14.24: vekhiy-yirbeh mimkha haderekh ki lo tukhal se'eto: 'But if the distance is too great for you to carry the tithe.' The tithe law includes a practical accommodation for distance: if the central sanctuary is too far, the tithe may be converted to money (kesep). This kesef tiqquph (money conversion) provision anticipates the reality of scattered settlement in Canaan after the conquest. The conversion is not a debasement of the requirement but an adaptation: the purpose (feast before YHWH) is maintained; the form (carrying physical produce vs. carrying money and buying at the destination) is adjusted for practical necessity.
14.25: venatatah bakkesef vetzarerta hakesef beyadkha: 'then you shall exchange the tithe for money, and take the money in hand to the place that the LORD your God will choose.' wᵉṣārartā hakkesep bᵉyāḏᵉkā (wrap/tie the money in your hand) — the idiom means to carry money safely bundled. The practical detail — wrap it and take it — grounds the religious obligation in ordinary commercial reality. Deuteronomy consistently moves between the transcendent (YHWH's holiness) and the mundane (practical livestock management, commercial exchange) without embarrassment: the covenant governs all of life, including how to carry money to the festival.
14.26: unatata hakesef bekhol asher te'aveh nafshkha babaqar uvatsaon uvayayin uvashekhar: 'and spend the money for whatever you desire — cattle, sheep, wine, strong drink, or whatever you want — and you shall eat it before YHWH your God and rejoice.' wᵉšāmḥtā ʾattāh ûḇêtekā (you shall rejoice, you and your household) — the tithe feast is explicitly joyful. The permission for šēḵār (strong drink, fermented beverage) — often prohibited or restricted — is here explicitly permitted for the tithe feast. The feast is an expression of YHWH's abundance: eat what you desire, rejoice before YHWH. The NT counterpart is the messianic banquet (Luke 14:15-24; Rev 19:9), the ultimate feast-before-YHWH to which all these communal meals pointed.
14.27: vehaLevi asher bishareykha lo ta'azvennu ki ein lo helek venachalah imakh: 'And do not neglect (lōʾ taʿazĕḇennû) the Levite who lives in your towns, for he has no portion or inheritance among you.' The Levite's landlessness is the structural rationale for the tithe provision. ʾên lô ḥēleq wᵉnaḥălāh ʿimmāk (he has no portion or inheritance with you) — the Levites received no territorial allotment in the Canaan distribution (Num 18:20-24; Deut 18:1-2) because YHWH himself is their inheritance. Their economic sustenance therefore depends on the tithe system. The verb taʿazĕḇ (to abandon, forsake) is a strong term: neglecting the Levite is the covenant equivalent of abandoning a dependent member of the covenant community.
14.28: miqqets shalosh shanim totzi et-kol-maʿasar tevuatekha bashanah hahi vehinikhta bishearekha: 'At the end of every three years you shall bring out all the tithe of your produce from that year and store it within your towns.' The third-year tithe (maʿasar shanim shalosh) — distinct from the regular annual tithe — is stored locally rather than taken to the central sanctuary. This local-storage tithe creates a community welfare reserve, accessible throughout the town for the Levite, the resident alien, the fatherless, and the widow (v. 29). The three-year cycle aligns with the Sabbatical year system (every seventh year there is a full release, ch. 15) and creates a multi-layered provision infrastructure.
14.29: uvaa haLevi ki ein lo helek venachalah imakh veha-ger vehayatom vehaalmanah asher bishareykha veakhelu vesaveyu: 'so that the Levite — who has no portion or inheritance among you — and the resident foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow who are in your towns may come and eat and be satisfied.' The beneficiary list — Levite, gēr (resident alien), yātôm (fatherless), ʾalmānāh (widow) — is Deuteronomy's standard quadruplet of the covenant's economically vulnerable. Their needs are structurally built into the tithe calendar: the provision for the poor is not charity but covenant obligation, scheduled and institutionalized. lᵉmaʿan yᵉḇārᵉkᵉkā YHWH Eloheycha bᵉkol-maʿăśēh yāḏᵉkā ʾăšer taʿăśeh (so that YHWH your God may bless you in all the work of your hands) — generosity to the vulnerable is the condition for YHWH's blessing, making the welfare system a covenant-economic investment, not merely humanitarian sentiment.

15.1: mikets sheva-shanim ta'aseh shemitah (miqqēṣ šebaʿ-šānîm taʿăśeh šĕmiṭṭāh): 'At the end of every seven years you shall grant a release.' Shemittah from the root šāmaṭ (to let drop, to release, to let fall) — used elsewhere for letting a sword fall (2 Sam 6:6 variant) or releasing an animal. The sabbatical year (shnat hashemittah) is the seventh year in which debts between Israelites are released and the land is allowed to rest (Exod 23:10-11; Lev 25:1-7). The year of release images the Jubilee (Lev 25:10: deror, release/liberty) — which Jesus applies to his own ministry in Luke 4:18-19 (shevat shevuyim deror, proclaim liberty to captives). The whole sabbatical-Jubilee complex encodes the principle of periodic economic reset as a covenantal obligation.
15.11: ki lo-yechdal evyon mikerev ha'aretz al-ken anochi metzavecha lemor pato'ach tiftach et-yadcha le'achicha la'aniyecha ule'evyonecha be'artzecha (kî lōʾ-yeḥdal ʾebyôn miqqereb hāʾāreṣ ʿal-kēn ʾānōkî mĕṣawwĕkā lēmōr pātōaḥ tip̄taḥ ʾet-yādĕkā lĕʾāḥîkā lĕʿăniyyĕkā ûlĕʾebyônĕkā bĕʾarṣĕkā): 'For there will never cease to be poor in the land. Therefore I command you, You shall open wide your hand to your brother, to the needy and to the poor, in your land.' Jesus cites this verse twice: Matt 26:11 and Mark 14:7 (the poor you always have with you, but you will not always have me) — not as a fatalistic dismissal of poverty but as a citation of Deut 15:11 to make a Christological point about the unique and irreplaceable moment of his physical presence. The double infinitive absolute pātōaḥ tip̄taḥ (open wide, you shall open) is intensifying idiom — categorical generosity, not selective charity.
15.2: wezeh devar hashemittah shamot kol-baal masheh yado asher yashe bere'ehu: 'And this is how the release (šᵉmiṭṭāh) shall work: every creditor shall cancel (šāmôṭ) what he has lent to his neighbor.' šāmôṭ — the root šāmaṭ (to let drop, release, remit) is the same root used in Exod 23:11 for letting the land lie fallow in the seventh year. The debt-release mirrors the land-sabbath: as the land is released from cultivation every seventh year, so debts are released every seventh year. lo-yigos et-re'ehu ve'et-aḥiv (he must not press his neighbor or his brother) — the prohibition on pressing for payment uses the verb nāgas (to exact, press, drive), the same verb used of Pharaoh's slave-masters pressing Israel in Exodus (Exod 3:7; 5:6,10,13,14). The debt-release laws prevent the recreation of Egyptian bondage within Israel.
15.3: et-hanokri tiggos ve'asher yihyeh lekha et-aḥikha tashmet yadekha: 'You may collect from a foreigner, but whatever your brother owes you, you must release.' The distinction between nokrî (foreigner, non-resident alien) and ʾāḥ (brother, fellow Israelite) is an economic-covenantal distinction: the release operates within the covenant community, not universally. This has generated significant discussion in Jewish ethics about the scope of neighbor-obligation. The NT's expansion — 'Is he not also my neighbor?' (Luke 10:29-37) — challenges the limiting function of the ʾāḥ/nokrî distinction in the parable of the Good Samaritan.
15.4: efes ki lo-yihyeh bekha evyon ki-barekh yevarekhkha YHWH be'aretz asher YHWH Eloheicha noten lekha: 'There need be no poor among you, for the LORD will surely bless you in the land.' efes kî (except that, but) — the phrase introduces not a contradiction to v. 11 ('the poor will never cease,' which Jesus quotes in John 12:8) but a conditional vision: poverty is not structurally necessary in the covenant community if covenant obedience is maintained. The doubled blessing verb bārēk yᵉḇārᵉkekā (YHWH will surely bless you) — the Piel infinitive absolute + Piel finite — is the emphatic assurance of prosperity. The tension between v. 4 (need be no poor) and v. 11 (poor will always exist) is Deuteronomy's realistic covenant pedagogy: the ideal is stated, then the practical reality acknowledged.
15.5: raq im-shamo'a tishma bekol YHWH Eloheicha lishmor la'asot et-kol-hamitzvah hazot: 'if only you carefully obey the voice of the LORD your God, being diligent to keep all this commandment.' The doubled imperative šāmôaʿ tišmaʿ (hear-obey you shall hear-obey) — Hebrew's emphatic construction — qualifies the promise of v. 4: the blessing-prosperity is conditional on covenant faithfulness. šāmar laʿăśôt (guard to do) — the paired verbs of covenant faithfulness: guard (internalize, not forget) and do (implement). Deuteronomy consistently pairs these two verbs because knowledge without practice is covenant failure, and practice without understanding is mere external compliance.
15.6: ki-YHWH Eloheicha berakhekha kaasher dibber-lakh: 'For the LORD your God will bless you as he has promised.' kaʾăšer dibber-lāk (as he has spoken to you) — the blessing is not a new promise but the fulfillment of a prior word; the debt-release and generosity laws are embedded in a covenant where YHWH's promise precedes and underwrites the commands. vehaavet goyim rabbim ve'ata lo tilve (you will lend to many nations but not borrow) — the reversal of debtor-creditor status between Israel and the nations is the economic expression of covenant supremacy: the covenant people, when obedient, become the lenders rather than the borrowers. Paul applies this economic reversal spiritually in Phil 4:19: 'my God will supply every need of yours according to his riches in glory.'
15.7: ki-yihyeh vekha evyon me'aḥad aḥeicha be'aḥad she'arekha be'artzekha: 'If there is a poor person (ʾeḇyôn) among you, one of your brothers, in any of your towns.' ʾeḇyôn (needy, poor) — the specific term for one in want, in contrast to ānî (oppressed/humble-poor) and dal (weak/low). The combination of ʾeḇyôn and ʾāḥ (brother) personalizes the economic obligation: the poor person is not an abstract social problem but your brother, a member of the covenant family. lo-tĕ'ammēṣ ʾet-lᵉḇāḇᵉkā wᵉlōʾ tiqpōṣ ʾet-yāḏᵉkā mēʾāḥîkā hāʾeḇyôn (do not harden your heart or shut your hand against your poor brother) — the double prohibition uses body-part metaphors: the heart-hardening and hand-closing are the emotional and practical failures of covenant solidarity.
15.8: ki-phato'ah tiphtaḥ et-yadekha lo: 'Instead, you shall open your hand wide (pāṯôaḥ tipṯaḥ) to him.' The doubled infinitive absolute + finite verb construction (pāṯôaḥ tipṯaḥ — open wide you shall open wide) is the emphatic contrast to the hand-closing of v. 7. vehaavet taavit lo dei mahsoro asher yehsar lo (and lend him whatever he needs, enough for his lack) — mahsoro (his deficit) specifies that the loan amount is calibrated to need, not to lender-convenience. The open-hand imagery recurs in Ps 145:16 as a description of YHWH himself: 'you open your hand and satisfy the desire of every living thing' — Israel's open-handed generosity to the poor imitates YHWH's own provision-character.
15.9: hishamer lekha pen-yihyeh davar im-levavekha veliyyaal: 'Guard against an unworthy thought in your heart (dāḇār ʿim-lᵉḇāḇᵉkā beliyyāʿal).' beliyyāʿal — the compound term (literally ‘without profit/worth’) used for thoroughly wicked or worthless persons and things (cf. 'sons of Belial' in Judges, 1 Samuel, and later used as a name for Satan in 2 Cor 6:15). The thought prohibited here is calculating the approach of the shemittah year as a reason to withhold loans to the poor — using the release law as a cover for refusing generosity. Deuteronomy here commands the regulation of internal motivation, not merely external behavior: the problem is bᵉlibbekā (in your heart), not just in your actions.
15.10: naton tittennu lo velo-yera levavekha betitkha lo: 'Give to him generously (nāṯôn tiṯṯennû lô), and do not be reluctant in your heart when you give.' The doubled verb construction — nāṯôn tiṯṯennû (give you shall give) — insists on both external action and internal attitude: give, and do so without heart-resistance. kî biglal haddāḇār hazzeh yᵉḇārᵉkekā YHWH Eloheycha bᵉkol-maʿăśekā (for because of this the LORD your God will bless you in all your work) — the causal link between generosity and blessing is direct: the blessing flows from the giving, connecting the agrarian economy to covenant faithfulness.
15.12: ki-yimmakher lekha aḥikha haivri o haivriyvah va'avadkha shesh shanim: 'If your brother, a Hebrew man or a Hebrew woman, is sold to you and serves you six years.' The slave-manumission law (vv. 12-18) parallels Exod 21:2-6 with significant Deuteronomic expansions. The inclusion of haʿiḇriyyāh (the Hebrew woman) alongside the Hebrew man is Deuteronomy's egalitarian expansion of Exod 21:2, which applies only to male slaves. The six-year service + seventh-year release mirrors the agricultural sabbatical: as the land rests in the seventh year, so the Hebrew slave is released. The Exodus background is explicit in v. 15: 'you were a slave in Egypt.'
