11.18: vesamtem et-devarai eleh al-levavchem (wĕśamtem ʾet-dĕbārāy ʾēlleh ʿal-lĕbabkem): 'Place these words of mine on your hearts.' śûm ʿal-lēb (to set upon the heart): the Hebrew idiom for internalization, taking something to heart. The subsequent actions — binding on the hand, between the eyes, teaching to children, writing on doorposts (mĕzûzôt) — externalize what is first internal. tôṭāp̄ôt (frontlets/phylacteries): the symbol between the eyes; later worn as physical tĕp̄illîn in Jewish practice. The placement sequence (heart → hand → forehead → doorpost → gate) moves from the most internal to the most public.
11.19: velimadtem otam et-beneihem (wĕlimmdtem ʾōtām ʾet-bĕnêkem): 'Teach them to your children.' limmēd (to teach, train): the piel intensive of lāmad (to learn). The Torah's transmission is communal and generational — the command-love of 6:6-9 is repeated here in identical language, emphasizing the pedagogical responsibility of parents. dabbēr bām (speak them): in every life-setting — sitting, walking, lying down, rising up — Torah saturates daily existence.
11.20: ukhtavtam al-mezuzot beitecha (ûkĕtabtām ʿal-mĕzûzôt bêtekā): 'Write them on the doorposts of your house.' mĕzûzâ (doorpost): the vertical wooden post at a door's side; the practice of affixing a small scroll (mĕzûzâ in later usage) to doorposts derives from this command. The gate (šaʿar) extends the Torah-marking to public space. The household and its public entry point are both claimed by the covenant word.
11.21: leyan yirbu yemeichem (lĕmaʿan yirbû yĕmêkem): 'That your days may be multiplied.' The lĕmaʿan (in order that) teleological formula — the covenant's purpose is life and length of days in the promised land. yimê haššāmayim ʿal-hāʾāreṣ (the days of heaven upon the earth): a beautiful hyperbole for indefinite duration — as long as sky is above earth. The land-tenure formula pairs with obedience; disobedience forfeits the promise.
11.22: ki im-shamor tishmeroon (kî ʾim-šāmōr tišmĕrûn): 'If you carefully observe.' Again the double infinitive absolute intensifier (šāmōr tišmĕrûn). dābaq (to hold fast, cleave): the covenantal-adhesion term — used of husband and wife in Gen 2:24, of Ruth and Naomi in Ruth 1:14, and here of Israel's obligation to YHWH. dābaq is the most intimate of the covenant-loyalty verbs, denoting personal attachment rather than mere compliance.
11.23: vehorish YHWH et-kol-hagoyim: 'YHWH will drive out all the nations.' yāraš (to dispossess, take possession): the key land-theology verb — both the dispossessing of the Canaanites and the possessing by Israel are expressed by the same root. The nations are described as 'greater and mightier than you' — the dispossession is explicitly beyond Israel's own military capacity, requiring divine action.
11.24: kol-hamakom asher tidroch kaf-raglechem bo lachem yihyeh: 'Every place where you set the sole of your foot shall be yours.' The land grant is expressed in the bodily imagery of foot-on-ground — the physical act of walking the territory constitutes possession in ancient Near Eastern legal custom. The promised boundaries: wilderness to Lebanon, Euphrates to Western Sea (hayyām hāʾaḥărôn, the Mediterranean).
11.25: pachdam vemoraam (paḥdĕkem ûmôrāʾăkem): 'Your terror and dread.' paḥad (terror, dread) and môrāʾ (fear, reverential dread): the two fear-words denote the existential dread YHWH will place in the nations' hearts at Israel's approach. This reverses the fear-formula: Israel need not fear (v.25a: 'no man shall stand against you') because YHWH instills fear in the nations. The holy-war theology of Deut is grounded in divine action, not Israelite strength.
11.26: reeh anochi noten lifneichem hayom (rĕʾēh ʾānōkî nōtēn lip̄nêkem hayyôm): 'See, I am placing before you today.' rĕʾēh (see!) — the singular imperative of perception, calling each individual to direct moral attention. berākâ ûqĕlālâ (blessing and curse): the covenant's binary structure that governs Deut 28-30. The 'today' (hayyôm) is the existential now of covenant decision — the choice is always present and urgent.
11.27: et-haberacha asher tishmeʿu: 'The blessing, if you obey.' The blessing is conditional on šāmaʿ (hearing/obeying) — 'if you obey the commandments of YHWH your God that I am commanding you today.' The Hebrew conditional structure: ʾăšer tišmĕʿû... hammitsvâ... ʾăšer ʾānōkî mĕṣawweh ʾetkem hayyôm — the blessing is inextricably bound to obedience.
11.28: vehakelala im-lo tishmeʿu: 'The curse, if you do not obey.' The curse (qĕlālâ) is the consequence of not-hearing — lōʾ tišmĕʿû. sārtem min-hadderek (turning aside from the way): sûr (to turn aside, deviate) is the standard verb for apostasy in Deuteronomy — leaving the covenant path. The 'other gods' you have not known: ʾĕlōhîm ʾăḥērîm ʾăšer lōʾ-yĕdaʿtem — the gods without covenant history with Israel.
11.29: venatata et-haberacha al-har gerizim: 'Set the blessing on Mount Gerizim, the curse on Mount Ebal.' The two mountains of the Shechem valley — the geography of covenant ratification (Josh 8:30-35 enacts Deut 27-28). Gerizim (traditionally the blessing-mountain, to the south; the Samaritan temple stood here) and ʿEbal (the curse-mountain, to the north). The landscape itself becomes the covenant's visible declaration.
11.30: halo-hema beeiver haYarden: 'Are they not beyond the Jordan?' The geographical orientation — 'beyond the Jordan, toward the setting sun, in the land of the Canaanites in the Arabah, opposite Gilgal, beside the oaks of Moreh' — grounds the covenant ceremony in a specific, visitable landscape. The precision of the geographical description serves the covenant's historical particularity: this is not mythology but history attached to known places.
11.31: ki atem ovrim et-haYarden: 'For you are crossing the Jordan.' The crossing (ʿābar) is the liminal moment — the covenant people on the threshold of the promised land. The entire book of Deuteronomy is Moses's final address before the crossing; the covenant renewal here is the preparation for what follows in Joshua. The imperative to obey 'all the statutes and ordinances' applies to the land they are entering to possess.
11.32: ushmartem laasot et-kol-hachuqqim: 'Keep and do all the statutes and ordinances.' The closing verse of the chapter pairs šāmar (keep, guard) with ʿāśâ (do, perform) — both verbs required: internalized guarding AND external performance. The land they are about to enter demands both dimensions of covenant loyalty.

12.1: eleh hachuqqim vehamishpatim (ʾēlleh haḥuqqîm wĕhammišpāṭîm): 'These are the statutes and ordinances.' Chapter 12 opens the Deuteronomic Law Code (chs 12-26) — the specific legal applications of the broad covenant command of chs 1-11. ḥuqqîm (statutes, fixed rules) and mišpāṭîm (ordinances, case-law decisions): the two principal legal categories. The land is the context — 'in the land which YHWH the God of your fathers has given you to possess, all the days that you live on the earth.'
12.2: abbed tabdun et-kol-hamqomot (ʾabbēd tĕʾabbĕdûn ʾet-kol-hammĕqōmôt): 'Completely destroy all the places.' Again the intensive double construction (ʾabbēd tĕʾabbĕdûn — double piel of ʾābad, to destroy). mĕqōmôt (places): the high places, hillsides, and trees where the Canaanite gods were worshipped. The command is not merely to stop using them but to eliminate them — ʿal-hehārîm hārāmîm wĕʿal-haggĕbāʿôt wĕtaḥat kol-ʿēṣ raʿănān (on the high mountains, on the hills, under every green tree).
12.3: nittazttem et-mizbehotam: 'Tear down their altars.' The four verbs of cultic destruction: nāttaṣ (tear down), šābar (smash), śārap̄ (burn), kārat (cut down). Each object: mizbĕḥôt (altars), maṣṣēbôt (sacred pillars, standing stones), ʾăšērîm (Asherah poles, wooden cult objects), and pĕsîlîm (carved/graven images). The divine name (šēm) of the Canaanite deities is to be destroyed — the name represents the deity's presence and power.
12.4: lo-taaʿsun ken laYHWH eloheichem: 'You must not do so to YHWH your God.' The explicit prohibition of Canaanite worship-practices applied to YHWH — YHWH is not to be worshipped in the same manner as the Canaanite gods. The verse is cryptic but decisive: whatever the Canaanites did at their scattered high places is forbidden for YHWH's worship. The positive alternative follows in v.5: YHWH chooses a single place.
12.5: ki im el-hamaqom asher-yivchar YHWH eloheichem (kî ʾim-ʾel-hammāqôm ʾăšer-yibḥar Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkem): 'Only to the place that YHWH your God chooses.' The theological heart of Deuteronomy 12: the centralization formula. yibḥar (he will choose): the divine election of the sanctuary site is YHWH's prerogative, not Israel's. lĕšakkēn šĕmô šām (to make his name dwell there): the Name-theology of Deuteronomy — YHWH does not corporally dwell in the temple (contrast Solomon's prayer, 1 Kings 8:27: 'even heaven cannot contain you') but places his šēm (name, representative presence) there. This nuance safeguards divine transcendence while asserting real divine presence.
12.6: uveatem shama oloteichem: 'There you shall bring your burnt offerings.' The list of sacrificial types required at the central sanctuary: ʿōlôt (burnt offerings), zeḇāḥîm (sacrifices), maʿăśrôt (tithes), tĕrûmat yēdkem (heave offerings), nĕdārîm (vow offerings), nĕdābôt (freewill offerings), and the firstborn of flocks and herds. The comprehensive catalog emphasizes that all worship-transactions with YHWH require the central place.
12.7: vaachaltem sham lifnei YHWH eloheichem: 'There you shall eat before YHWH your God.' The covenant meal eaten 'before YHWH' (lip̄nê Yhwh) in the sanctuary is not merely a meal but a communion act — Israel eating in the divine presence. ûśĕmaḥtem (rejoice): the joy-command that runs through Deuteronomy's festival legislation (14:26; 16:11,14; 26:11). The centralized sanctuary is not an onerous restriction but the place of covenant celebration and abundance.
12.8: lo taaʿsun kekol asher anachnu osim po hayom: 'You shall not do as we are doing here today.' The ʾîš kol-hayyāšār bĕʿênāyw (each man doing what is right in his own eyes) formula — the antithesis of the centralized covenant order. The chaotic worship of the wilderness period (each according to his own judgment) is contrasted with the ordered central-sanctuary worship that the entry into the land will establish. Judges 17:6; 21:25 use the same phrase for the moral chaos of the settlement period.
12.9: ki lo batem ad-ata el-hamenucha veEl-hanachala: 'You have not yet come to the rest and the inheritance.' mĕnûḥâ (rest, resting-place): the theological rest that the land represents — the cessation of the wilderness wandering. naḥălâ (inheritance): the land as divine gift-inheritance, not merely a territory won by military conquest. The double term anticipates the settled, stable worship at the chosen place — rest and inheritance are prerequisites for the centralized sanctuary order.
12.10: vaavartm et-haYarden vishavtem baareẓ: 'When you cross the Jordan and dwell in the land.' The conditional structure of Deut 12:1-28 — the laws apply fully only when the land is possessed and rest is achieved. wĕhēnîaḥ lākem mibbāl ʾōyĕbêkem (when he gives you rest from all your enemies): the military rest is the precondition for the cultic order. Hebrews 4:1-11 uses the mĕnûḥâ concept as a type for the final eschatological rest in Christ.
12.11: vehaya hamaqom asher-yivchar YHWH eloheichem bo: 'It shall be the place YHWH your God chooses for his name to dwell.' The centralization formula repeated (vv.5,11,14,18,21,26) — its ninefold repetition in ch 12 marks it as the theological spine of the entire legal corpus. The accumulated weight of 'the place that YHWH chooses' drives home the principle: divine election, not human convenience, determines the locus of worship.
12.12: usmachtem lifnei YHWH eloheichem: 'Rejoice before YHWH your God.' śāmaḥ (to rejoice) is a covenant-obligation as much as a response. The inclusion of sons, daughters, male and female servants, and the Levite within your gates — the total household including dependents — in the sanctuary celebration is Deuteronomy's consistent social vision: covenant joy is communal and inclusive.
12.13: hishamer lecha pen-taaleh oloteicha: 'Take care not to offer your burnt offerings at every place.' The negative form of the centralization command — explicit prohibition of sacrifice at any location chosen by the worshipper. hišāmer (take care, be careful): the reflexive warning. The positive follows: 'only in the place that YHWH chooses in one of your tribes.'
12.14: ki im-bamaqom asher-yivchar YHWH: 'Only in the place that YHWH chooses in one of your tribes.' The indefiniteness of 'one of your tribes' is deliberate — the specific place (Jerusalem/Zion) is not named in Deuteronomy. The principle of divine election precedes the specific location; the people commit to following YHWH's choice before the choice is made known.
12.15: raq bechol-avat nafshecha tizbach veachalta vasar: 'Only you may slaughter and eat meat within your towns whenever you desire.' The concession for secular slaughter (mĕhôl) outside the sanctuary — meat may be eaten for sustenance without sacrificial ritual, unlike the earlier system (Lev 17:1-7 required all slaughter to be sacrificial). Deuteronomy permits what is called šĕḥîṭat ḥullîn (secular/non-sacred slaughter) — a practical concession for the increased distance from the central sanctuary. The clean and unclean together may eat: haṭṭāhôr wĕhaṭṭāmēʾ — without the purity constraints of sacrificial eating.
12.16: raq hadam lo tochal: 'Only you shall not eat the blood.' dām (blood): the prohibition of blood-consumption is foundational (Lev 17:10-14; Gen 9:4; Acts 15:29). The blood belongs to YHWH as the vehicle of life — ʿal-hāʾāreṣ tišpĕkennû kammāyim (pour it on the ground like water): the blood is returned to the earth rather than consumed, acknowledging that the life belongs to God.
12.17: lo tuchal leechol bisheareicha maʿsar: 'You may not eat within your towns the tithe of your grain, wine, and oil.' The distinction between ordinary sustenance (permitted locally, v.15) and the covenant-sacred items (tithes, firstborn, vow offerings) that must be eaten at the central sanctuary. The list of items requiring pilgrimage to the central place: maʿăśar dĕgānĕkā wĕtîrōšĕkā wĕyiṣhārekā (tithes of grain, new wine, oil), firstborn of herd and flock, vow offerings, freewill offerings, heave offerings.
12.18: ki im-lifnei YHWH eloheicha tocharlennu: 'Only before YHWH your God shall you eat it.' The items withheld from local consumption must be consumed 'before YHWH' — in his presence, at the central sanctuary, in a communal celebration that includes the household and the Levite. The joy-command returns: ûśāmaḥtā lip̄nê Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā — the centralization's purpose is not restriction but celebration in the divine presence.
12.19: hishamer lecha pen-taazov et-halevi: 'Take care not to neglect the Levite.' The Levite has no territorial inheritance (ḥēleq wĕnaḥălâ) — his portion is YHWH himself (10:9; 18:1-2). The repeated concern for the Levite within Israel's towns across Deuteronomy (12:12,18,19; 14:27,29; 16:11,14; 26:12-13) reflects the social realities of cultic centralization: Levites who served at local shrines needed sustained community support once those shrines were abolished.
12.20: ki-yarchiv YHWH eloheicha et-gevulcha: 'When YHWH your God extends your territory.' The provision for secular meat-eating is made in anticipation of the land's expansion, when distance from the central sanctuary makes pilgrimage for every meat-meal impractical. weʾāmartā ʾōklâ bāśār (you say 'I will eat meat'): the natural human desire for meat is legitimate; YHWH's provision is practical and human-scale.
12.21: veHAYA im-yirchaq mimcha haMaqom asher yivchar YHWH: 'If the place that YHWH chooses is far from you.' Distance is the practical consideration that triggers the secular-slaughter permission. wĕzābaḥtā mibbĕqārĕkā ûmiṣṣōʾnĕkā (slaughter from your herd and flock) 'as I have commanded you' — even secular slaughter has a prescribed manner (ʾăšer ṣiwwîtîkā). The humane slaughter laws implicit in this phrase became the basis for rabbinic šĕḥîṭâ regulations.
12.22: ach kaaher yochal et-hatzvi veEt-haAYAL: 'Just as the gazelle and the deer are eaten.' The clean wild animals (whose blood is not brought to the altar and poured against it) become the model for secular slaughter — neither the blood-sprinkling ritual nor the fat-burning applies. The democratization of meat-eating that Deuteronomy permits is a significant departure from the Leviticus 17 system, reflecting the changed circumstances of settled land life far from the central sanctuary.
12.23: raq chazaq levilti achal hadam (raq ḥăzaq lĕbiltî ʾăkōl haddām): 'Only be strong in not eating the blood.' ḥăzaq (be strong, be firm): the blood prohibition requires moral strength and resolution to maintain. The theological rationale: kî haddām hûʾ hannep̄eš (for the blood is the life/soul). nepeš (life, soul, being): the blood as the carrier of the nepeš (the animating life-force) is the most fundamental principle of Israelite food law. Lev 17:11 adds: 'the life of the flesh is in the blood; I have given it to you on the altar to make atonement for your souls.'
12.24: lo tochlenu: 'Do not eat it.' The repeated blood prohibition (vv.16,23,24,25) through intensification. ʿal-hāʾāreṣ tišpĕkennû kammāyim (pour it on the ground like water): the blood returned to the earth reverses the sacrificial blood that is collected and dashed against the altar. Non-sacrificial slaughter still requires the blood's return — the life belongs to YHWH even when the killing is secular.
12.25: lo tochlenu lemaʿan yitav lecha: 'Do not eat it, that it may go well with you.' The motivational formula: obedience to the blood law produces well-being (ṭôb) for the individual and descendants. The covenant-reward structure: right practice → divine blessing. The blood law is not arbitrary but carries the weight of the covenant's own survival logic — the life belongs to the giver of life.
12.26: raq qodasheicha asher-yihyu lecha: 'But your holy things and your vow offerings.' The return to the central-sanctuary requirement for the sacred items — vowed sacrifices (nĕdārîm) must be brought to the chosen place. The distinction sacred/secular (consecrated offering vs. ordinary meat) determines where the eating occurs.
12.27: veAsita olotecha habasar vehadam: 'Offer your burnt offerings, the flesh and the blood, on the altar.' The burnt offering is the total offering — bāśār wĕdām (flesh and blood) both belong to YHWH, consumed entirely. The peace offering (zeḇaḥ šĕlāmîm) differs: the blood is dashed against the altar, the fat burned, but the flesh eaten by the worshipper 'before YHWH.' The two-way flow of the communion meal: YHWH receives the blood and fat, Israel eats the flesh.
12.28: shemor veshimaʿta et-kol-hadevarim haeleh: 'Observe and obey all these words.' The summary command closing the central-sanctuary legislation. lĕmaʿan yîṭab lĕkā ûlĕbānêkā (that it may go well for you and your children): the covenant-benefit formula applied to the entire legal corpus of ch 12. The chapter ends with the same well-being motivation as v.25 — the laws are for Israel's flourishing, not arbitrary restriction.
12.29: ki-yachrit YHWH eloheicha et-hagoyim: 'When YHWH your God cuts off the nations before you.' The dispossession formula opens the warning against Canaanite worship-imitation. pen-tinaqesh achareihem (lest you be ensnared by following them): nāqaš (to be ensnared, trapped): the Canaanite practices as a trap. The theological danger: Israel, having displaced the Canaanites, might be attracted to the cultic practices that 'worked' in the land.
12.30: pen-tidrosh leEloheihem leymor: 'Lest you inquire about their gods, saying.' The curiosity-trap — not active worship but theological inquiry into Canaanite religion. êkâ yaʿabdû haggôyîm hāʾēlleh ʾet-ʾĕlōhêhem (how did these nations serve their gods?): the inquiry that leads to syncretism. The imitative impulse is the gateway to apostasy — the Deuteronomist understands the psychological seduction of religious curiosity.
12.31: lo-taaʿseh ken lYHWH eloheicha: 'You shall not do so to YHWH your God.' The Canaanite worship practices are described as abominations (tôʿēbat Yhwh) — including child sacrifice (ki gam ʾet-bĕnêhem wĕʾet-bĕnōtêhem yiśrĕpû bāʾēš lēʾlōhêhem, 'for they even burned their sons and daughters in the fire for their gods'). The Molech cult is the extreme point of Canaanite religious practice — the total sacrifice of children to secure divine favor.
12.32: et-kol-haDavar asher anochi metzaveh etchem oto tishmerunu laasot: 'Everything I command you, be careful to do it.' The closing formula of chapter 12: neither add nor subtract (lōʾ tōsēp̄ ʿālāyw wĕlōʾ tigdaʿ mimmennû). The completeness and sufficiency of the Mosaic Torah — no human additions or subtractions permitted. Deut 4:2 opens with the same formula; the book is bracketed by the sufficiency of the divine word.

13.1: ki-yaqum beqirBEKHA navi o ḥolam ḥalom (kî-yāqûm bĕqirbĕkā nābîʾ ʾô ḥōlēm ḥălôm): 'If a prophet or a dreamer of dreams arises among you.' Chapter 13 deals with three scenarios of enticement to apostasy. nābîʾ (prophet) and ḥōlēm ḥălôm (dreamer of dreams): the two recognized channels of divine communication in Israel's prophetic tradition. The test case is deliberately extreme — what if the sign actually comes to pass?
13.2: venatan eleicha ot o mofet (wĕnātan ʾēlêkā ʾôt ʾô mōp̄ēt): 'And the sign or wonder comes to pass.' ʾôt (sign) and mōp̄ēt (wonder, portent): the same sign-wonder pair from the exodus (4:34; 6:22; 7:19; 26:8) — but here fulfilled by a false prophet. The scenario is shocking: even verified predictive ability does not authenticate the prophet if he directs Israel toward other gods. Accuracy is necessary but not sufficient for prophetic authority — the content determines the verdict.
13.3: lo tishmaʿ el-divrei hanavi hahu (lōʾ tišmaʿ ʾel-dibrê hannābîʾ hahûʾ): 'Do not listen to the words of that prophet.' The command is absolute — regardless of the sign's fulfillment. ki menasseh YHWH eloheichem etchem (for YHWH your God is testing you): the false prophet who produces genuine signs is permitted by YHWH as a nissāyôn (test, trial) — to reveal whether Israel's love for YHWH is absolute or contingent on miraculous display. Matt 24:24 and Rev 13:13-14 use the same logic: false prophets will produce great signs to deceive.
13.4: acharei YHWH eloheichem telchu: 'Follow the LORD your God.' The five verbs of total covenant loyalty: hālak ʾaḥărê (follow after), yārēʾ (fear), šāmar miṣwōt (keep commandments), šāmaʿ bĕqōlô (obey his voice), ʿābaḏ (serve), dābaq (hold fast). The five-fold response is the proper orientation that the false prophet's enticement tests. The test reveals whether this comprehensive loyalty is genuine or merely formal.
13.5: veHAnavi hahu o ḥolam haḥalom hahu yumat (wĕhannābîʾ hahûʾ ʾô ḥōlēm haḥălôm hahûʾ yûmāt): 'That prophet or dreamer shall be put to death.' The death penalty for the false prophet — regardless of whether his signs came true. kî dibber-sārâ ʿal-Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkem (for he has spoken rebellion against YHWH your God): sārâ (rebellion, apostasy) — the technical term for the covenant treason that warrants the most severe penalty. ûbiʿartā hārāʿ miqqirbĕkā (purge the evil from your midst): the biʿēr (purge/burn out) formula that runs through Deuteronomy's penal code (17:7,12; 19:13,19; 21:21; 22:21-24; 24:7).
13.6: ki yesitcha acheicha ben-immecha (kî yĕsîtĕkā ʾāḥîkā ben-ʾimmekā): 'If your brother, the son of your mother, entices you.' The second scenario: the intimate enticer — family member (brother, son, daughter, wife, close friend). mēsît (enticer): from sût (to incite, persuade, entice) — the same root used for Satan enticing David (1 Chr 21:1) and YHWH testing Job (Job 2:3). The social proximity makes the enticement most dangerous — it comes wrapped in personal loyalty and love.
13.7: nelcha venavda elohim aḥerim: “Let us go and serve other gods.” The content of the enticement: gods of the surrounding peoples — mēʾĕlōhê hāʿammîm ʾăšer sĕbîbōtêkem (from the gods of the peoples surrounding you), near or far, unknown. The anonymity of the suggested gods ('which neither you nor your fathers have known') emphasizes their lack of covenant history — no basis for loyalty except the enticer's relationship with you.
13.8: lo-tove lo velo tishmaʿ elav: 'Do not yield to him or listen to him.' The four prohibitions toward the intimate enticer: lōʾ-ʾābeh (do not yield), lōʾ-tišmaʿ (do not listen), lōʾ-tāḥôs ʿênĕkā (do not have pity), lōʾ tĕḥuml (do not show compassion), lōʾ tĕkassē ʿālāyw (do not conceal him). The cascade of negatives describes the total severance of relationship loyalty where covenant loyalty is at stake. The law is radical and deliberately shocking — it requires placing YHWH above even the most intimate human bonds.
13.9: ki harog tahargennu (kî hārōg tahargennû): 'You shall surely kill him.' The intensified double construction (hārōg tahargennû — infinitive absolute + finite verb) demands the death penalty. yādĕkā tihyeh bô bāriʾšōnâ lĕhāmîtô (your hand shall be first against him to put him to death): the accuser executes first. The public execution and the subsequent lapidation (rāgam bāʾăbānîm) witnesses the community's covenant solidarity.
13.10: usekaltho baavanim vaMET: 'Stone him to death.' ûsĕqaltô bāʾăbānîm wāmēt: the stoning is the community's collective act — the mob-execution that distributes responsibility across the whole body. kî biqqēš lĕhadd ōtĕkā mēʿal Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā (for he sought to drive you from YHWH your God): niddaḥ (to drive away, banish): the enticement's goal is covenant expulsion — separation from the relationship that alone gives life.
13.11: vechal-Yisrael yishmeʿu veyireʾu: 'All Israel shall hear and fear.' yišmĕʿû wĕyîrĕʾû (shall hear and fear): the public execution's deterrent function. The execution's publicity transforms individual punishment into communal instruction. wĕlōʾ-yōsîp̄û laʿăśôt kaddābār hārāʿ hazzeh bĕqirbĕkā (shall no longer do such evil in your midst): the deterrence logic of Deuteronomy's penal code — the punishment prevents the replication of the crime.
13.12: ki-tishmaʿ beachat areicah asher YHWH eloheicha noten lecha: 'If you hear in one of your cities that YHWH is giving you.' The third scenario: the devoted city (ʿîr hanniddaḥat). The scope expands from individual false prophet (v.1-5) to individual enticer (v.6-11) to an entire city that has embraced apostasy. The city-scale apostasy is the maximal community test.
13.13: yatzeʾu anashim bĕnei-veliyaʿal (yāṣĕʾû ʾănāšîm bĕnê-bĕliyyaʿal): 'Worthless men have gone out from among you.' bĕnê bĕliyyaʿal (sons of Belial/worthlessness): bĕliyyaʿal = bĕlî (without) + yaʿal (profit/worth) — 'men of no worth.' In later usage Belial becomes a name for Satan (2 Cor 6:15). midduchê (enticement): the same root as niddaḥ (driven away) — the worthless men push (dāḥâ) the city toward other gods.
13.14: vedarashta vehiqarta vechalqarta heitev: 'Inquire, investigate, and question thoroughly.' The judicial process for the city charge: dāraš (inquire, seek out), ḥāqar (investigate), šāʾal (ask, question) — all in the piel/intensive form. hêṭēb (thoroughly, well): the thorough-investigation requirement protects against mob justice. The same investigative standard applies to individual capital cases (17:4; 19:18).
13.15: hakov takov et-yoshevei hair hahi lefi-ḥarev: 'Strike the inhabitants of that city with the edge of the sword.' If the investigation confirms apostasy, the city receives the ḥērem (devoted-thing ban). ḥāram taḥărîm ʾōtāh: the double infinitive absolute (ḥāram taḥărîm) marks the totality of the destruction. The devoted city becomes like Canaanite cities under the ban — the apostasy of an Israelite city is the functional equivalent of the Canaanite opposition to YHWH.
13.16: veet-kol-shelala tiqbots el-toch rechova: 'Gather all its spoil into the middle of its open square.' rĕḥōb (open square, broad place): the central public space. The spoil (šālāl) and the city itself are burned as a ḥērem to YHWH (kālîl laYhwh ʾĕlōhêkā — a whole offering to YHWH). Unlike normal war plunder which is distributed, the devoted-city's wealth returns entirely to YHWH — it cannot enrich Israel. The city is never to be rebuilt.
13.17: veLo-yidbaq beyadcha meuma min-haḥerem: 'Let nothing devoted remain in your hand.' dābaq (cling, adhere): the same hold-fast verb used positively for covenant loyalty (v.4; 11:22) — here the negative: nothing of the ḥērem shall cling to you. Achan's sin in Josh 7:1-26 is the enacted violation of this principle — one man's taking from the devoted things brings defeat on the whole community. The devoted-city's total destruction is the purity required for YHWH to 'turn from his fierce anger' (šûb mēḥărôn ʾappô).
13.18: ki tishmaʿ beqol YHWH eloheicha: 'When you obey the voice of YHWH your God.' The closing covenant formula: obedience (šāmaʿ bĕqôl) to all the commandments produces YHWH's mercy (raḥămîm) and compassion and multiplication. laʿăśôt hayyāšār bĕʿênê Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā (doing what is right in the eyes of YHWH your God): the positive form of the formula that v.8 condemned in its chaotic negative version. The chapter that began with the false prophet test closes with the promise of covenant restoration through obedience.

26.5: Arami oved avi vayered Mitzraymah vayagar sham bimtei me'at vayehi sham legoy gadol atzum varav (ʾărammî ʾōbēd ʾābî wayyēred miṣrāymāh wayyāgār šām bimtê mĕʿāṭ wayyĕhî šām lĕgôy gādôl ʿāṣûm wārāb): 'A wandering Aramean was my father, and he went down to Egypt and sojourned there, few in number, and there he became a nation, great, mighty, and populous.' This sentence — the opening of the first-fruits liturgical creed (vv. 5-10) — is Israel's compressed covenant history in confessional form. Arami oved (wandering/perishing Aramean): oved is the participle of abad (to perish, to be lost, to be destroyed), the same root as eved (servant/slave) — though different. The father is Jacob (whose mother was Aramean, and who lived among Arameans); he was one who was about to perish, a non-entity. From this near-extinction, the nation of Israel emerged — the covenant pivot from one man's destitution to a great nation. Paul's doctrine of election (not many wise... not many powerful... God chose what is foolish, 1 Cor 1:26-29) follows the same logic: the covenant community is defined by what it was before YHWH chose it, not by what it was in itself.
26.17: et-YHWH he'emarta hayom lihyot lecha l'Elohim uve'lekhta bidrachav ve'lishmor chukkav umitzvotav umishpatav velishmo'a beqolo. ve'YHWH he'emircha hayom lihyot lo le'am segulah (ʾet-Yhwh heʾĕmartā hayyôm lihyôt lĕkā lēʾlōhîm ûbĕleket bidĕrākāyw wĕlišmōr ḥuqqāyw ûmiṣwōtāyw ûmišpāṭāyw wĕlišmōaʿ bĕqōlô. wYhwh heʾĕmîrkā hayyôm lihyôt lô lĕʿam sĕgullāh): 'You have caused YHWH to declare today that he will be your God... and YHWH has caused you to declare today that you will be his people of treasured possession.' The Hiphil form heʾĕmartā and heʾĕmîrkā are both causatives of amar (to say/declare) — literally, you have caused him to say, and he has caused you to say. This is covenant ratification language: the two parties publicly commit each other to their respective roles before witnesses. The mutual covenant-declaration structure (Israel declares YHWH as God; YHWH declares Israel as his treasured people) is the OT form of what Paul describes as adoption (Rom 8:15-16: the Spirit of adoption by whom we cry Abba, Father — the Spirit bearing witness with our spirit that we are children of God).
26.1: veha'ya ki-tavo el-ha'aretz asher YHWH Eloheicha noten lecha nachalah uvarishtah veyashavta vah (wĕhāyâ kî-tāb̠ōʾ ʾel-hāʾāreṣ ʾăšer Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā nōtēn lĕkā nĕḥālâ ûb̠ĕrišhtāh wĕyāšab̠tā b̠āh): 'And it shall be when you come into the land that the LORD your God gives you as an inheritance, and you possess it and dwell in it.' The verse frames the first-fruits ritual as a future obligation tied to the gift of land. Nachalah (inheritance, portion): the land is not property acquired but a covenant inheritance granted — the same term used for the tribal allotments and for Israel's own status as YHWH's nachalah (cf. Deut 4:20). Uvarishtah (you possess it) from yarash: to dispossess, to inherit by taking possession. The first-fruits law is prospective — a liturgical response embedded into the act of settlement itself, so that the memory of gift does not erode into an assumption of entitlement.
26.2: velakachta mereishit kol-pri ha'adamah asher tavi me'artzecha... vesamt batel vehalacht el-hamaqom asher yivchar YHWH Eloheicha leshakken shemo sham (wĕlāqaḥtā mērēʾšît kol-pĕrî hāʾădāmâ... wĕśamtā b̠aṭene wĕhālaḵtā ʾel-hammāqôm ʾăšer yib̠ḥar Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā lĕšakkēn šĕmô šām): Take the first of all the fruit of the ground and put it in a basket, then go to the place that YHWH shall choose to make his name dwell there. Reishit (first, beginning): the first of the harvest is set apart — not because it is better but because it is first, a synecdoche for the whole. Batel (basket, container): the portable vessel for presenting the offering. Hamaqom asher yivchar (the place YHWH shall choose): the centralizing formula of Deuteronomy, pointing to Jerusalem and the Temple. First-fruits are not distributed locally but brought to the singular sanctuary — the spatial corollary of monotheism.
26.3: uvata el-hakohen asher yihyeh bayamim hahem ve'amarta elav higadeti hayom l'YHWH Eloheicha ki-vati el-ha'aretz asher nishba YHWH la'avoteinu latet lanu (ûb̠āʾtā ʾel-hakkōhēn ʾăšer yihyê b̠ayyāmîm hāhēm wĕʾāmartā ʾēlāyw higgadtî hayyôm lYhwh ʾĕlōhêkā kî-b̠āʾtî ʾel-hāʾāreṣ ʾăšer nišbaʿ Yhwh lāʾăb̠ôtênû lātēt lānû): 'And you shall come to the priest who is in office in those days and say to him, I declare today to the LORD your God that I have come into the land that the LORD swore to our fathers to give us.' Higadeti (I declare, I report, I make known) from nagad: the formal declaration is performative — the gift of land is publicly acknowledged as a fulfilled oath. Nishba (swore): the oath to the patriarchs (Gen 12:7; 13:15; 15:18) has been kept; the worshipper's presence at the sanctuary with first-fruits is its proof.
26.4: velakach hakohen hatel miyadecha vehinichah lifnei mizbach YHWH Eloheicha (wĕlāqaḥ hakkōhēn haṭṭene miyyāḏĕkā wĕhinnîḥô lip̄nê mizbēaḥ Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā): 'And the priest shall take the basket from your hand and set it down before the altar of the LORD your God.' The priest's action mediates the transfer: the basket moves from the hand of the worshipper to the presence of YHWH at the altar. Hinichah lifnei (set it down before): placement before the altar is the spatial equivalent of presenting it to YHWH himself — the altar as the meeting point of human offering and divine presence. The priest functions here not as a sacrificial agent but as a courier of the worshipper's declaration.
26.6: vayarei'u otanu haMitzrim vaye'anunu vayitnu aleinu avodah kashah (wayyārēʿû ʾōtānû hammiṣrîm wayyĕʿannûnû wayyitĕnû ʿālênû ʿăb̠ôdâ qāšâ): 'And the Egyptians treated us harshly and afflicted us, and laid on us hard labor.' Part of the recited creed (vv. 5-10). Three verbs of oppression: vayarei'u (made things bad for us, treated us ill — ra'a), vaye'anunu (afflicted us — anah: to humble, to oppress), avodah kashah (hard labor — kasha: harsh, severe). The liturgical creed encodes the memory of bondage so that prosperity never erases the knowledge of what Israel was without YHWH's intervention.
26.7: vanitztaq el-YHWH Elohei avoteinu vayishma YHWH et-qoleinu vayar et-onyenu ve'et-amaleinu ve'et-lachatzenu (wanniṣʿaq ʾel-Yhwh ʾĕlōhê ʾăb̠ôtênû wayyišmaʿ Yhwh ʾet-qōlênû wayyarʾ ʾet-ʿonyênû wĕʾet-ʿămālênû wĕʾet-laḥăṣênû): 'And we cried to the LORD, the God of our fathers, and the LORD heard our voice, and saw our affliction, our toil, and our oppression.' Three terms for suffering: oni (affliction, humiliation), amal (toil, travail), lachatz (oppression, pressure). The creed emphasizes YHWH's perception — he heard (shama) and saw (ra'ah) — the divine attention that precedes rescue. This forms the theological grammar of the Exodus: cry → YHWH hears and sees → deliverance.
26.8: vayotzi'enu YHWH miMitzrayim beyad chazakah uvizroa netuyah uvemorah gadol uviotot uvemoftim (wayyôṣiʾênû Yhwh mimmîṣrāyim b̠ĕyād ḥăzāqâ ûb̠izrôaʿ nĕṭûyâ ûb̠emôrāʾ gādôl ûb̠ʾōtôt ûb̠emōp̄tîm): 'And the LORD brought us out of Egypt with a mighty hand and an outstretched arm, with great deeds of terror, with signs and wonders.' The four-fold epitome of the Exodus: yad chazakah (mighty hand), zroa netuyah (outstretched arm), morah gadol (great awe/terror), otot umoftim (signs and wonders). This formula recurs throughout Deuteronomy (4:34; 7:19; 11:2) as the canonical summary of YHWH's redemptive act. The creed performs the Exodus anew each time it is spoken — liturgical memory as identity formation.
26.9: vayevi'enu el-hamaqom hazeh vayiten lanu et-ha'aretz hazot eretz zavat chalav udvash (wayyĕb̠iʾênû ʾel-hammāqôm hazzê wayyittēn-lānû ʾet-hāʾāreṣ hazzōʾt ʾereṣ zāb̠at ḥālāb̠ ûd̠ĕb̠aš): 'And he brought us to this place and gave us this land, a land flowing with milk and honey.' The creed reaches its destination: from Aram to Egypt to the wilderness to this place. Eretz zavat chalav udvash (land flowing with milk and honey): the covenant formula first spoken in Exod 3:8 and repeated throughout Deuteronomy. Zavat from zub (to flow): the land is abundant in pastoral and agricultural produce — milk (livestock) and honey (wild honey or date honey, dibs). The creed culminates in gift: YHWH's action ends not in rescue alone but in bestowal.
26.10: ve'atah hinei heveti et-reishit pri ha'adamah asher-natata li YHWH vehinhachto lifnei YHWH Eloheicha vehishtachavita lifnei YHWH Eloheicha (wĕʿattâ hinnê hēb̠ēʾtî ʾet-rēʾšît pĕrî hāʾădāmâ ʾăšer-nātattā lî Yhwh wĕhinnăḥtô lip̄nê Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā wĕhištaḥăwîtā lip̄nê Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā): 'And now, behold, I have brought the first of the fruit of the ground, which you, O LORD, have given me. And you shall set it down before the LORD your God and worship before the LORD your God.' The creed pivots to the present act: the historical recitation (vv. 5-9) ends in ve'atah (and now, therefore) — the past determines the present obligation. Hinei heveti (here I bring): the gesture of presentation is the embodied response to the history just recited. Vehishtachavita (and you shall bow down/worship): the presentation culminates in prostration — acknowledgment of lordship.
26.11: vesimachta bechol-hatov asher natan lecha YHWH Eloheicha ulveitecha atta vehaLevi veha'ger asher beqirbecha (wĕśāmaḥtā b̠ĕḵol-haṭṭôb̠ ʾăšer nātan lĕkā Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā ûlĕb̠êtĕkā ʾattâ wĕhalLēwî wĕhagēr ʾăšer b̠ĕqirb̠ĕkā): 'And you shall rejoice in all the good that the LORD your God has given to you and to your house, you, and the Levite, and the sojourner who is among you.' Vesimachta (and you shall rejoice): the ritual of first-fruits ends not in solemn duty but in communal joy. Three parties rejoice together: the Israelite householder (atta uveitekha), the Levite (who has no land allotment), and the ger (sojourner/stranger). The economic vulnerability of the Levite and the alien makes their inclusion in covenant joy theologically significant — the gift of land extends its circle beyond those who legally hold it.
26.12: ki techale la'aser et-kol-ma'aser tevuatecha bashanah hashlishit shenat hama'aser venatata laLevi lager layatom velalmana ve'achlu vishveicha vesave'u (kî tĕḵallê laʿaśēr ʾet-kol-maʿăśar tĕb̠ûʾātĕkā baššānâ haššĕlîšît šĕnat hamma'ăśēr wĕnātattā lalLēwî laggēr lāyyātôm wĕlāʾalmānâ wĕʾāḵĕlû b̠išʿārêkā wĕśāb̠ĕʿû): 'When you have finished paying all the tithe of your produce in the third year, which is the year of tithing, you shall give it to the Levite, the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow, that they may eat within your towns and be filled.' The third-year tithe (ma'aser sheni variant: the triennial tithe for the poor, distinct from the annual tithe consumed at the sanctuary) redirects the offering to local distribution. The four-fold welfare beneficiary list: Levite, ger, yatom (orphan), almanah (widow) — the same quartet of the vulnerable that recurs throughout Deuteronomy (cf. 14:29; 16:11, 14; 24:17-19). The law institutionalizes welfare as a tithe-obligation, not voluntary charity.
26.13: ve'amarta lifnei YHWH Eloheicha bi'arti haqodesh min-habayit vegam netativ laLevi velager layatom velalmana kechol-mitzvaticha asher tzivitani lo-avarti mimitzvotelcha velo-shachachti (wĕʾāmartā lip̄nê Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā biʿartî haqqōḏeš min-hab̠bayit wĕgam nĕtattîw lalLēwî wĕlaggēr layyātôm wĕlāʾalmānâ kĕḵol-miṣwātĕkā ʾăšer ṣiwwîtānî lōʾ-ʿāb̠artî mimmîṣwōtêkā wĕlōʾ šāḵāḥtî): 'Then you shall say before the LORD your God, I have removed the sacred portion (haqodesh) out of my house, and moreover I have given it to the Levite, the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow, according to all your commandment that you have commanded me. I have not transgressed any of your commandments, nor have I forgotten any of them.' The tithe declaration is a covenant compliance report. Bi'arti haqodesh (I have cleared out the sacred portion): bi'er means to remove, clear out, consume entirely — the tithe must be completely distributed, not stored or hoarded. The declaration is first-person, specific, and addressed directly to YHWH — a covenant audit conducted in divine presence.
26.14: lo-achalti ve'oni mimenu velo-bi'arti mimenu betame'a velo-natati mimenu leMet shamati beqol YHWH Elohai asiti kechol asher tzivitani (lōʾ-ʾāḵaltî b̠ĕʾônî mimmennû wĕlōʾ-b̠iʿartî mimmennû b̠ĕṭāmēʾ wĕlōʾ-nātattî mimmennû lāmēt šāmaʿtî b̠ĕqôl Yhwh ʾĕlōhāy ʿāśîtî kĕḵōl ʾăšer ṣiwwîtānî): 'I have not eaten of the tithe while I was mourning, or removed any of it while I was unclean, or offered any of it to the dead. I have obeyed the voice of the LORD my God. I have done according to all that you have commanded me.' Three negative prohibitions — not eaten in mourning (oni), not handled while ritually impure (tame'), not used it in funerary offerings (laMet, for the dead). Each addresses a specific contamination risk: grief-eating might compromise the joyful character of the offering; ritual impurity would defile a sacred portion; funerary use would blend covenant worship with ancestor cult. The declaration is a formal purity oath.
26.15: hashkifah mimeon kodshecha min-hashamayim uvarech et-amecha et-Yisrael ve'et ha'adamah asher natata lanu ka'asher nishbata la'avoteinu eretz zavat chalav udvash (hašqîp̄â mimmĕʿôn qoḏšĕkā min-haššāmayim ûb̠āreḵ ʾet-ʿammĕkā ʾet-Yiśrāʾēl wĕʾēt hāʾădāmâ ʾăšer nātattā lānû kāʾăšer nišbaʿtā lāʾăb̠ôtênû ʾereṣ zāb̠at ḥālāb̠ ûd̠ĕb̠aš): 'Look down from your holy habitation, from heaven, and bless your people Israel and the ground that you have given us, as you swore to our fathers, a land flowing with milk and honey.' Hashkifah (look down): the verb shaqaf implies a peering downward from above, the posture of a sovereign surveying his domain. Me'on kodshecha (your holy habitation): heaven itself is YHWH's dwelling — the sanctuary on earth is a copy of the heavenly archetype (cf. Exod 25:40; Heb 9:24). The worshipper who has completed the tithe now appeals from the completed act on earth to YHWH's vantage in heaven — compliance enables intercession.
26.16: hayom hazeh YHWH Eloheicha metzavecha la'asot et-hachuqqim ha'eleh ve'et-hamishpatim ushemartem va'asitem otam bechol-levavecha uvechol-nafshecha (hayyôm hazzê Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā mĕṣawwĕkā laʿăśôt ʾet-haḥuqqîm hāʾēllê wĕʾet-hamišpāṭîm ûšĕmartem waʿăśîtem ʾôtām b̠ĕḵol-lĕb̠āb̠ĕkā ûb̠ĕḵol-nap̄šĕkā): 'This day the LORD your God commands you to do these statutes and rules. You shall therefore be careful to do them with all your heart and with all your soul.' Hayom hazeh (this day): the Deuteronomic emphasis on contemporaneity — the commandment is given not to the wilderness generation alone but to every generation that hears it (cf. 5:3; 29:14). The Shema vocabulary: bechol-levavecha uvechol-nafshecha (with all your heart and with all your soul) — covenant obedience requires total disposition, not merely external compliance.
26.18: veYHWH he'emircha hayom lihyot lo leam segulah ka'asher diber lach velishmor kol-mitzvotav (wYhwh heʾemîrĕkā hayyôm lihyôt lô lĕʿam sĕgullâ kāʾăšer dib̠ber lāk wĕlišmôr kol-miṣwōtāyw): 'And the LORD has avouched you this day to be a people for his treasured possession, as he has promised you, and to keep all his commandments.' He'emircha: as with v17, the Hiphil of amar means to cause to declare or to declare solemnly — YHWH has formally declared Israel to be his. Am segulah (treasured people, people of special possession): segulah in Akkadian means moveable personal wealth of the king — the private treasure of the sovereign. Israel is not merely YHWH's people but his personal treasure (cf. Exod 19:5; Mal 3:17). The covenant is a mutual declaration of exclusive belonging.
26.19: ultetecha elyon al kol-hagoyim asher asah litehilah uleShem uletifaret ulihyot am kadosh l'YHWH Eloheicha ka'asher diber (ûlĕtittĕkā ʿelyôn ʿal kol-haggôyîm ʾăšer ʿāśâ litĕhillâ ûlĕšēm ûlĕtip̄ʾāret wĕlihyôt ʿam qāḏôš lYhwh ʾĕlōhêkā kāʾăšer dib̠ber): 'And he will set you in praise and in fame and in honor high above all nations that he has made, and that you shall be a people holy to the LORD your God, as he has spoken.' Three-fold elevation: tehilah (praise, renown), shem (name, reputation), tifaret (glory, splendor) — Israel is to be the showcase of YHWH's covenant faithfulness among the nations. Am qadosh (holy people): qadosh (set apart, consecrated) — Israel's holiness is derivative, not intrinsic; it flows from YHWH's sanctifying choice. The NT reapplies this language to the church as the new covenant people (1 Pet 2:9).

27.5: uvaneita sham mizbea'ch l'YHWH Eloheicha mizbea'ch avanim lo-tanif aleihem barzel (ûb̠ānîtā šām mizbēaḥ laYhwh ʾĕlōhêkā mizbēaḥ ʾăbānîm lōʾ-tānîp̄ ʿălêhem barzel): 'And you shall build there an altar to the LORD your God, an altar of stones. You shall not wield an iron tool on them.' The altar at Mt. Ebal (the curses-mountain) is to be built from uncut, undressed stones — natural stones untouched by iron tools. The same prohibition appears in Exod 20:25: to wield an iron tool on the stone would defile it (wa-tachallelah). The principle is that YHWH's altar must not be shaped by human craft — the natural roughness of the stone is the appropriate approach to a holy altar. The contrast with Solomon's temple (1 Kings 6:7: no hammer, chisel, or any iron tool was heard in the house) maintains the same principle: the place of divine encounter is not a human achievement but a divine provision. The roughness of Ebal's altar amid the covenant curses may foreshadow the cross — the rough wood of a tree, a place of curse (Deut 21:23), becoming the altar of the covenant.
27.26: arur asher lo-yakim et-divrei hatorah hazot la'asot otam ve'amar kol-ha'am amen (ʾārûr ʾăšer lōʾ-yāqîm ʾet-dibrê hattôrāh hazzōʾt laʿăśôt ʾôtām wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who does not confirm the words of this law by doing them. And all the people shall say, Amen.' The climactic curse of the Deuteronomic ceremony. Arur (cursed) is a performative declaration: to speak arur over someone is to invoke covenant malediction, the opposite of baruch (blessed). Yakim (confirm, uphold, establish) from qum — to stand/rise; in the Hiphil: to cause to stand, to establish. The requirement is not merely to hear or to intend but to do (la'asot). Paul's citation of this verse in Galatians 3:10 is decisive: all who rely on works of the law are under a curse, for it is written, cursed is everyone who does not abide by all things written in the book of the law and do them. The Deuteronomic curse reveals the law's own logic: it requires total performance, which no one achieves, making it a curse-pronouncing instrument rather than a salvation vehicle. Christ redeems from this curse (Gal 3:13) by himself becoming the arur of Deut 21:23 — cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree.
27.1: vayetzav Moshe veziqnei Yisrael et-ha'am lemor shamor et-kol-hamitzvah asher anochi metzaveh etchem hayom (wayyĕṣaw mōšê wĕziqnê yiśrāʾēl ʾet-hāʿām lēʾmōr šāmôr ʾet-kol-hammiṣwâ ʾăšer ʾānōḵî mĕṣawwê ʾetḵem hayyôm): Moses and the elders of Israel command the people to keep all the commandment this day. The plural authority — Moses and the elders — signals the beginning of the covenant ratification ceremony that will be performed at Shechem (between Gerizim and Ebal). The ziqnei Yisrael (elders of Israel) act as co-commanders, representing the people's representative leadership structures that will persist after Moses' death. The ceremony is prospective but commanded now — obedience is not waiting for entry but is already demanded.
27.2: vehayah beyom asher ta'avru et-haYarden el-ha'aretz asher YHWH Eloheicha noten lach vahaqimota lecha avanim gedolot vesadta otam beshid (wĕhāyâ b̠ayyôm ʾăšer taʿăb̠rû ʾet-hayyardēn ʾel-hāʾāreṣ ʾăšer Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā nōtēn lāk wĕhăqîmōtā lĕkā ʾăb̠ānîm gĕdōlôt wĕśadtā ʾôtām b̠aśśîḏ): 'And on the day you cross the Jordan to the land that the LORD your God is giving you, you shall set up large stones and plaster them with plaster.' Avanim gedolot (great/large stones): standing stones as monuments and law-carriers. Sadta otam beshid (plaster them with plaster): the inscribed law is made durable and public — plastered steles were a known ancient practice (cf. the Karnak reliefs and Amarna correspondence). The Jordan crossing is the threshold event; the monument is to be raised on the same day — immediacy signals the covenant's urgency.
27.3: vekatavta aleihem et-kol-divrei hatorah hazot be'ovrecha lema'an asher tavo el-ha'aretz asher YHWH Eloheicha noten lach eretz zavat chalav udvash ka'asher diber YHWH Elohi avoteicha lach (wĕkātab̠tā ʿălêhem ʾet-kol-dibrê hattôrāh hazzōʾt b̠ĕʿob̠rĕkā lĕmaʿan ʾăšer tāb̠ōʾ ʾel-hāʾāreṣ ʾăšer Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā nōtēn lāk ʾereṣ zāb̠at ḥālāb̠ ûd̠ĕb̠aš kāʾăšer dib̠ber Yhwh ʾĕlōhê ʾăb̠ôtêkā lāk): Write on the stones all the words of this law when you cross over, so that you may enter the land YHWH your God is giving you, a land of milk and honey as he promised. The writing of the law is the precondition for inhabiting the land. Kol-divrei hatorah hazot (all the words of this law): the totality of the covenant document — not a selection but the complete Torah — is to be publicly inscribed. The law is not esoteric or priestly property but a public text accessible to all.
27.4: vehayah be'ovretchem et-haYarden taqimu et-ha'avanim ha'eleh asher anochi metzaveh etchem hayom behar Eival vesadta otam beshid (wĕhāyâ b̠ĕʿob̠rĕḵem ʾet-hayyardēn tāqîmû ʾet-hāʾăb̠ānîm hāʾēllê ʾăšer ʾānōḵî mĕṣawwê ʾetḵem hayyôm b̠ĕhar ʿêb̠āl wĕśadtā ʾôtām b̠aśśîḏ): Set up these stones on mount Ebal — the mountain of cursing. The choice of Eival (Ebal) for the inscription is theologically provocative: the law is written on the mountain of curses, not the mountain of blessings. This sequence underscores that the Torah's primary function in the fallen human condition is to expose transgression (cf. Rom 3:20; Gal 3:19) — the law reveals the curse before it reveals the path to blessing. The Samaritan Pentateuch reads Gerizim here (a textual variant driven by Samaritan interests in their sacred mountain).
27.6: avanim shelemot tibne et-mizbach YHWH Eloheicha veha'alita alav olot l'YHWH Eloheicha (ʾăb̠ānîm šĕlēmôt tib̠nê ʾet-mizbēaḥ Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā wĕhaʿălîtā ʿālāyw ʿōlōt lYhwh ʾĕlōhêkā): 'You shall build the altar of the LORD your God with whole stones, and offer burnt offerings on it to the LORD your God.' Avanim shelemot (whole stones, complete/sound stones): the same principle as v5's prohibition of iron tools — the altar must be built with natural, unworked stones. Shalem (whole, complete, at peace) shares a root with shalom; the altar's wholeness mirrors the peace it facilitates. Olot (burnt offerings, whole offerings): the olah is entirely consumed on the altar — the first offering on the Ebal altar is one of total consecration.
27.7: vezavachta shelamim ve'achalta sham vesimachta lifnei YHWH Eloheicha (wĕzāb̠aḥtā šĕlāmîm wĕʾāḵaltā šām wĕśāmaḥtā lip̄nê Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā): 'And you shall offer peace offerings and shall eat there, and you shall rejoice before the LORD your God.' Shelamim (peace offerings, shared offerings): unlike the olah (entirely burned), the shelamim are shared between the altar (fat portions), the priests, and the worshipper's household — a communion meal in divine presence. Vesimachta (you shall rejoice): on the mountain of curses, after the law is inscribed and the offerings made, covenant joy is commanded. The sequence — law inscription → whole burnt offering → shared communion meal → joy — is the covenant pattern: accountability, consecration, reconciliation, celebration.
27.8: uchtavta al-ha'avanim et kol-divrei hatorah hazot be'er heitiv (ûḵtab̠tā ʿal-hāʾăb̠ānîm ʾet kol-dibrê hattôrāh hazzōʾt b̠āʾēr hêṭēb̠): 'And you shall write on the stones all the words of this law very plainly.' Be'er heitiv (very plainly, making it plain and clear): be'er from ba'ar — to explain, to make clear, to engrave. The Septuagint translates saphōs (clearly). The word deuteronomion in the Septuagint's name for this book comes from a related phrase in 17:18 (mishneh hatorah hazot — a copy/repetition of this law); here the law is written plainly so that all who pass may read it without priestly mediation. The publicness of the Torah is a mark of its covenant character.
27.9: vayedaber Moshe vehakohanim haLevi'im el-kol-Yisrael lemor hasket ushma Yisrael hayom hazeh nihyeita le'am l'YHWH Eloheicha (waydab̠bēr mōšê wĕhakkōhănîm hălĕwiyyim ʾel-kol-yiśrāʾēl lēʾmōr hasqēt ûšmaʿ yiśrāʾēl hayyôm hazzê nihyêtā lĕʿam lYhwh ʾĕlōhêkā): 'Then Moses and the Levitical priests said to all Israel, Keep silence and hear, O Israel: this day you have become the people of the LORD your God.' Hasket (keep silence, be still, pay attention): a strong command for attentive listening. Nihyeita le'am (you have become a people): the covenant constitution is being publicly ratified — in this ceremonial moment, the covenant relationship is formally enacted. The Levitical priests speak alongside Moses, signaling the priestly mediation of Torah that will continue after Moses' death.
27.10: ushama'ta beqol YHWH Eloheicha ve'asita et-mitzvotav ve'et-chuqqav asher anochi metzavecha hayom (ûšāmaʿtā b̠ĕqôl Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā wĕʿāśîtā ʾet-miṣwōtāyw wĕʾet-ḥuqqāyw ʾăšer ʾānōḵî mĕṣawwĕkā hayyôm): 'You shall therefore obey the voice of the LORD your God, keeping his commandments and his statutes, which I command you today.' Shama' beqol (hear, obey the voice): shema with the preposition be — to listen into, to act upon hearing. The Shema pattern is repeated: hearing is the mode of covenant relationship; obedience is the content of hearing. The verse functions as the response to the proclamation of v9 — the newly constituted people's obligation immediately follows their constitution.
27.11: vayetzav Moshe et-ha'am bayom hahu lemor (wayyĕṣaw mōšê ʾet-hāʿām bayyôm hahûʾ lēʾmōr): 'And Moses commanded the people on that day, saying.' A brief hinge verse introducing the tribal assignment for the Gerizim-Ebal ceremony. Bayom hahu (on that day): the ceremony is tied to the specific day of crossing — the ratification of the covenant in Canaan is not deferred. Moses' authoritative command allocates the tribes to their positions, ensuring the ceremony's order is divinely specified rather than improvised.
27.12: eleh ya'amdu levarech et-ha'am al-har Gerizim be'ovretchem et-haYarden Shimon veLevi viYhudah veYissachar veYosef uvBinyamin (ʾēllê yaʿamdû lĕb̠ārēk ʾet-hāʿām ʿal-har gĕrizzîm b̠ĕʿob̠rĕḵem ʾet-hayyardēn šimʿôn wĕlēwî wîhûdâ wĕyiśśāḵār wĕyôsēp̄ ûb̠inyāmîn): 'These shall stand on mount Gerizim to bless the people when you have crossed the Jordan: Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Joseph, and Benjamin.' Six tribes on Gerizim (blessing): note that both Levi and Joseph (Ephraim/Manasseh) are among the blessing-tribes — Levi here as the priestly mediator, Joseph as the double-portioned heir. The assignment is not by geographic proximity or birth order — it follows a theological logic that later commentators have debated (e.g., the Rachel-and-Leah tribal groupings).
27.13: ve'eleh ya'amdu al-haqelalah behar Eival Reuven Gad ve'Asher uZevulun Dan veNaftali (wĕʾēllê yaʿamdû ʿal-haqqĕlālâ b̠ĕhar ʿêb̠āl rĕʾûb̠ēn gāḏ wĕʾāšēr ûzĕb̠ûlun dān wĕnap̄tālî): 'And these shall stand for the curse on mount Ebal: Reuben, Gad, Asher, Zebulun, Dan, and Naphtali.' Six tribes on Ebal (curse). Reuben stands on the curse-side despite being the firstborn — his disqualification (Gen 35:22; 49:3-4) shadows him even in the ceremony. Al-haqelalah (for the curse, over the curse): the tribes do not themselves curse but stand as witnesses and responders to the curses proclaimed by the Levites below (vv. 14-26). The geographic staging — twin mountains facing each other — dramatizes the covenant's binary: blessing or curse, obedience or disobedience.
27.14: ve'anu haLevi'im ve'amru el-kol-ish Yisrael qol ram (wĕʿānû hallĕwiyyim wĕʾāmrû ʾel-kol-ʾîš yiśrāʾēl qôl rām): 'And the Levites shall declare to all the men of Israel with a loud voice.' Qol ram (a high/loud voice): the curses are proclaimed audibly, publicly, across the valley between the two mountains — the volume ensures that all Israel hears and cannot claim ignorance. The Levites' mediating role (cf. v9) is here performative: they enact the covenant ceremony by declaration. The use of qol ram anticipates the Mishnaic description of the ceremony as a liturgical responsory (m. Sotah 7:5).
27.15: arur ha'ish asher ya'aseh pesel umasechah to'avat YHWH ma'aseh yedei charash veSam basater ve'anu kol-ha'am ve'amru Amen (ʾārûr hāʾîš ʾăšer yaʿăśê p̄esel ûmasēḵâ tôʿăb̠at Yhwh maʿăśê yĕdê ḥārāš wĕśām bassēter wĕʿānû kol-hāʿām wĕʾāmrû ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be the man who makes a carved or cast metal image, an abomination to the LORD, a thing made by the hands of a craftsman, and sets it up in secret. And all the people shall answer and say, Amen.' The dodecalogue of curses (vv. 15-26) begins with idolatry — the foundational covenant violation. Arur (cursed): a performative declaration of exclusion from covenant blessing. Basater (in secret): the curse specifically targets hidden idolatry — public apostasy could be addressed by courts, but the arur curses penetrate what human courts cannot see. The communal Amen is the congregation's self-imprecation: each person accepts the curse as applicable to themselves should they commit this act.
27.16: arur meqalel aviv ve'imo ve'amar kol-ha'am Amen (ʾārûr mĕqallēl ʾāb̠îw wĕʾimmô wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who dishonors his father or his mother. And all the people shall say, Amen.' Meqalel (dishonors, treats lightly, curses): from qalal — to be light, to treat as worthless, the opposite of kaved (to honor, to make heavy/weighty — cf. the Fifth Commandment, Deut 5:16). The curse again targets the hidden act: public dishonoring of parents could be prosecuted under the law (21:18-21), but the arur addresses contempt that is performed in secret or through subtle patterns of neglect.
27.17: arur masig gevul re'ehu ve'amar kol-ha'am Amen (ʾārûr massîg gĕb̠ûl rēʿēhû wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who moves his neighbor's landmark. And all the people shall say, Amen.' Masig gevul (moves a boundary marker, encroaches on a landmark): gevul is a property boundary — the stone markers that defined land allotments in Israel. Moving them was a secret act of theft that also violated the covenant's land theology: the land was YHWH's gift allocated to each family by lot; encroaching on a neighbor's allotment was encroaching on what YHWH had given them. Cf. Prov 22:28; 23:10; Job 24:2.
27.18: arur mashgeh iver baderech ve'amar kol-ha'am Amen (ʾārûr mašgê ʿiwwēr b̠addāreḵ wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who misleads a blind person on the road. And all the people shall say, Amen.' Mashgeh iver (makes the blind wander/go astray): from shagah — to lead astray, to cause to err. The blind person's inability to verify the directions given makes them wholly dependent on the guide's honesty. Leviticus 19:14 prohibits this in positive form: 'you shall not put a stumbling block before the blind.' The vulnerability of the disabled creates an obligation of care that the law enforces through the covenant curse.
27.19: arur mateh mishpat ger yatom ve'almanah ve'amar kol-ha'am Amen (ʾārûr maṭṭê mišpaṭ gēr yātôm wĕʾalmānâ wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who perverts the justice due to the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow. And all the people shall say, Amen.' Mateh mishpat (perverts justice, bends the judgment): natah in the Hiphil — to cause to turn aside, to deflect what is straight. The three parties again: ger, yatom, almanah — those without legal advocates in a patriarchal society. Judicial perversion of their cases would be done secretly (bribed judges, false witnesses) — making it a prime candidate for the arur curses. The prophets return repeatedly to this violation as evidence of covenant failure (Isa 1:17, 23; Jer 7:6; Zech 7:10).
27.20: arur shochev im-eshet aviv ki gilah kenaf aviv ve'amar kol-ha'am Amen (ʾārûr šōḵēb̠ ʿim-ʾēšet ʾāb̠îw kî gillâ kĕnap̄ ʾāb̠îw wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who lies with his father's wife, because he has uncovered his father's nakedness. And all the people shall say, Amen.' Eshet aviv (his father's wife): includes the stepmother — any wife of the father, not necessarily the man's own mother. Gilah kenaf aviv (he has uncovered his father's skirt/wing): sexual union with a wife is expressed as being under her husband's protective wing (kanaf, cf. Ruth 3:9; Ezek 16:8). To lie with the father's wife is to usurp the father's authority and violate his most intimate boundary — echoing Reuben's disqualification (Gen 35:22; 49:4).
27.21: arur shochev im-kol-behemah ve'amar kol-ha'am Amen (ʾārûr šōḵēb̠ ʿim-kol-bĕhēmâ wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who lies with any kind of animal. And all the people shall say, Amen.' Prohibited in Lev 18:23 and 20:15-16, this act violates the order of creation (species boundaries established at Genesis 1) as well as the covenant code. Its inclusion in the Ebal curses signals its deep violation of the created order that the covenant is designed to uphold. The universality of kol-behemah (any animal) leaves no exceptions.
27.22: arur shochev im-achoto bat-aviv o vat-imo ve'amar kol-ha'am Amen (ʾārûr šōḵēb̠ ʿim-ʾăḥōtô b̠at-ʾāb̠îw ʾô b̠at-ʾimmô wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who lies with his sister, whether the daughter of his father or the daughter of his mother. And all the people shall say, Amen.' The curse covers both full sisters and half-sisters (paternal and maternal) — Abraham's marriage to his half-sister Sarah (Gen 20:12) belongs to the pre-Sinai era; the covenant code established at Sinai (Lev 18:9, 11) prohibits it and the Deuteronomic curse ratifies that prohibition. The double specification (daughter of father or of mother) closes the definitional loophole Abraham's case might suggest.
27.23: arur shochev im-chamotoh ve'amar kol-ha'am Amen (ʾārûr šōḵēb̠ ʿim-ḥămôtô wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who lies with his mother-in-law. And all the people shall say, Amen.' The prohibition in Lev 18:17 and 20:14 covers this: the mother-in-law relationship creates affinity (the covenant of marriage makes the mother of one's spouse a quasi-mother). The violation would typically occur in household proximity — a hidden act that courts could not easily prosecute but the arur formula reaches.
27.24: arur makeh re'ehu basater ve'amar kol-ha'am Amen (ʾārûr makkê rēʿēhû bassēter wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who strikes down his neighbor in secret. And all the people shall say, Amen.' Makeh re'ehu basater (strikes his neighbor secretly): nakah in the Hiphil — to strike down, possibly fatally. Bassater (in secret) is the key qualifier: public killing could be prosecuted by the avenger of blood and the elders; secret assassination evades human justice. The curse invokes YHWH as the ultimate witness to concealed violence — the covenant's eye penetrates where human courts cannot see.
27.25: arur loqeach shochad lehakot nefesh dam naki ve'amar kol-ha'am Amen (ʾārûr lōqēaḥ šōḥad lĕhakkôt nep̄eš dām nāqî wĕʾāmar kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn): 'Cursed be anyone who takes a bribe to shed innocent blood. And all the people shall say, Amen.' Shochad (bribe): the repeated prohibition of bribery in Deuteronomy (cf. 16:19; 27:25) targets the corruption of the justice system at its point of highest vulnerability — paid witnesses or hired assassins. Dam naki (innocent blood): the blood of the wrongfully killed cries out (Gen 4:10) and defiles the land (Num 35:33-34). The curse against bribe-murder thus protects both covenant justice and the land's purity. Paul's citation of Deut 27:26 (the next verse) in Gal 3:10 implicitly encompasses all these specific curses as evidence of universal human failure under the law.

22.1: "You shall not see your brother's ox or his sheep going astray and ignore them (hitalamta, H5956). You shall take them back to your brother" — hitalamta (H5956, to hide oneself, to pretend not to see): the sin of studied inattention to a neighbor's need. The law prohibits not just theft but indifference. The obligation to return lost property extends to all livestock found wandering without their owner, even if the owner is an enemy (Exod 23:4-5 adds the enemy stipulation). The covenant creates an economy of mutual care: the neighbor's loss is my responsibility to remedy. The anti-indifference principle underlies the Good Samaritan parable (Luke 10:30-37: the Levite who "passed by on the other side" violates the Deuteronomic spirit).
22.2: If the owner is far away or unknown, you shall bring the animal home and keep it until the owner comes looking for it — the burden of temporary stewardship: Israel's law creates a system of responsible custody for lost property rather than the opportunistic appropriation of found goods. The temporary keeping (asafta el-tokh beitecha) is the social infrastructure that assumes community trust and assumed return. The law implicitly prohibits the "finders keepers" principle in favor of the "steward until the owner appears" principle.
22.3: The same law applies to a donkey, a garment, or anything lost by a neighbor — the enumeration broadens the principle beyond livestock to all lost property. The specificity of examples (ox, sheep, donkey, garment, anything) ensures no category of property is exempted by the narrowness of the law's letter. The garment-inclusion is significant: clothing is intimate, personal, and often representing identity — its loss and return is an act of covenant solidarity.
22.4: "You shall not see your brother's donkey or his ox fallen down by the way and ignore them. You shall help him to lift them up" — the active assistance command: not only return of lost property but physical help with a neighbor's struggling animal. The repetition of hitalamta (hide yourself/ignore) from v1 frames both laws: the prohibition is the same indifferent non-response. The "lift up with him" (hakem taqim imo) requires cooperation — this is not a solo task but a communal effort. The double infinitive absolute (hakem taqim) emphasizes the certainty of the obligation.
22.5: "A woman shall not wear a man's garment, nor shall a man put on a woman's cloak, for whoever does these things is an abomination (toevah, H8441) to the LORD your God" — the boundary legislation: keli gever (man's vessel/article) is the broad term for what belongs to the male domain. The toevah term (usually reserved for idolatry and sexual sin) marks this as a boundary violation of high seriousness. The law resists the dissolution of the male-female distinction created in Genesis 1:27 — the sexual differentiation of image-bearers is a creation order that the covenant protects. The specific social context (Canaanite cultic cross-dressing practices in fertility worship) may shape the law's intensity.
22.6: "If you come across a bird's nest in any tree or on the ground, with young ones or eggs and the mother sitting on the young or on the eggs, you shall not take the mother with the young" — the bird nest law: a remarkably tender environmental-ethical instruction interrupting the surrounding social legislation. The mother bird must be released before taking the young. The law's logic includes: (1) ecological sustainability (not destroying breeding pairs); (2) mercy as a character-forming practice extended to non-human life; (3) the covenant's concern for creation-care built into everyday activity.
22.7: "You shall let the mother go, but the young you may take for yourself, that it may go well with you, and that you may live long" — lema'an yitav lach veha'arachta yamim (that it may go well with you and you may prolong days): precisely the life-extension promise attached to honoring parents (Deut 5:16; Eph 6:2-3). The parallelism is theologically significant: mercy shown to a mother bird receives the same promised reward as mercy shown to one's mother. The covenant creates a continuity of compassion across the human-animal boundary. Matthew 23:37: "How often I would have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings" — Jesus uses the mother-bird image as the paradigm of his own pastoral desire for Jerusalem, invoking the Deuteronomic mercy-toward-mother-bird in his own mission's framework.
22.8: "When you build a new house, you shall make a parapet for your roof, that you may not bring the guilt of blood upon your house, if anyone should fall from it" — the building code: a parapet (H4624, maaqeh) on flat roofs prevents accidental death. The law creates personal liability for preventable death: not just murder but negligence that results in death makes one guilty (dam, blood-guilt). The rooftop was a place of daily life in ancient Israelite homes — sleeping, drying grain, social gathering. The safety requirement reflects covenant care for life extended to architecture: your home must not be a hazard to others.
22.9: "You shall not sow your vineyard with two kinds of seed, lest the whole yield be forfeited (tiqdash, H6942), the crop that you have sown and the yield of the vineyard" — mixed seeds (kil'ayim, H3610, two kinds): the prohibition of mixing is a category-purity principle that runs through Deuteronomy 22 (mixed seeds, mixed yoking, mixed fibers). The rationale is debated: (1) creation-order preservation — each species reproduces after its kind (Gen 1:11-12); (2) symbolic distinction between Israel (YHWH's planting) and the nations (different seeds). The forfeiture consequence (tiqdash, the whole becomes holy/set apart, unusable for common use) makes the violation self-defeating: you lose the entire harvest by mixing.
22.10: "You shall not plow with an ox and a donkey together" — the mixed-yoking prohibition: two different animals yoked together creates unequal strain (the ox's heavier pull vs. the donkey's lighter trot) and is cruel to the weaker animal. But the law also participates in the same categorical-distinction logic as v9. 2 Corinthians 6:14: "Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers." Paul's application of the mixed-yoking image to the covenant community's relationships — partnerships, marriages, business — follows the Deuteronomic principle: different kinds functioning under the same yoke create distortion, cruelty, and inefficiency for both.
22.11: "You shall not wear cloth of wool and linen mixed together" — sha'atnez (H8162, the mixed fabric): another kil'ayim prohibition. Wool (animal-derived) and linen (plant-derived) must not be woven together. The law is one of the chuqqim (statutes without explicitly stated reason) that Deuteronomy preserves from Sinai. The paradox is that the high priest's garments and the tabernacle curtains did use wool and linen together — marking those sacred items as uniquely set apart from the common domain. The prohibition for ordinary Israelites may thus distinguish common life from sacred space.
22.12: "You shall make yourself tassels (gedilim, H1434) on the four corners of the garment with which you cover yourself" — the tassel command: gedilim (braided/twisted tassels) are the Deuteronomic version of the Numbers 15:38-40 tzitzit with the blue cord (petil tekhelet). The tassels are embodied memory devices: "you shall see it and remember all the commandments" (Num 15:39). Matthew 9:20: "And behold, a woman who had suffered from a discharge of blood for twelve years came up behind him and touched the fringe (kraspedon, LXX translates tzitzit with this word) of his garment." The woman's touch of Jesus's tzitzit-tassel is a touch of the covenant sign: she reaches out to the one who embodies the law's fulfillment, and healing flows from that touch. Mark 6:56: crowds begged to touch "the fringe of his garment" — the tassel-touch as faith-gesture throughout Jesus's ministry.
22.13: "If any man takes a wife and goes in to her and then hates her and accuses her of misconduct and brings a bad name upon her" — the beginning of the marriage-dispute legislation (vv13-21): the law creates a judicial process for accusations of non-virginity at marriage. The husband's accusation must be adjudicated by the town elders with specific evidence required. The law protects women from defamation: a false accusation carries penalty (v18-19). The "bad name" (shem ra) is a legal concept — damaging someone's reputation requires judicial remedy, not merely social consequence.
22.14: "I did not find this woman to be a virgin" — the specific accusation. The evidence in question is the "evidence of virginity" (betulim, H1331): in the ancient context, the bloodstained cloth from the wedding night preserved as communal proof. The system is deeply embedded in ancient honor-culture practices. The law's purpose is to prevent the arbitrary dismissal or defamation of wives by taking the accusation out of private resolution into judicial process — a protection for women in a context where false accusation could be fatal.
22.15: The father and mother shall take the evidence of virginity and bring it to the town elders at the gate — the gate (sha'ar) as judicial space: the city gate is consistently the location of legal proceedings in Israel (Ruth 4:1-12; Amos 5:12,15; Prov 31:23). The evidence is presented to the public authority, not decided privately. The parents' role as advocates for their daughter — bringing the evidence before the elders — is a procedural protection: the woman has legal representation through her family.
22.16: "I gave my daughter to this man to marry, and he hates her" — the father's advocacy statement. Sane (H8130, hate) in Deuteronomy's marriage laws often functions as a quasi-legal term for the desire to dissolve a marriage (Deut 24:3: "if the latter man hates her"). The husband's emotion is a legal category that triggers specific legal consequences: dislike alone does not justify defamation.
22.17: "And now he has accused her of misconduct, saying, 'I did not find your daughter a virgin.' Yet here is the evidence of my daughter's virginity" — the presentation of evidence. The father's formal challenge: the accusation is legally disputed with physical evidence. The law establishes that accusation must meet counter-evidence; the elder-court adjudicates between the two. The procedural protection prevents naked accusation from carrying automatic legal weight.
22.18: Then the elders of that town shall take the man and whip him — the penalty for false accusation: the man who defamed his wife is punished corporally. The whipping and the financial penalty (v19: a hundred shekels of silver to the father) are substantial sanctions. The law's logic is symmetrical to the penalty it would impose if the accusation were true (v21: the woman would die): false accusation of a capital offense receives serious punishment, though not matching in kind.
22.19: "And they shall fine him a hundred shekels of silver and give them to the father of the young woman, because he has brought a bad name upon a virgin of Israel. And she shall be his wife. He may not divorce her all his days" — the compounded consequence: financial penalty + permanent loss of the right to divorce. The man who weaponized the divorce threat through defamation loses the divorce option entirely. The law creates a permanent bond as both protection for the woman and punishment for the man — an unusual covenant innovation that makes the consequence of the sin the structure of the remedy.
22.20: But if the thing is true — evidence of virginity not found — then the woman shall be brought to the door of her father's house and stoned to death — the capital consequence: the woman who entered marriage having violated her betrothal is guilty of the same offense as adultery (because betrothal was legally binding in Israel — cf. Matt 1:18-19: Joseph's discovery before their marriage required a "divorce" certificate to end the betrothal). The severity reflects the covenant's understanding of sexual faithfulness as a covenant matter, not merely a personal choice.
22.21: "She has done an outrageous thing (nevalah, H5039) in Israel by whoring in her father's house" — nevalah (H5039, outrage/disgrace): a term for acts that violate the social-ethical fabric of the covenant community in a deeply destructive way. The same term is used for Dinah's rape (Gen 34:7), the Gibeah gang-rape (Judg 19:23), Amnon's rape of Tamar (2 Sam 13:12). The father's house is the domain of covenantal oversight: the violation happened under the father's responsibility. "So you shall purge the evil from your midst" (uvi'arta hara miqirbecha) — the standard Deuteronomic formula for removing covenant-corrupting sin from the community.
22.22: "If a man is found lying with the wife of another man, both of them shall die, the man who lay with the woman, and the woman" — the adultery law: both parties receive equal penalty. The symmetrical punishment contrasts with ancient Near Eastern laws that typically punished only the woman. Deuteronomy's equal application prevents the law from being used as a tool of gender-selective punishment. Jesus in John 8:1-11 (the woman caught in adultery): the Pharisees bring only the woman, violating the Deuteronomic symmetry — the absent man is itself a problem with their application. Jesus's response ("let the one without sin cast the first stone") is consistent with the law's requirement for dual-guilt prosecution.
22.23: "If there is a betrothed virgin, and a man meets her in the city and lies with her" — the rape-or-consensual-sex-in-city law: the legal presumption when the encounter occurs in the city is that the woman could have called for help and did not. The presumption of urban accessibility to help creates the legal inference of consent when no cry was raised. The law is making a probabilistic legal inference, not a moral judgment about all possible scenarios — it establishes the burden of evidence for determining consent in the pre-forensic legal context.
22.24: Both shall be brought to the gate of the city and stoned to death — in the city scenario: both guilty, both sentenced. "The young woman because she did not cry for help though she was in the city, and the man because he violated his neighbor's wife" — the parallel guilt structure.
