Both brothers died in elevated places outside the promised land, their deaths marking the end of the wilderness generation's leadership.
32.51: ʿal ʾăšer mĕʿaltem bî bĕtôkh bĕnê yisrāʾel — 'Because you broke faith with me among the sons of Israel.' The word māʿal (H4603 — breach of faith, covenant treachery, the same word for priests embezzling sacred things) applied to Moses is severe. Moses's sin at Meribah was not simply anger but māʿal — a covenant violation that was communicable, leading Israel to fail to trust YHWH's holiness. The connection between Moses's failure and Israel's subsequent failures suggests that leadership sin had communal consequences. Moses died not for his own sake alone but as the bearer of Israel's corporate failure at Meribah.
32.52: kî minneged tirʾeh ʾet-hāʾārets weʾl-šāmmāh lōʾ tāḇôʾ — 'For you shall see the land only from a distance; you shall not enter it.' The vision without entry — minneged tirʾeh (you will see from opposite/afar — H5048 minneghed) but lōʾ tāḇôʾ šāmmāh (you will not enter there) — was the final gracious gift within the judgment: Moses would see the fulfillment of the promise even as the punishment stood. The divine refusal was not cruelty but consistency: the God who judged Israel for failing to trust His holiness could not exempt His own servant from the same covenant standard. The land ʾăšer-ʾănî nōtēn liḇnê yisrāʾel (which I am giving to the sons of Israel) — the promise was kept, just not through Moses.

33.26: ein kaEl Yeshurun rokev shamayim be'ezrecha uvegeavo shechaqim (ʾên kāʾēl yĕšurûn rōkēb šāmayim bĕʿezrekkā ûbĕgaʾăwātô šĕḥāqîm): 'There is none like God, O Jeshurun, who rides through the heavens to your help, through the skies in his majesty.' The incomparability formula (ein kaEl, there is none like God) applied to YHWH as the celestial rider — rokev shamayim, the one who rides the heavens. Psalm 68:33-34 develops the same image: Sing to God who rides in the ancient heavens; praise his strength. Ephesians 4:8 cites Psalm 68:18 (the rider who ascends and distributes gifts) in a Christological reading. The celestial rider of Deut 33:26 and Psalm 68 becomes the ascending and gift-giving Christ of Ephesians 4, and finally the rider on the white horse of Revelation 19:11-16 (Faithful and True, whose name is the Word of God).
33.27: me'onah Elohei qedem umitachat zero'ot olam vayegaresh mipaneicha oyev vayomer hashmeid (mĕʿōnāh ʾĕlōhê qedem ûmittaḥat zĕrōʿōt ʿôlām waygāreš mippānêkā ʾôyēb wayyōʾmer hašmēd): 'The eternal God is your dwelling place, and underneath are the everlasting arms. And he thrust out the enemy before you and said, Destroy.' Elohei qedem (God of old/ancient God) — YHWH as the primal, eternal divine being who preceded all creation. Zerovot olam (everlasting/eternal arms): the arms of YHWH as the foundation beneath Israel, both the shelter that surrounds and the support that sustains. The image is of a parent whose arms are always under the child who might fall. The NT equivalent is John 10:28-29 (no one can snatch them out of my hand... no one can snatch them out of the Father's hand) — the same absolute security mediated by divine power rather than creature strength.
33.1: wĕzōʾt habbĕrākhāh ʾăšer bērakh mōšeh ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm — 'This is the blessing with which Moses the man of God blessed the sons of Israel.' The superscription frames the passage as the final act of Moses's prophetic-priestly role: ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm (man of God — H376, H430 — a prophetic title for one who has direct access to YHWH, used of Elijah, Elisha, Samuel) is the highest honorific in Israel's prophetic tradition. The blessing (bĕrākhāh — H1293) was the patriarchal prerogative Moses carried as Israel's leader. Like Jacob's blessing of the twelve sons (Gen 49), Moses's blessing reviewed each tribe's character and destiny — but from the perspective of YHWH's election rather than merely genealogical traits.
33.2: YHWH missînay bāʾ wĕzāraḥ miśśēʿîr lāmô — 'YHWH came from Sinai and dawned from Seir upon them.' The theophanic hymn that opens Moses's blessing is one of the oldest Hebrew poems: YHWH as the divine warrior marching to Israel's aid from Sinai (sînay), Seir (śēʿîr), and Mount Paran (har pārān). The three directional references correspond to the Sinai peninsula region. The word zāraḥ (H2224 — to rise, shine like the sun) applies the solar imagery of divine theophany. ʾātāh mirĕḇĕḇōt qōdeš (he came from the holy myriads — from Sinai's ten-thousands of holy ones) identifies the angelic/covenant retinue accompanying YHWH. The parallel in Judg 5:4-5 (Deborah's Song) and Hab 3:3-4 shows this was a standard Israelite theophanic tradition.
33.3: ʾaf ḥōḇēḇ ʿammîm kol-qĕdōšāyw bĕyādekā — 'Indeed, he loves his peoples; all his holy ones are in your hand.' Ḥōḇēḇ (H2245 — to cherish, love warmly) — a rare verb found only here — describes YHWH's affectionate love for Israel. The phrase kol-qĕdōšāyw bĕyādekā (all his holy ones are in your hand) places all who belong to YHWH under His sovereign protection. The subsequent phrase hēm tukku lĕraglikhā yiśśā middabbĕrōtêkhā — 'they bowed down at your feet, accepted your words' — describes Israel's covenant submission at Sinai: the people who received Torah bowed before YHWH as covenant suzerain.
33.4: tôrāh tsiwwāh-lānû mōšeh mōrāšāh qĕhillat yaʿăqōḇ — 'Moses commanded us the Torah, a possession/heritage of the congregation of Jacob.' The verse is a liturgical formula recited in Israel — mōrāšāh (H4181 — possession, inheritance, heritage; from H3423 yāraš, to inherit/possess) frames Torah not as an external imposition but as Israel's family inheritance. Qĕhillat yaʿăqōḇ (the congregation/assembly of Jacob — H6951) identifies the community as the covenantal gathering of Jacob's descendants. The Torah was the family document of YHWH's household — as much a possession as the land itself.
33.5: vayhî ḇîšurûn melek — 'He became king in Jeshurun.' The kingship of YHWH over Israel (melek — H4428) was established at the covenant assembly at Sinai: bĕhitʾassep rāʾšê ʿām yaḥad šiḇṭê yisrāʾel (when the heads of the people assembled, the tribes of Israel together). The Sinai theophany was Israel's coronation of YHWH as king — the covenant ratification was a formal acknowledgment of YHWH's sovereignty. This verse anticipates the later debates about human kingship (1 Sam 8:7: 'they have not rejected you, but they have rejected me from being king over them') — YHWH was always king; human kings were his vice-regents.
33.6: yĕhî rĕʾûḇēn weʾal-yāmōt ûyĕhî mĕṯāyw mispar — 'Let Reuben live and not die, though his men be few.' The brevity of Reuben's blessing reflects the tribe's historical trajectory: forfeiting the firstborn's double portion after the sin with Bilhah (Gen 35:22; 49:4), and the tribe's later instability and modest size. Yĕhî rĕʾûḇēn (let Reuben live) is a petition, not a declaration — Moses prayed for a tribe that was in danger of disappearing. The phrase ûyĕhî mĕṯāyw mispar (his men few in number) is either a description of their current state or a qualification of the blessing: even with few men, let them live.
33.7: wĕzōʾt lîhûdāh vayyōʾmar — 'And this for Judah; he said.' Judah's blessing is a military prayer: šĕmaʿ YHWH qôl yĕhûdāh (hear, YHWH, the voice of Judah) and weʾel-ʿammô tĕḇîʾennû (bring him to his people). The hands of Judah contend (yādāyw rāḇ-lô) and YHWH is asked to be his helper against enemies (weʿēzer missārāyw tihyeh). The blessing anticipates Judah's military leadership in Israel's history (Judg 1:1-2 — Judah goes first) and the Davidic dynasty that would emerge from the tribe. The prayer for Judah to return to his people may reflect a historical circumstance of isolation in battle.
33.8: ûlĕlēwî ʾāmar tummêkhā weʾûrîkhā lĕʾîš ḥăsîdêkhā — 'Of Levi he said: Your Thummim and Urim belong to your godly one.' The Levitical blessing centers on the priestly instruments: tummêkhā weʾûrîkhā (your Thummim and Urim — H8550, H224, the lots for divine oracle in the high priest's breastplate). Ḥăsîdêkhā (your godly/faithful one — H2623 from H2617 ḥesed) refers to Levi as YHWH's covenant-loyal servant. The historical basis: Massah (nissîtô bemassāh) and Meribah (tĕrîḇēhû ʿal-mê mĕrîḇāh) — where Levi (cf. Exod 32:26-29 — the golden calf incident) proved covenant loyalty by standing with YHWH against idolaters, even family members.
33.9: hāʾōmēr lĕʾāḇîw ûlĕʾimmô lōʾ rĕʾîtîw — 'Who said of his father and mother, “I do not see them.”' The Levitical commitment to YHWH surpassed family ties: lōʾ rĕʾîtîw (I do not see him), weʾet-ʾeḥāyw lōʾ hikhîr (did not acknowledge his brothers), weʾet-bānāyw lōʾ yādāʿ (did not know his children). This absolute loyalty — the same radical commitment Jesus demanded for discipleship (Matt 10:37; Luke 14:26) — was demonstrated at the golden calf incident when the Levites 'consecrated themselves to YHWH that day at the cost of sons and brothers' (Exod 32:29). The Levitical priestly vocation required the displacement of all other loyalties by covenant loyalty to YHWH.
33.10: yôrû mishpāṭêkhā lĕyaʿăqōḇ wetôrāthkhā lĕyisrāʾel — 'They will teach your ordinances to Jacob and your Torah to Israel.' The Levitical three-fold ministry: teaching (yōrû — H3384, to teach, direct; the root of tôrāh), offering incense (yāśîmû qĕṭôrāh bĕʾappêkhā), and burnt offerings (wekhālîl ʿal-mizbeḥekhā). The teaching ministry of the Levites was the mechanism of covenant transmission — they were the living library of Israel's law (2 Chr 17:7-9; Neh 8:7-9; Mal 2:6-7). The prophetic complaint against priests who failed to teach the Torah (Hos 4:6; Mal 2:8) shows how crucial this function was to Israel's covenant continuity.
33.11: bārēkh YHWH ḥêlô ûfoʿal yādāyw tirtsāh — 'Bless YHWH his wealth and accept the work of his hands.' The prayer for Levitical blessing encompasses both resources (ḥayil — wealth, capacity, substance) and labor (poʿal yādāyw — the work of his hands, the priestly service). The imprecation against enemies: mehātsats mĕqāmāyw ûmeśōnĕʾāyw min-yĕqûmûn (shatter the loins of his attackers and those who hate him, so they do not rise) echoes the protection of the priestly office itself. Those who opposed Levi's ministry — as Korah (Num 16), Saul (1 Sam 13), Uzziah (2 Chr 26) — would find their opposition divinely crushed.
33.12: lĕḇinyāmîn ʾāmar yĕdîd YHWH yiškōn lāḇeṭaḥ ʿālāyw — 'Of Benjamin he said: The beloved of YHWH dwells in safety beside him.' Yĕdîd YHWH (beloved of YHWH — H3039 from H1730, love) gives Benjamin YHWH's personal affection — the tribe that would carry the temple in Jerusalem on its territory. Yiškōn lāḇeṭaḥ ʿālāyw (he dwells in security beside him) and ḥōpēp ʿālāyw kol-hayyôm (he covers/shields him all the day long) and ûḇên kĕtēpāyw šākhēn (he dwells between his shoulders/flanks) — the last phrase locates YHWH's dwelling between Benjamin's mountain ridges, anticipating the Jerusalem temple which sat on the Benjamin-Judah border.
33.13: ûlĕyôsēp ʾāmar mĕḇōreket YHWH ʾartsô — 'Of Joseph he said: Blessed by YHWH is his land.' Joseph's blessing is the most lavishly detailed, covering geography (heavens, earth, depths), time (perennial fruit, every month), and cosmic abundance. Mimmeghed šāmayim miṭṭāl (from the bounty of heaven's dew), ûmittĕhôm rōḇetset tāḥat (from the deep lying beneath) — the blessing comes from above (rain/dew) and below (springs/groundwater). The fertile Manasseh and Ephraim territories in the central highlands (modern West Bank and Jezreel Valley) were indeed among the most productive agricultural regions in Canaan.
33.14: ûmimmeghed tĕḇûʾōt šāmeš — 'From the bounty of the produce of the sun.' The solar blessing (tĕḇûʾōt šāmeš — sun-ripened harvest) and lunar blessing (meghed garēš yĕrāḥîm — bounty yielded by the moon) refer to the agricultural rhythms governed by celestial bodies. The complete round of the agricultural year — spring rains from the heavens, summer sun ripening, autumn harvest, winter depths supplying groundwater — was YHWH's gift to Joseph's land. The verse reflects sophisticated agricultural understanding: both solar radiation for ripening and lunar cycles governing tidal/rainfall patterns contributed to the land's productivity.
33.15: ûmerōʾš harĕrê-qedem ûmimmeghed giḇĕʿôt ʿôlām — 'From the finest of the ancient mountains, the bounty of the age-old hills.' The mountains (harĕrê-qedem — ancient mountains, from the beginning of time) and everlasting hills (giḇĕʿôt ʿôlām) provided the mineral richness and forest resources that supplemented the valley agriculture. The phrase echoes Gen 49:26 (Jacob's blessing of Joseph: 'bounties of the ancient mountains, the age-old hills'). The geologically ancient landscape of Canaan — limestone ridges, fertile valleys, springs — was Joseph's inheritance, and its abundance was traced to YHWH's creation of the land from primordial times.
33.16: ûmimmeghed ʾerets ûmĕlôʾāh ûrĕtsôn šōkhĕnî sĕneh — 'From the bounty of the earth and its fullness, and from the favor of the one who dwelt in the bush.' The reference to šōkhĕnî sĕneh (the dweller in the bush — H5572, the burning bush of Exod 3) is extraordinary: YHWH appeared to Moses in the thorn-bush of the desert, and this theophany on behalf of Joseph's tribes is now invoked for their blessing. The connection: YHWH met Moses in the wilderness to announce Israel's redemption from Egypt — that same divine favor (rĕtsôn — H7522, favor, goodwill, acceptable pleasure) would rest on Joseph's territory. The theophany-blessing connection honored both the means of Exodus revelation and its beneficiaries.
33.17: bekôr šôrô hādār lô weqarnê rĕʾēm qarnāyw — 'A firstborn bull — majesty is his! His horns are the horns of a wild ox.' The bull-imagery for Ephraim (šôr — H7794, bull/ox, symbol of power and agricultural strength) was YHWH's chosen imagery for Joseph's supremacy. Rĕʾēm (H7214 — wild ox, the aurochs, the most formidable bovine) with its qarnāyw (horns — H7161) would gore (yenaggaḥ) peoples together. The military application: ʾappê-ʾereṣ yaḥdāw (pushing the peoples to the ends of the earth). The ten thousands of Ephraim (rĕḇāḇôt ʾeprayim) and the thousands of Manasseh (ʾalpê manašše) were the numerical legacy of Joseph's blessing — the largest tribal census counts in Num 1 and 26.
33.18: ûlizĕḇûlun ʾāmar śĕmaḥ zĕḇûlun bĕṣēʾtekhā — 'Of Zebulun he said: Rejoice, Zebulun, in your going out.' The twin blessing of Zebulun and Issachar (Gen 49:13-14) reflects their geographic and economic partnership: Zebulun's bĕṣēʾtekhā (going out — maritime/trade ventures) and Issachar's bĕʾohālêkhā (in your tents — agricultural stability). The pairing celebrates the complementarity of trade and farming: Zebulun's sea commerce (yirʾû — they will suck the wealth of the sea) and Issachar's land productivity. The reference to safûn yammîm (the hidden treasures of the sand) likely refers to glassmaking sand from the Carmel coast, an important trade commodity in antiquity.
33.19: ʿammîm har-yiqrāʾû šāmmāh yizbeḥû ziḇḥê-tsedeq — 'They will summon peoples to the mountain; there they will offer right sacrifices.' The mountain (har) where peoples are summoned to worship anticipates Zion theology (Isa 2:3; Mic 4:2 — 'let us go up to the mountain of YHWH'). Ziḇḥê-tsedeq (sacrifices of righteousness — H6664) are the properly offered, covenantally correct sacrifices (cf. Ps 4:5; 51:19). The Zebulun-Issachar region sucked the wealth of the seas (šĕfaq yammîm yinyaqû) and the hidden treasures of the sand (ûśĕfûnê tĕmûnê ḥôl) — indicating a prosperous region that would support proper worship.
33.20: ûlĕgād ʾāmar bārûkh marḥîḇ gād — 'Of Gad he said: Blessed is he who enlarges Gad.' The blessing for Gad emphasizes territorial expansion (marḥîḇ — H7337, to widen, enlarge) and military prowess: Gad dwells like a lion (kĕlāḇîʾ šākhēn), tearing arm and crown of the head (veyiṭrap zĕrôaʿ ʾap-qodqōd). The verb ṭārap (H2963 — to tear, rend — the lion's attack) portrays Gad as a fierce warrior tribe. The first portion (rēʾšît lô) was taken for Gad — possibly referring to their early conquest east of the Jordan (Num 32) or the firstfruits of the spoil. Gad's territory in Gilead was frontier land requiring both military strength and territorial aggression.
33.21: vayyarʾ rēʾšît lô kî šām ḥelqat mĕḥōqēq sāfûn — 'He chose the best land for himself, for a commander's portion was reserved for him.' Mĕḥōqēq (H2710 — lawgiver, commander, the one who enacts decrees) in Gad's territory may allude to Moses's burial there (Deut 34:6 — on the east side of the Jordan) or to a leader born from Gad. The verse ends with praise: wayyēṯē rāʾšê ʿām tsidqat YHWH weʾet-mišpāṭāyw ʿim-yisrāʾel (he came with the heads of the people, executed YHWH's righteousness and his ordinances with Israel). Gad's loyalty to the trans-Jordan covenant commitment (Josh 22) validated this blessing.
33.22: ûlĕdān ʾāmar dān gûr ʾaryēh yĕzanneq min-habbāšān — 'Of Dan he said: Dan is a lion's cub that leaps from Bashan.' The Dan blessing uses the lion-cub imagery (gûr ʾaryēh — H1482 gûr, young animal; H738 ʾaryēh, lion) that Gen 49:9 applied to Judah. Dan's migration from their original southern allotment to Bashan in the far north (Judg 17-18) was apparently anticipated here — the tribe that leapt northward to find new territory. Yĕzanneq (H2187 — to leap, spring — a hapax legomenon) suggests sudden movement. Dan's military agility and territorial flexibility made them a distinct tribe, though their ultimate apostasy (setting up idols at Laish/Dan) was the shadow over their blessing.
33.23: ûlĕnaptālî ʾāmar naptālî śĕbaʿ rātsôn ûmālēʾ birkhat YHWH — 'Of Naphtali he said: Naphtali, satisfied with favor, full of the blessing of YHWH.' The three-fold abundance: śāḇaʿ rātsôn (satisfied with favor — H7646, H7522), mālēʾ birkhat YHWH (full of YHWH's blessing — H4390), and possession of yam wedarôm yrāšāh (the sea and the south take possession). The 'sea' likely refers to the Sea of Galilee (Kinneret) on Naphtali's western border, and the 'south' to its territory extending southward. Naphtali's territory included some of the most fertile land in the Galilee region — the Kinnereth basin, source of abundant fish and agricultural produce.
33.24: ûlĕʾāšēr ʾāmar bārûkh mibbānîm ʾāšēr — 'Of Asher he said: Most blessed of sons is Asher.' Asher's blessing is triple: most blessed among sons (bārûkh mibbānîm), favored by brothers (yĕhî rĕtsûy ʾeḥāyw), and destined to dip his foot in oil (weṭōḇēl bašemen raglô). The olive oil (šemen) for which the Asher coastal territory was famous (the modern Carmel coast and western Galilee) is legendary — still today the finest olives in the region grow along this coast. The combination of material prosperity (barezel ûnĕḥōšet — iron and bronze sandals for strength, v25) and covenant security made Asher's tribal portion one of the most desirable.
33.25: barzel ûnĕḥōšet mĕnaʿălêkhā ûkeḏāḇrekhā yaḏmôtekhā — 'Your bars shall be iron and bronze; as your days, so your strength.' The promise to Asher has two parts: barzel ûnĕḥōšet mĕnaʿălêkhā (iron and bronze are your shoes/bolts — either sandals or door-bolts, symbolizing impregnable defense) and keḏāḇrekhā yaḏmôtekhā (as your days, so your strength — literally 'as your day, so your rest/security'). The formula keḏāḇrekhā (as your day) means: whatever each day demands, YHWH provides the corresponding strength. This promise was later universalized by Paul (Phil 4:19: 'my God will supply every need of yours according to his riches in glory') and is the model for covenant sufficiency.
33.28: wayyiškōn yisrāʾēl beṭaḥ bādād ʿên yaʿăqōḇ — 'Israel lived in safety, Jacob in security alone.' The closing vision of Israel's blessed state: beṭaḥ bādād (in security, alone — H983, H910) — the security of those who need no international alliances, dwelling safely because YHWH alone is their protector. The phrase ʾên yaʿăqōḇ (the spring/eye of Jacob) is ambiguous but likely refers to YHWH as the living spring sustaining Jacob. The agricultural bounty: ʾel ʾereṣ dāgān wĕṯîrôš (to a land of grain and new wine), ʾap-šāmāyw yaʿărpû ṭāl (whose heavens drip with dew). The land is the material expression of YHWH's covenant faithfulness to the patriarchal promises.
33.29: ʾašrêkhā yisrāʾēl mî khāmôkhā — 'Blessed are you, O Israel! Who is like you?' The macarism (ʾašrêkhā — H835, blessed, happy — the plural of fullness, comprehensive wellbeing) closes the entire blessing collection. The rhetorical question mî khāmôkhā (who is like you?) echoes Exod 15:11 (mî khāmōkhā bāʾēlim YHWH — who is like you among the gods, O YHWH) — Israel's uniqueness mirrors YHWH's uniqueness: both are incomparable in their respective spheres. The reason: ʿam nôšaʿ baYHWH (a people saved by YHWH) and māgēn ʿezrekā (shield of your help) and ʾăšer-ḥereb gaʾwātekā (the sword of your majesty). Israel's greatness was entirely derivative — it was great because its God was great, saved because its Savior was YHWH.

34.6: vayiqbor oto bagei be'eretz Moav mul Beit-Peor velo-yada ish et-qevuroto ad hayom hazeh (wayyiqbōr ʾōtô baggay bĕʾereṣ mōʾāb mul bêt pĕʿôr wĕlōʾ-yādaʿ ʾîš ʾet-qĕbûrātô ʿad hayyôm hazzeh): 'And he buried him in the valley in the land of Moab opposite Beth-peor; but no one knows the place of his burial to this day.' The subject of the verb vayiqbor (and he buried) is YHWH — Moses is buried by God himself. The unusual hiddenness of Moses's tomb — unknown to his own people — prevented it from becoming a shrine or a site of veneration. Jude 9 mentions that Michael the archangel contended with the devil over Moses's body — suggesting a heavenly concern about where Moses was laid, and why. The contrast with Christ is total: Moses's burial place is unknown and unremembered; Christ's tomb is the most precisely located and historically significant burial site in human history — precisely because it was empty.
34.10: velo-qam navi od beYisrael keMoshe asher yeda'o YHWH panim el-panim (wĕlōʾ-qām nābîʾ ʿôd bĕyiśrāʾēl kĕmōšeh ʾăšer yĕdāʿô Yhwh pānîm ʾel-pānîm): 'And there has not arisen a prophet since in Israel like Moses, whom the LORD knew face to face.' Deuteronomy closes with a declaration of Moses's prophetic incomparability — and simultaneously with the expectation it creates: the prophet-like-Moses is still to come (Deut 18:15). The closing words of the Torah are simultaneously a tribute to its author-figure and a gap in the narrative: the greatest prophet has died without Israel entering the fullest covenant relationship with YHWH. The NT fulfills this gap: Hebrews 3:3-6 places Jesus above Moses as the Son over the house that Moses served in; John 1:17 contrasts the law given through Moses with grace and truth come through Jesus Christ; and Matthew 17:5 (listen to him — the voice at the Transfiguration) echoes Deut 18:15 (to him you shall listen) and confirms that Jesus is the prophet-like-Moses whom Israel had awaited since the close of Torah.
34.1: vayyaʿal mōšeh mēʿarḇōt môʾāḇ ʾel-har nĕḇô — 'Moses went up from the plains of Moab to Mount Nebo.' The final ascent: ʿarḇôt môʾāḇ (plains of Moab — the trans-Jordanian plateau where Israel had camped), har nĕḇô (Mount Nebo — the peak of the Abarim range). The peak (rōʾš happisgāh — the summit of Pisgah — H6449, a ridge of the Abarim range) is specified, confirming the view-angle toward the west. The land Moses saw: gilʿād ʿad-dān (Gilead to Dan — the northern Jordan River region), comprising the entire visible extent of the promised land. The Chronicler of Moses's death presents it as a farewell vision — the final gift of seeing what he could not enter.
34.2: weʾēt kol-naptālî weʾet-ʾerets ʾeprayim ûmanašše — 'And all of Naphtali, the land of Ephraim and Manasseh.' The panoramic list continues: Naphtali's territory in upper Galilee, Ephraim and Manasseh's central hill country, Judah's territory to the western sea (ʾel-hayyām hāʾaḥărôn — to the Mediterranean), the Negev (hannegev), and the Plain of Jericho (kikkar biqʿat yĕrîḥô) to Zoar. The completeness of the vision — from north to south, from east to west — confirms that Moses saw the entire promised inheritance. The vision was not partial but comprehensive: YHWH gave Moses the full inheritance in sight even when denying him the full inheritance in body.
34.3: weʾet-hannegev weʾet-hakkikkār gê yĕrîḥô — 'The Negev and the Plain, the valley of Jericho, the city of palms, as far as Zoar.' The southern extent: hannegev (the Negev wilderness), kikkār gê yĕrîḥô (the plain/circle of the valley of Jericho — the Jordan valley floor, one of the lowest points on earth), ʿîr hattĕmārîm (city of palms = Jericho, famous for its date palm groves), and ṣōʿar (Zoar — the small city spared in Lot's escape from Sodom, Gen 19:22, at the southern end of the Dead Sea). The complete circuit — from Dan in the north to Zoar in the south — gave Moses a comprehensive survey of the covenant inheritance.
34.4: vayyōʾmer YHWH ʾēlāyw zōʾt hāʾārets ʾăšer nišbaʿtî lĕʾaḇrāhām lĕyitsḥāq ûlĕyaʿăqōḇ — 'YHWH said to him: This is the land I swore to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.' The divine speech at the moment of death is YHWH's fulfillment declaration: the land Moses saw was the land of the Abrahamic oath (Gen 12:7; 15:18-21; 17:8; 26:3; 28:13-15). Nišbaʿtî lĕʾaḇrāhām (I swore to Abraham — the Patriarchal covenant) was the legal foundation of Israel's claim to Canaan. The inclusion of all three patriarchal names confirms the covenant's intergenerational continuity: the oath to Abraham was renewed with Isaac and Jacob, creating an unbroken chain of divine commitment. Moses's death did not invalidate the oath — Joshua would carry out what Moses viewed.
34.5: vayyāmot šāmmāh mōšeh ʿeḇed-YHWH bĕʾerets môʾāḇ — 'Moses died there, the servant of YHWH, in the land of Moab.' The death of Moses is recorded with the title ʿeḇed YHWH (servant of YHWH — H5650, H3068) — the highest possible title in Israel's prophetic tradition. ʿEḇed YHWH would become the name of the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 40-55, the one who would perfectly fulfill what Moses foreshadowed. Moses died ʿal-pî YHWH (by the mouth/command of YHWH — literally 'by the mouth of YHWH'; a rabbinic tradition read this as 'by a divine kiss'). The location — Moab, east of the Jordan — meant Moses died having completed his mission but not entered his reward: the model of the servant who gives everything without personal benefit.
34.7: ûmōšeh ben-meʾāh weʿeśrîm šānāh bĕmōtô — 'Moses was 120 years old when he died.' The triple structure of Moses's life was recognized in ancient Jewish tradition: 40 years in Egypt, 40 years in Midian, 40 years in the wilderness (Acts 7:23,30,36). The triple perfection (3 × 40) of Moses's 120-year lifespan made his biography a symbol of completeness. Lōʾ-khāhătāh ʿênô welōʾ-nās lēḥōh (his eye was not dim nor had his vigor diminished) — a physiological statement that Moses's death was not from natural decline but from divine appointment. His capacities were fully intact at death — making his inability to enter the land a matter of divine decision, not incapacity.
34.8: vayyiḇkû ḇnê yisrāʾēl ʾet-mōšeh bĕʿarḇōt môʾāḇ šlōšîm yôm — 'The sons of Israel wept for Moses in the plains of Moab for thirty days.' The mourning period: šelōšîm yôm (thirty days) was the standard royal and prophetic mourning period (cf. Aaron's thirty days in Num 20:29; Saul in 1 Sam 31:13 with seven days). The phrase vayyitmĕû yĕmê vekhi ʾēbel mōšeh (the days of weeping and mourning for Moses were completed) closes the era with formal grief. Moses was mourned as Israel's founder-prophet — the one through whom the nation had been constituted at Sinai. The thirty-day mourning equated Moses with Aaron, honoring both pillars of the wilderness leadership equally in death.
34.9: wîhôšuaʿ bin-nûn māleʾ rûaḥ ḥokhmāh — 'Joshua son of Nun was full of the spirit of wisdom.' The transition: rûaḥ ḥokhmāh (spirit of wisdom — H7307, H2451) was imparted to Joshua when Moses laid hands on him (kî-sāmakh mōšeh ʾet-yādāyw ʿālāyw — because Moses had laid his hands on him). The spirit of wisdom for governance was distinct from the prophetic spirit — it equipped Joshua for the specific task of conquest administration and tribal distribution. Vayyišmĕʿû ʾēlāyw bĕnê yisrāʾēl (the sons of Israel obeyed him) — the leadership transfer was complete and accepted. YHWH's command to obey Joshua (Josh 1:16-18) was already anticipated in Israel's voluntary submission.
34.11: lĕkhol-hāʾōtôt wĕhammôpĕṯîm ʾăšer šĕlaḥô YHWH laʿăśôt bĕʾerets mitsrāyim — 'For all the signs and wonders YHWH sent him to do in Egypt.' The summary of Moses's unique qualification: the ʾōtôt (signs — H226) and môpĕṯîm (wonders — H4159) in Egypt against Pharaoh (lĕpharʿōh ûlĕkhol-ʿăḇādāyw ûlĕkhol-ʾartsô). The ten plagues, the parting of the Reed Sea, the provision of manna and water — these constituted Moses's prophetic credential. No prophet before or after equaled these signs in scope or directness (lekhol yad hăḥăzāqāh ûlĕkhol hammôrāʾ hāggādōl — all the mighty hand and great terror, v34:12). The conclusion establishes Moses as the unequaled prophet of the Old Covenant, awaiting his antitype.
34.12: ûlĕkhol hayyād hahăzāqāh ûlĕkhol hammôrāʾ hāggādôl — 'And for all the mighty hand and all the great terror that Moses did in the sight of all Israel.' The closing words of the Pentateuch: the mighty hand (yaḏ hahăzāqāh — H3027, H2389) is the formula used for the Exodus deliverance throughout Deuteronomy (4:34; 5:15; 6:21; 7:8; 11:2; 26:8). Moses performed these wonders lĕʿênê kol-yisrāʾēl (in the sight of all Israel) — a public, witnessed, verifiable revelation. The Pentateuch ends where it must: at Moses's death, with the covenant in place, the land in sight, and the prophetic office awaiting its fulfillment. The Mosaic era closes expecting its successor — both the immediate Joshua and the ultimate One of whom Moses said 'YHWH your God will raise up a prophet like me' (Deut 18:15).

31.1: The opening וַיֵּלֶךְ מֹשֶׁה (wayyēlek mōsheh) — "and Moses went" — is slightly unusual; some ancient versions read "and Moses finished speaking." The MT as it stands emphasizes physical movement — Moses departs one context (the parenetic discourse) to enter another (the formal transition). Chapter 31 is the climax of the Mosaic career: the writing, transmission, and cosmic witnessing of the Torah.
31.2: בֶּן מֵאָה וְעֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה אָנֹכִי (ben meʾāh wĕʿeśrîm shānāh ʾānōkî) — "I am one hundred and twenty years old." One hundred and twenty is a round number in Israel's numerical symbolism; it also happens to divide into three periods of 40 (Egypt, wilderness before Horeb, wilderness after Horeb). Lōʾ ʾûkal ʿôd lāṣēʾt wĕlābōʾ — "I am no longer able to go out and come in": the idiom for military leadership and active governance. The incapacity is presented first as physical/functional before the divine prohibition (Yhwh ʾāmar ʾēlay lōʾ taʿăbōr ʾet hayyardēn) is added as the definitive reason.
31.3: יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ הוּא עֹבֵר לְפָנֶיךָ (Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā hûʾ ʿōbēr lĕpānêkā) — "YHWH your God himself is crossing over before you." The emphatic pronoun hûʾ (he himself) places the stress on divine agency: the conquest is YHWH's crossing, not merely Israel's. Hûʾ yashḥît ʾet haggôyim hāʾēlleh — "he will destroy these nations before you": the divine warrior motif frames the entire conquest as YHWH's military campaign in which Israel participates. Joshua (yĕhôshûaʿ) is named as the human instrument but under YHWH's leadership.
31.4: כַּאֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה לְסִיחֹן וּלְעוֹג (kaʾăsher ʿāśāh lĕSîḥōn ûlĕʿÔg) — "as he did to Sihon and Og." The Transjordanian victories function as proof-of-concept throughout Deuteronomy: YHWH has already defeated two powerful Amorite kings; the conquest of Canaan will extend the same pattern. The argument is analogical: from prior demonstration to present expectation.
31.5: The command "do to them according to all the commandment I have commanded you" (kaḵol hammiṣwāh ʾăsher ṣiwwîtî ʾetkem) situates the military action within the covenant framework — this is not arbitrary violence but a commanded action with its own Torah-regulated form. The ḥērem of ch 7 governs the expected conduct.
31.6: חִזְקוּ וְאִמְצוּ (ḥizqû wĕʾimṣû) — "be strong and courageous." This is the covenant-commissioning formula that will define Joshua's leadership throughout the subsequent narrative (31:7, 23; Josh 1:6, 7, 9, 18). Ḥāzaq (H2388) = firm, strong — military fortitude; ʾāmaṣ (H553) = resolute, bold — the inner determination that fortitude requires. Together they form a hendiadys for unshakeable covenantal resolve. The ground for the command — lōʾ tîrĕʾû wĕlōʾ taḥpĕṣû mippĕnêhem ("do not be afraid or in dread of them") — is YHWH's presence: kî Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā hûʾ hahōlēk ʿimmāk lōʾ yarpĕkā wĕlōʾ yaʿazbekkā ("for YHWH your God himself goes with you; he will not relax his hold on you or forsake you").
31.7: Moses's words to Joshua are spoken lĕʿênê kol-yiśrāʾēl (before the eyes of all Israel) — the public commissioning is a covenantal transfer, not merely a private appointment. The double formula ḥăzaq weʾĕmāṣ is repeated; its repetition emphasizes that the whole command-structure of Israel's future passes through Joshua's resolve.
31.8: לֹא יַרְפְּךָ וְלֹא יַעַזְבֶךָּ (lōʾ yarpĕkā wĕlōʾ yaʿazbekkā) — "he will not relax his hold on you and will not forsake you." Two distinct verbs: rāpāh (H7503) means to relax, let go, slacken — the image of a hand loosening its grip; ʿāzab (H5800) means to abandon, forsake completely. Together they form a comprehensive negative: YHWH will neither loosen his hold nor walk away. Heb 13:5 quotes this verse (via LXX: ou mē se anō, oude ou mē se enkatalipō) as the ground for Christian contentment — the same covenant-assurance given to Joshua is applied to believers whose God is the same.
31.9: וַיִּכְתֹּב מֹשֶׁה (wayyiktōb mōsheh) — "and Moses wrote": the writing (kāṯab, H3789) of the Torah is a formal act in the transition narrative. The Torah becomes a written artifact — not merely an oral tradition but a text that can be entrusted to others. The recipients are the Levitical priests (hakkōhănîm bĕnê lēwî) who carry the ark (hāʾārôn bĕrît Yhwh) and "all the elders of Israel" — the dual custodians of the covenant text represent both sacred/priestly and civic/elder authority structures.
31.10: מִקֵּץ שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים (miqqēṣ shebaʿ shānîm) — "at the end of seven years": the septennial reading cycle aligns with the Sabbatical Year (shĕmiṭṭāh, ch 15), which itself rests on the seven-day Sabbath pattern. The recurrence guarantees that no generation of Israelites will grow up without public Torah instruction. Bĕmôʿēd shĕnat hashshĕmiṭṭāh bĕḥag hasukkôt — at the Feast of Tabernacles — the covenant renewal ceremony is embedded in the liturgical calendar's harvest celebration.
31.11: בְּבוֹא כָל יִשְׂרָאֵל (bĕbôʾ kol yiśrāʾēl) — "when all Israel comes." The verb bôʾ here carries the technical sense of pilgrimage-appearance before YHWH (as in Exod 23:17, where Israel "appears" before YHWH at the three festivals). The reading occurs lĕʾoznêhem ("in their ears") — not silently but as public oral proclamation. Torah is constituted by being heard communally, not merely possessed textually.
31.12: הַקְהֵל אֶת הָעָם (haqhēl ʾet hāʿām) — "assemble the people": the Hiphil imperative of qāhal (H6950) — to convene the assembly (qāhāl). The list that follows — men, women, children (haṭṭap, the little ones), and the sojourner (gēr) — is the comprehensive covenant community. No social category is excluded from the hearing. The purpose: lĕmaʿan yishĕmĕʿû ûlĕmaʿan yilmĕdû wĕyārĕʾû — "so that they may hear, learn, and fear." The sequence is instructional: hearing precedes learning; learning produces the fear-of-YHWH that is the goal of Torah.
31.13: וּבְנֵיהֶם אֲשֶׁר לֹא יָדְעוּ (ûbĕnêhem ʾăsher lōʾ yādĕʿû) — "and their children who have not known": the explicit concern for the generation that has not yet encountered the covenant text establishes intergenerational education as a covenantal obligation. The child who "does not know" (lōʾ yādaʿ) is not ignorant of all things but specifically lacks the formational encounter with Torah that constitutes covenant identity. The septennial reading ensures each new generation receives the foundational text.
31.14: קָרַב יְמֵי מֹשֶׁה לָמוּת (qārab yĕmê mōsheh lāmût) — "the days of Moses have drawn near to die." The same verb qārab (H7126, to draw near, approach) used in ceremonial contexts of approaching the sanctuary. Moses's death approaches in the language of a sacred threshold. Môʿēd (H4150, tent of meeting) — YHWH's summons to the tent marks the transition as divinely orchestrated: this is not merely biological death but covenantal completion.
31.15: בְּעַמּוּד עָנָן (bĕʿammûd ʿānān) — "in a pillar of cloud": the theophanic sign that marked YHWH's guidance through the wilderness (Exod 13:21-22; 14:19; Num 14:14). The cloud's standing over the door of the tent (ʿal petaḥ hāʾōhel) is a formal divine arrival — YHWH's presence in a visible but veiled form. The occasion is the formal commissioning of Joshua, requiring direct divine presence as the authorizing act.
31.16: YHWH's announcement of future apostasy — זֹנֶה אַחֲרֵי אֱלֹהֵי נֵכַר הָאָרֶץ (zōneh ʾaḥărê ʾĕlōhê nēkar hāʾāreṣ) — uses the verb zānāh (H2181, to prostitute, commit adultery/harlotry) for apostasy, the same metaphor the prophets will develop at length (Hosea, Jeremiah, Ezekiel). The people's covenant relationship with YHWH is marital; going after foreign gods is an act of marital betrayal. The divine foreknowledge is not fatalistic predetermination but prophetic realism about the heart's inclination without divine transformation (see 30:6).
31.17: חָרָה אַפִּי (ḥārāh ʾappî) — "my anger will burn": the idiom of divine anger as heat — ḥārāh (H2734) from the root of burning/glowing. The consequence is הִסְתַּרְתִּי פָנַי מֵהֶם (histartî pānay mēhem) — "I will hide my face from them": the concealment of the divine face is the most severe form of divine absence — not the active curse but the withdrawal of the sustaining presence that makes life possible. Wĕhāyāh lĕʾekōl ("and will become food for devouring") — when the divine presence is withdrawn, the covenant protections go with it.
31.18: וְאָנֹכִי הַסְתֵּר אַסְתִּיר פָּנַי (wĕʾānōkî hastēr ʾastîr pānay) — the infinitive absolute with finite verb: "I will surely/completely hide my face." The doubled form of sātar (H5641, to hide, conceal) intensifies beyond v17's past-tense report to a present-tense guarantee. The pronoun ʾānōkî (I myself) is emphatic. Bĕkol hārāʿāh ʾăsher ʿāśāh kî pānāh ʾel ʾĕlōhîm ʾăḥērîm — "because of all the evil they have done in turning to other gods." The covenant logic is strict: turning away from YHWH results in YHWH's own turning away.
31.19: כִּתְבוּ לָכֶם אֶת הַשִּׁירָה הַזֹּאת (kitbû lākem ʾet hashshîrāh hazzōʾt) — "write for yourselves this song." The Song of Moses (ch 32) is commanded as a written text — its being-written is itself a covenantal act. The purpose: lĕmaʿan tihyeh-lî hashshîrāh hazzōʾt lĕʿēd bibĕnê yiśrāʾēl — "so that this song may be a witness (ʿēd, H5707) for me against the children of Israel." Torah is also given as a ʿēd-text (v26); the Song doubles the witness-function of the written word. Both testify to the people's covenant obligation and to YHWH's faithfulness and righteousness when judgment comes.
31.20: שָׁמֵן וְשָׂבֵעַ (shāmēn wĕsābēaʿ) — "fat and full": the Deuteronomic prosperity trap stated with characteristic directness — abundance produces the contempt that breaks the covenant. Wĕpānāh ʾel ʾĕlōhîm ʾăḥērîm — "and will turn to other gods": the same verb pānāh (H6437) used of the heart turning away in 30:17. Physical satiation turns the heart from its proper object. The Song will remain as a standing indictment even when the people forget the Law.
31.21: כִּי לֹא תִשָּׁכַח מִפִּי זַרְעוֹ (kî lōʾ tishshākaḥ mippî zarʿô) — "for it will not be forgotten from the mouths of his offspring": the Song's permanence is the ground of its witness-function. While the written law might be lost or ignored, the Song is oral — it lives in the mouth and passes down through recitation. The two witness-structures (written Torah + oral Song) are complementary: Israel may neglect one but the other persists. Kî yādaʿtî ʾet yiṣrô — "for I know their inclination": the divine knowledge of the fallen inclination (yēṣer, H3336 — the formed thing, the bent of the will) grounds the need for the witness-structure.
31.22: וַיִּכְתֹּב מֹשֶׁה (wayyiktōb mōsheh) — "and Moses wrote": the execution of the divine command of v19. Moses writes the Song "that day" (bayyôm hahûʾ) — the immediacy of obedience is a mark of the faithful mediator. The Song's writing is Moses's last recorded act of writing before his death.
31.23: וַיְצַו אֶת יְהוֹשֻׁעַ (wayĕṣaw ʾet yĕhôshûaʿ) — YHWH commissions Joshua directly, not through Moses. The subject is YHWH (note the context: YHWH appeared in the pillar of cloud at v15). The formula ḥăzaq weʾĕmāṣ is now spoken by YHWH himself — the same formula Moses used in 31:6 and 31:7. The divine repetition of the commissioning formula gives it ultimate authority: Joshua's strength-and-courage is not a merely human charge but a divine mandate. Kî ʾattāh tābîʾ ʾet bĕnê yiśrāʾēl ʾel hāʾāreṣ — "for you will bring the children of Israel into the land": the divine certainty of the conquest is built into the commission.
31.24: כְּכַלּוֹת מֹשֶׁה לִכְתֹּב (kĕkallôt mōsheh liktōb) — "when Moses finished writing": kāllāh (H3615) marks the completion as total — not a draft but the finished artifact. The scope — ʾet dibĕrê hattôrāh hazzōʾt ʿal sēper ʿad tummām ("the words of this Torah on a scroll, to their completion") — emphasizes the exhaustiveness of the written deposit. Torah is a completed, bounded text, not an open-ended accumulation.
31.25: הַלְּוִיִּם נֹשְׂאֵי אֲרוֹן בְּרִית יְהוָה (hallĕwiyyîm nōśĕʾê ʾărôn bĕrît Yhwh) — "the Levites who carried the ark of the covenant of YHWH": the ark-carriers are the proper recipients of the Torah scroll. The ark of the covenant (ʾărôn habbĕrît) is the covenant container — it houses the stone tablets; now the Torah scroll is added alongside it. The Levites' role as carrier-priests makes them the covenant custodians in a dual sense: they carry the container of the original Ten Words and now also the full written Torah.
31.26: לָקֹחַ אֵת סֵפֶר הַתּוֹרָה הַזֶּה (lāqōaḥ ʾēt sēper hattôrāh hazzeh) — "take this book of the Torah." The Torah is designated sēper (H5612, scroll/document) — a physical, portable object. Its placement bĕṣad ʾărôn bĕrît Yhwh ("at the side of the ark of the covenant of YHWH") — not inside but alongside — distinguishes it from the stone tablets while giving it covenantal proximity. The stated purpose: wĕhāyāh shām bĕkā lĕʿēd — "and it shall be there in you as a witness." The Torah as witness (ʿēd) against Israel mirrors the Song as witness (v19); the two together form the covenant's double-witness structure (requiring two witnesses for legal testimony, Deut 17:6).
31.27: כִּי אָנֹכִי יָדַעְתִּי אֶת מֶרְיְךָ (kî ʾānōkî yādaʿtî ʾet meryĕkā) — "for I myself know your rebellion." Mĕrî (H4805, rebellion) is a strong term — not merely disobedience but defiant resistance. Moses grounds the need for the double-witness structure (Torah scroll + Song) in his own experiential knowledge of Israel's character: ʿorpĕkem qĕshê hûʾ — "you are stiff-necked" (literally: your neck is hard). The ʿōrep (H6203, nape of the neck) is the part that stiffens when refusing to bow or yield; the idiom captures the posture of the defiant subject before the sovereign.
31.28: הַקְהִילוּ אֵלַי אֶת כָּל זִקְנֵי שִׁבְטֵיכֶם (haqhîlû ʾēlay ʾet kol ziqnê shivṭêkem) — "assemble before me all the elders of your tribes." The formal assembly (qāhal) of elders and officers (shōṭĕrêkem) constitutes the covenant-legal body that can receive the formal witness-declaration. Wĕʾaʿîdāh bām ʾēt hashshāmayim wĕʾet hāʾāreṣ — "and I will cause heaven and earth to witness against them": the same cosmic-witness invocation of 30:19, now formally invoked at the covenant's close.
31.29: כִּי יָדַעְתִּי (kî yādaʿtî) — "for I know": the prophetic-certainty formula. Moses does not merely suspect future apostasy; he declares it as foreknown. Maḥărê môtî hashḥēt tashḥîtûn ("after my death you will surely corrupt yourselves") — the infinitive absolute hashḥēt tashḥîtûn intensifies: "certainly corrupt, utterly go to ruin." Wĕqārāʾt ʾetkem hārāʿāh bĕʾaḥărît hayyāmîm — "and evil will befall you in the latter days": the phrase ʾaḥărît hayyāmîm (H319 + H3117) — "latter days/end of days" — carries eschatological weight throughout the prophets (Isa 2:2; Jer 23:20; Ezek 38:16; Dan 10:14). Moses's farewell points not just to the next generation's apostasy but to the full arc of Israel's covenantal history.
31.30: וַיְדַבֵּר מֹשֶׁה בְּאָזְנֵי כָּל קְהַל יִשְׂרָאֵל (wayĕdabbēr mōsheh bĕʾoznê kol qĕhal yiśrāʾēl) — "and Moses spoke in the ears of all the assembly of Israel." The phrase bĕʾoznê (in the ears of) is the oral-proclamation formula: Torah and Song are not privately transmitted but publicly auditioned. Kol qĕhal yiśrāʾēl — the full assembly as recipient of the Song — closes the narrative-frame of ch 31 and opens the Song of Moses (ch 32), the final and most sustained piece of Mosaic poetry in the Pentateuch.

28.1: shamoa tishma (šāmōaʿ tišmaʿ): the emphatic infinitive absolute + imperfect construction intensifies the condition — 'if you truly, fully, obedient-hearing hear.' This is not cursory compliance but complete covenantal attention. The promised result: YHWH will set you elyon (highest, uppermost) over all nations. The blessing section (vv1-14) frames obedience as the precondition for the blessings that YHWH then actively sends. Passivity on Israel's part (hearing, following) generates divine action (setting high, sending blessings).
28.2: hissigucha (wĕhissîgûkā): 'will overtake you' — the blessings are not merely available but pursuing; they hunt down the obedient like an enemy pursues a retreating army. The same verb (nasag) is used of enemies overtaking Israel in the curse section (v15). Whether you are overtaken by blessings or curses depends entirely on covenantal orientation. The reversal of the verb in v15 makes the structural symmetry explicit: blessings and curses are not different categories but the same covenantal mechanism applied in two directions.
28.3: baruch atta ba'ir uvaruch atta basadeh: 'blessed in the city and blessed in the field' — the spatial blessing covers both domains of Israelite life: the urban (city, commerce, assembly) and the rural (field, agriculture, livestock). No place of Israelite existence falls outside the blessing sphere. The city-field pair is a merism: the whole of life. Verse 16 mirrors this exactly with the curse.
28.4: The three domains of fruitful production: peri bitnecha (fruit of your womb/body — children), peri adamatecha (fruit of your ground — crops), peri behemtecha (fruit of your livestock — herds and flocks). The threefold fruitfulness mirrors the creation mandate (be fruitful, multiply, fill the earth) and the Abrahamic promises. Blessing is never abstract but always bodied — in children, harvests, and animals.
28.5: tene (ṭenĕʾ) and misharetech (mišʾarteṯ): 'basket' and 'kneading bowl' — the two vessels of food processing: the basket that carries grain to market and the bowl that processes it into bread. The blessing reaches into the most domestic and practical points of daily life. No part of the food cycle escapes covenantal significance.
28.6: bevo'acha uvetzetecha (bĕbōʾĕkā ûbĕṣēʾṯĕkā): 'in your coming in and your going out' — the temporal merism completing the spatial one in v3: all activities, all movements, all moments of daily life enclosed within the blessing. This formula appears in the Aaronic blessing structure; it expresses comprehensive coverage rather than specific circumstances.
28.7: negifu lifneicha (nĕgûpîm lipnêkā): 'defeated/struck before you' — the military blessing; YHWH is the primary combatant. The coming-from-one-direction/fleeing-in-seven pattern inverts exactly in v25 for the curse: the seven-to-one ratio in battle describes either overwhelming military dominance or complete military collapse. The number seven marks totality.
28.8: yetzav YHWH ittecha et-habrakha ba'asameicha: 'the LORD will command the blessing to be with you in your storehouses' — blessing is not an impersonal force but a divine command; YHWH orders it to attach to specific locations of human labor and storage. The blessing is commanded (from tzavah), not merely granted or released — a word of royal authority dispatching the blessing to its assigned destination.
28.9: yequimecha YHWH lo le'am qadosh (yĕqîmĕkā Yhwh lô lĕʿam qādôš): 'the LORD will establish you as his holy people' — qadosh (holy, set apart) is not primarily a moral category but a relational one: belonging to YHWH, separated for his purposes. The establishment (qum hiphil) is covenant-constituting action; YHWH makes Israel what they are called to be through the covenant itself. The condition (im tishmor...) grounds holiness in obedience, not in natural or ethnic identity.
28.10: ki shem YHWH nikra alecha (kî šem Yhwh niqrāʾ ʿālêkā): 'because the name of YHWH is called over you' — the name-bearing formula; to have YHWH's name called over you is to belong to him, to bear his identification. The Aaronic blessing ends by YHWH putting his name on Israel (Num 6:27). The result: the nations stand in awe (yare'u). The covenant community's holiness functions as a witness to YHWH's reality among the nations.
28.11: hoterekha YHWH letovah (wĕhôṯirĕkā Yhwh lĕṭôbāh): 'the LORD will make you overflow with good things' — hotir means to leave a surplus, to make abundant; the land gives more than enough. The threefold abundance (body, livestock, ground) repeats the pattern of v4; the Abrahamic promise of multiplication finds agricultural and demographic expression in the land.
28.12: et-otzaro hatov et-hashamayim latet matar artzecha be'ito: 'his good storehouse — the heavens — to give rain to your land in its season' — the heavenly storehouse (otzer): YHWH controls the water supply through a treasury in the sky; rain is not a natural phenomenon but a covenantal allocation. The lend/borrow inversion (you will lend to many, borrow from none) transforms the Exodus refugee into a creditor-nation: abundance that can share its surplus.
28.13: rosh velo zanav (rōʾš wĕlōʾ zānāb): 'head and not tail' — the head-tail image for social and political leadership versus dependency/subjection. The exact reversal in v44 (the foreigner as head, Israel as tail) makes clear what is at stake: covenantal obedience determines whether Israel leads or follows. Isaiah's polemic against Egypt uses the same image (Isa 9:14-15).
28.14: The negative conditional frames the blessing: im lo tasur... yamin usmol ('if you do not turn aside to the right or to the left'). The spatial metaphors of deviation — turning right or left from the covenant path — appear throughout Deuteronomy as the image of apostasy. The specific form of apostasy here: going after other gods to serve them. Covenant faithfulness is monotheistic fidelity in embodied practice.
28.15: im-lo tishma beqol YHWH (ʾim-lōʾ tišmaʿ bĕqôl Yhwh): 'if you do not listen to the voice of YHWH' — the curse-section opens with the precise inversion of the blessing-condition: where v1 says shamoa tishma (if you truly hear), v15 says im-lo tishma (if you do not hear). The structural parallel is deliberate; the same covenant mechanism operates in reverse. The curses will come upon you and overtake you (uvisigucha) — the same overtaking-verb as the blessings (v2), now carrying curses.
28.16: The curse-mirror of v3: arur atta ba'ir ve'arur atta basadeh ('cursed in the city and cursed in the field'). The identical spatial merism now bears the covenant curse. The symmetry is perfect: the same verse structure, the same coverage of all space, the same comprehensive claim — reversed in valence. The curse is not a different covenant but the same covenant's opposite pole.
28.17: The curse-mirror of v5: basket and kneading bowl cursed. Where the blessing reaches into the vessels of food production, the curse contaminates those same vessels. The domestic and practical is never neutral in Deuteronomy; it is always covenantally charged.
28.18: The curse-mirror of v4: womb, ground, herds, flocks — all three domains of fruitfulness now under the curse. Miscarriage, drought, livestock death — the Deuteronomic curses will be fulfilled in Israel's historical experience. The agricultural failures of the prophetic era read as the specific actualization of this verse.
28.19: The curse-mirror of v6: cursed coming in and cursed going out. The temporal merism of all activity now under the curse. No movement, no undertaking, no moment of daily life escapes the curse's reach — exactly as no moment escaped the blessing's reach. The totality is symmetrical.
28.20: meera (mĕʾērāh): 'disaster/curse' — not the standard arar (curse) but the noun meera, the curse-substance itself; mehumah (mĕhûmāh): 'confusion, panic'; migeeret (migĕreʿeṯ): 'rebuke, frustration' — three negative forces YHWH dispatches against the apostate. The root cause: asher azavtani ('because you abandoned me') — the curse is named as the consequence of relational abandonment, not mere rule-violation.
28.21: yadvek YHWH becha et-hadaver (yadbiqqĕkā Yhwh bāddāber): 'the LORD will cling to you with plague/pestilence' — the irony of davaq here: the same verb used for the covenant-love of cleaving (Deut 10:20, 11:22) now applied to plague clinging to the covenant-breaker. What should have been covenant-adhesion (cleaving to YHWH) becomes plague-adhesion (pestilence cleaving to Israel).
28.22: The catalog of natural afflictions — shachefet (tuberculosis/wasting disease), qaddachat (fever), dalleket (inflammation), charcharah (scorching heat), cherev (drought/sword), shiddafon (blight), yerakon (mildew/jaundice). The seven afflictions form a complete curse-catalog; the natural world itself becomes YHWH's instrument of covenant enforcement.
28.23: venehyu shameicha asher al-roshecha nechoshet veha'aretz asher tachteicha barzel: 'the sky above you will be bronze and the ground beneath you iron' — two of the most prized metals (used for weapons and tools) become images of impenetrability: the sky seals shut against rain, the ground hardens against cultivation. The productivity of v7-12 (from YHWH's treasure-storehouse to the earth's abundance) is reversed: both sky and ground become barriers rather than channels of provision.
28.24: yeiten YHWH et-matar artzecha avaq ve'afar: 'the LORD will turn the rain of your land into dust and powder' — the inversion of the agricultural covenant; instead of water from the heavenly storehouse, dust falls. The dust-rain is both literal (drought causing topsoil loss) and covenantally symbolic: the land given to Abraham's seed returns to the pre-creation condition of formlessness (tohu).
28.25: The military inversion of v7: yittencha YHWH niguph lifnei oyeveika ('the LORD will cause you to be struck/routed before your enemies'). Where v7 says enemies flee in seven directions, here Israel flees in seven directions from enemies. Vezayat lizivaah ('you will become a thing of horror'): za'avah is a trembling/shaking caused by terror; Israel becomes the object that causes nations to shudder in horror — not from awe but from revulsion at their fate.
28.26: vehayta nivlatecha lema'achal le'of hashamayim ulivhemot ha'aretz: 'your bodies will become food for birds of the sky and animals of the earth' — burial was a fundamental covenant marker; the unburied corpse was the ultimate disgrace and the sign of covenant abandonment. The prophets use this image repeatedly for Jerusalem's judgment (Jer 7:33, 8:1-2; Ezek 29:5). The absence of a burier (ein macharid) marks total social collapse.
28.27: yakkecha YHWHbishchin Mitzraim: 'the LORD will strike you with the boils of Egypt' — Egypt's diseases returning to the people YHWH brought out of Egypt; the covenant reversal takes the form of a return to the condition of pre-redemption. The diseases YHWH inflicted on Egypt for refusing to release Israel now fall on Israel for refusing covenant fidelity.
28.28: shigga'on (šiggāʿôn): 'madness'; ivaron (ʿiwwārôn): 'blindness'; timhon levav (timmāhôn lēbāb): 'confusion/bewilderment of heart/mind' — the cognitive and psychological curses; not only external disaster but internal disintegration. The mind that refused covenant wisdom now loses coherent reality-apprehension. Isaiah 59:10 echoes this verse directly.
28.29: umishshesh batzohorayim ka'asher yemashesh ha'iver ba'afelah: 'at midday you will grope like a blind man in the dark' — the paradox of midday blindness: the one who should have covenant clarity (standing in full light of YHWH's revelation) gropes in darkness. The three verbs of failure: lo tatliach (you will not succeed), ashak ve'gazul (you will be oppressed and robbed), ein moshia (no one to save). The absence of a savior (moshia) is the covenant curse's most pointed dimension: the one who is not saved is the one who abandoned the Savior.
28.30: The dispossession triad: wife taken by another man, house built but not inhabited, vineyard planted but fruit not enjoyed. Techallelnah (tĕḥallelennāh): 'will violate/defile her' — sexual violation is the first in the list, the most intimate sphere of life. Then shelter (house), then sustenance (vineyard). The progressive loss moves from the most personal to the material; the covenant-breaker becomes an alien in his own construction.
28.31: Agricultural dispossession continues: ox slaughtered before your eyes but you eat none, donkey seized and not returned, sheep surrendered to enemies with no rescuer. The animals that worked the land are taken; the livestock that marked prosperity are stripped. The seeing-without-receiving pattern recurs — lefaneicha (before your eyes) — making the loss a witnessed experience, not a hidden one.
28.32: baneicha u-venoteicha nettunim le'am acher: 'your sons and daughters given to another people' — the generational horror of the exile; children given to foreigners while parents watch helplessly (ve'eineicha ro'ot vekalot aleihem kol-hayom, 'your eyes looking and longing for them all day long'). Ve'ein le'el yadecha ('and there is nothing your hand can do') — the covenant curse strips power precisely from those who used power autonomously rather than dependently. The Babylonian exile is the historical fulfillment.
28.33: peri adamatecha vekhol-yegiecha tokhal am asher lo-yadatah: 'a people you did not know will eat the fruit of your ground and all your labor' — the productivity that YHWH blessed in vv1-14 is consumed by an unknown foreign nation. The fruit of the land and the labor of hands — the two components of prosperity — become provision for enemies. Deuteronomy's economic theology: what is received as gift can be taken as judgment.
28.34: vehayita meshugga (wĕhāyîṯā mĕšuggāʿ): 'you will be driven mad by what you see' — meshugga is the state of madness from sustained horror; the seeing-without-rescue pattern (begun in vv30-33) generates madness. The covenant curse works through psychological devastation as much as material loss: the mind breaks under the accumulation of witnessed helplessness.
28.35: The boil-curse becomes comprehensive: mishoch raglecha ve'ad kodkodecha ('from the sole of your foot to the crown of your head') — the full-body coverage; no inch of the physical person escapes. Job's condition (Job 2:7) is an individual parallel; the Deuteronomic curse applies this body-devastation nationally. The incurability is emphasized: lo tukhal lehirapha ('you will not be able to be healed').
28.36: yolikha YHWH ve'et-malkecha asher taqim alecha: 'the LORD will drive you and the king you set over you' — the king is specifically included; covenant curse falls on the entire institutional structure, including the monarchy. El-goy asher lo-yadatah atah va'avoteicha ('to a nation unknown to you or your fathers') — the exile destination is described by its foreignness; Babylon exceeds all Israel's prior experience. There they will serve wood-and-stone gods — the ultimate covenant inversion: the people of the living God worshipping inert material.
28.37: mashal ushinah vetanah: 'a byword, a mocking taunt, and a proverb' — three words for mockery; Israel-in-exile becomes the reference point for disaster, the go-to illustration of catastrophe among nations. This is the inverse of v10 (nations in awe of Israel bearing YHWH's name): rather than inspiring fear-reverence, Israel now inspires ridicule. The covenant's public witness function works in both directions.
28.38: The agricultural curses begin — sowing much, harvesting little (zera rav totzi hasadeh ume'at ta'asof ki yachselen haarbe): the locust-curse. Every agricultural investment returns diminished; the land that was to give abundantly becomes a ground of futility. Haggai 1:6 echoes this precise pattern as the condition of the post-exilic community still under covenant judgment.
28.39: The vine-curse: planting vineyards but drinking no wine, gathering no grapes, because worms eat them. The shamir (worm/grub) consumes from within what external enemies could not reach. Internal corruption mirrors internal covenantal failure; the covenant-breaker's crop is eaten from the inside.
28.40: The olive-curse: olive trees throughout the land but no oil to use, because olives fall prematurely. Oil was fuel, cosmetic, and medicinal; the olive curse strikes health, light, and festivity simultaneously. The yishol (shall shed/drop off) is the term for premature falling — the fruit never matures.
28.41: The generational curse returns: sons and daughters, but not kept — ki yelchu bashvi ('because they go into captivity'). The most fundamental expression of covenant blessing — children to carry forward the name and the land — becomes the most pointed curse: children taken into captivity. The Babylonian exile is precisely this: the generation born in the land taken to the empire.
28.42: The locust-comprehensive curse: kol-etzecha u-feri admatecha ('all your trees and the fruit of your ground') — total agricultural devastation. Verses 38-42 form a systematic elimination of the seven-species abundance promised in ch 8: every productive category of the land stripped. Joel 1 describes the locust-plague as covenant curse in its full force.
28.43: hager asher beqirbecha ya'aleh alecha ma'la ma'la: 'the foreigner living among you will rise higher and higher' — the exact social inversion of the covenant blessing. Verse 12 promised Israel would lend to nations, borrow from none; v43-44 reverses this: the resident foreigner (ger) becomes the creditor, Israel the debtor. Atah tashkeh shafal shafal ('you will sink lower and lower') — the superlative doubled: absolute social descent.
28.44: The head-tail reversal completing v13: the ger (foreigner) as head, Israel as tail. The social order ordained in the covenant (Israel leading, nations following) inverts completely under the curse. This is not merely economic reversal but covenantal testimony: the watching nations see Israel under a curse more severe than any they bear.
28.45: uba'u alecha kol-haqelalot ha'eleh: 'all these curses will come upon you and pursue and overtake you' — the summary verse; the same overtaking-language as the blessing-summary (v2). The curses pursue with the same relentless energy as the blessings would have; there is no escape, only the question of which set of covenantal consequences pursues you. Ad hishamedeha ('until you are destroyed') — the terminal point of the curse-sequence is extinction from the land.
28.46: vehayu vecha le'ot ulemo'fat (wĕhāyû bĕkā lĕʾôṯ ûlĕmôpēṯ): 'they will be a sign and a wonder in you' — the covenant curses become otot umoftim, signs and wonders, in/on Israel. These are the same terms for the Exodus miracles (Exod 7:3; Deut 4:34): YHWH's signs and wonders brought Israel out of Egypt; the curses become counter-signs and counter-wonders that testify to covenant-judgment as clearly as the Exodus testified to covenant-salvation. The watching nations read Israel's devastation as divine text.
28.47: tachat asher lo avadeta et-YHWH Eloheicha besimcha uvtuv levav: 'because you did not serve the LORD your God with joy and gladness of heart' — this is the most theologically penetrating verse in the curse section: the root of all the curses is not spectacular apostasy but joyless religion. The covenant life is meant to be served (aved) with simcha ve-tuv levav (joy and goodness of heart); when prosperity becomes grounds for obligation without gratitude, the spiral begins. Paul's godliness with contentment is great gain (1 Tim 6:6) is the NT reformulation of this principle.
28.48: ol barzel (ʿōl barzel): 'an iron yoke' — the military subjugation described in material terms; the iron yoke (versus the wooden yoke of lighter vassalage) represents permanent and unbreakable servitude. The four conditions of the enemy's service — hunger, thirst, nakedness, and want (bera'av uvattzama uveirummah uvechesar kol) — are the precise inversion of the abundance promised in vv1-14 (eating to full, clothed, no want). Babylon as iron yoke is Jeremiah's primary metaphor (Jer 28:13-14).
28.49: yissa YHWH alecha goy merachok: 'the LORD will bring against you a nation from far away' — the eagle-nation (ka'asher yide'ah nesher, 'swooping like an eagle'). Babylon is consistently described as an eagle in the prophets (Jer 48:40; Ezek 17:3); the Babylonian standards bore eagle imagery. Asher lo-tishma leshono ('whose language you will not understand') — linguistic alienation as a dimension of the covenant curse; Isa 28:11 and 33:19 use incomprehensible speech as a sign of judgment.
28.50: goy az panim (gôy ʿaz pānîm): 'a fierce/brazen-faced nation' — shameless, unrestrained in cruelty. Asher lo-yissa panim lezaqen ve'naar lo yachanun ('who shows no respect to the old or mercy to the young') — the complete violation of natural social order; both the elderly (deserving respect) and the young (deserving protection) are equally unprotected. Babylon's siege warfare fulfilled this precisely.
28.51: The comprehensive agricultural stripping: grain (dagan), new wine (tirosh), olive oil (yitzhar), livestock calves and lambs — every category of the seven-species abundance systematically taken. The list of what the enemy will take is the exact reverse of what YHWH's abundance provided. Deuteronomy's agricultural theology is concrete: the specific bounties that obedience unlocks are the specific provisions that disobedience removes.
28.52: The siege of the fortified cities — the chomot gebohot u-vetzu'ot ('high, fortified walls') in which Israel trusted prove inadequate. The trust-in-walls is itself a form of covenant displacement: trusting the city's defenses rather than YHWH's protection. Jeremiah's temple-sermon (Jer 7:4, 'these are deceptive words: the temple of YHWH') applies the same logic to the temple as a false-security object.
28.53: ve'akhalta pri bitnecha: 'you will eat the fruit of your womb' — the most horrific curse in Deuteronomy: cannibalism of one's own children in the siege's final extremity. Peri bitna (fruit of her womb) is used in v4 for the supreme blessing; here the same phrase describes the ultimate curse. The historical fulfillment occurred at the fall of Jerusalem (Lam 4:10; 2 Kgs 6:28-29). The covenant's comprehensive claim on human existence means that its most extreme curses reach into the most intimate biological relationships.
28.54: The siege-stinginess (ra ayin, 'evil/grudging eye') of the most refined man — the rak ve-anugah ('tender and delicate') who in normal life is generous now refuses to share even with his beloved wife and children. The curses strip social and familial bonds: prosperity enables generosity; the extremity of the curse makes even the most civilized man a hoarder of horror.
28.55: The inversion of covenant sharing: the tender man refuses to share the flesh he is eating — his own child's flesh — because ein lo kol ('he has nothing left'). The scarcity generated by the siege reverses every impulse of familial affection. Lamentations 4:3-10 describes precisely this horror as the fulfillment of these curses in 586 BCE.
28.56: The raka veha'anugah ('delicate and sensitive woman') — the one so refined she never placed her sole on the ground — counterpart to v54's man. The covenant curse reaches the most sheltered and protected member of society; no social insulation prevents the curse's penetration.
28.57: The woman's extremity: eating the afterbirth (shiltah) and the children she bears (biladeiha asher teled) in secret, because of the siege's absolute need. The secrecy — beseter — indicates that even in the horror she knows the act is abominable; the curse has not removed moral awareness but has overwhelmed moral capacity. This is the absolute nadir of the curse-sequence: the covenant community that was to eat the covenant meal in joy has been reduced to consuming its own progeny in secrecy.
28.58: et-hashem hanichbad ve-hanora hazeh et-YHWH Eloheicha: 'this glorious and awesome name — the LORD your God' — the pivot verse; the basis of the curse is explicit: failure to fear this name. The nora (awesome, feared) name is the one that the covenant community should have feared; instead they feared other gods. Isaiah 57:11 uses the same vocabulary: 'whom have you so feared and dreaded that you have not been faithful to me?' The awesome name demands a corresponding awe-response; its absence generates all that precedes in this chapter.
28.59: peliot (pĕliʾōṯ): 'extraordinary, wondrous/severe' — the curses become exceptional in their intensity and duration (makot gedolot ve'ne'emanot vecholayim ra'im ve'ne'emanim: 'great and persistent plagues and terrible and persistent diseases'). The word ne'eman (faithful, persistent) normally describes YHWH's faithfulness to the covenant; here it describes the faithful persistence of judgment. The same divine reliability that sustains blessing sustains curse.
28.60: veheshiv becha et kol madveh Mitzraim: 'he will bring back to you all the diseases of Egypt' — the return of Egypt's plagues on Israel. The exodus was from Egypt's diseases (madveh); covenant apostasy reverses the direction: the diseases return with the people. The feared diseases of Egypt (asher yagortah, 'which you dreaded') were Israel's pre-covenant experience; they become post-apostasy reality. The restoration of pre-exodus conditions is the covenant curse's historical logic.
28.61: gam kol-choli ve'kol-makkah asher lo katub besefer ha-Torah hazeh: 'every sickness and plague not recorded in this Book of the Law' — the curse is not limited to the specific items enumerated; YHWH reserves the right to execute covenant justice beyond the enumerated list. The sefer ha-Torah (Book of the Law) is here mentioned as the covenant document; the written text is the basis of covenant accountability. Deuteronomy's self-awareness as a written document is explicit.
28.62: venishaartem bimtei me'at tachat asher heyitem kekochvei hashamayim larov: 'you who were as numerous as the stars will be left few in number' — the Abrahamic promise reversed. Genesis 15:5 promised Abraham's descendants as numerous as the stars; Deuteronomy 28:62 describes the same stellar analogy now marking the before-state of a people reduced to remnant by covenant curse. The population arithmetic of divine faithfulness (multiplication in blessing, reduction in judgment) is one of the most concrete ways the OT speaks about theological realities.
28.63: ka'asher sas YHWH aleichem leheitiv etkhem uleharboth etkhem ken yasis YHWH aleichem le'havid etkhem velehashmiid etkhem: 'just as the LORD delighted in making you prosperous and multiplying you, so he will delight in bringing you to ruin and destruction' — the startling covenant symmetry: YHWH's delight (sus) operates in both directions. The same divine pleasure that drove the blessing program drives the curse program; YHWH is not reluctantly cursing but covenantally delighted in covenant-consistency. This is the theological paradox that the prophets wrestle with: YHWH's wrath is not his reluctant default but his active, covenant-consistent will.
28.64: vehefiztecha YHWH bekhol-ha'ammim miqtzeh ha'aretz ve'ad qetzeh ha'aretz: 'the LORD will scatter you among all peoples from one end of the earth to the other' — the Diaspora as divine decree. The scattering (hefitz) is YHWH's action, not merely a political accident. The wood-and-stone gods of v36 recur here: in exile, Israel will serve the gods they chose over YHWH — the covenant curse is that YHWH gives them what they wanted. Romans 1:24-28 applies this same logic to idolatry.
28.65: lo-tashqot velo-yihyeh manoach lechaf raglecha: 'you will find no rest and no resting place for the sole of your foot' — the anti-Sabbath; where the covenant promised rest (menucha, Deut 3:20, 12:9-10), the curse denies rest entirely. The psychological dimensions: lev ragaz (trembling/anxious heart), kilyon einayim (failing eyes), deva'on nefesh (anguish/pining of soul) — the whole inner person disintegrated.
28.66: vechayu chayeicha taluyim lecha mineged: 'your life will hang before you in the balance' — the constant uncertainty of physical survival under oppression; the covenant community that had assured inheritance now has no certainty of tomorrow. Ufachdata laylah veyomam velo ta'amin bechayeicha ('you will dread night and day and have no assurance of your life') — the existential anxiety that replaces covenant security.
28.67: The morning-evening wish-reversal: baboker tomar mi-yitein erev ('in the morning you will say: if only it were evening') and ba'erev tomar mi-yitein boker ('in the evening you will say: if only it were morning') — the complete temporal desolation. No part of the day brings relief; each time-horizon holds terror. This is the exact opposite of Psalm 30:5's weeping may endure for a night, but joy comes in the morning — under the curse, neither morning nor evening brings joy.
28.68: ve'etichem YHWH Mitzraimah ba'oniyot: 'the LORD will send you back to Egypt in ships' — the definitive covenant reversal: the Exodus undone. Going back to Egypt by ships intensifies the reversal; the Exodus was a land journey, miraculously through the sea; the return is a maritime deportation, a slave cargo. Ve-hitnmakhartem sham le'oyeveichem la'avadim ule'shifchot ve'ein qoneh ('you will offer yourselves for sale as slaves, but no one will buy you') — the ultimate degradation: not even worth the price of a slave.

29.1: eleh divrei ha-berit asher tzivvah YHWH et-Mosheh liwrot im-benei Yisrael be'eretz Mo'av: 'these are the words of the covenant the LORD commanded Moses to make with the Israelites in the land of Moab' — the Moab covenant is explicitly identified as levad ha-berit asher karat itam be-Chorev ('in addition to the covenant made with them at Horeb'). Not a replacement but a supplement; the covenantal structure grows but the Sinai foundation remains. The treaty-renewal form is attested in ancient Near Eastern treaty practice: covenants could be renewed or extended without canceling the original.
29.2: atem re'item (ʾattem rĕʾîtem): 'you have seen with your own eyes' — eyewitness testimony as the basis for covenant accountability; what Israel has seen constitutes their epistemic obligation. The appeal to ra'ah (to see, to witness) recurs throughout Deuteronomy as the covenant's evidentiary foundation. Moses summarizes the entire Egypt-to-Moab experience as what they have seen: they cannot claim ignorance.
29.3: ha-massot ha-gedolot ha'otot ve-ha-mofiim ha-gedolim ha-hem: 'the great trials, the signs and wonders' — the standard Exodus vocabulary: massot (tests/trials, from the same root as Massah — testing-place), otot (signs), mofiim (wonders). The covenant is founded on demonstrated divine power; the signs and wonders are not incidental accompaniments but constitutive covenant-establishing events. They cannot be unknown; they were asher ra'u eineicha ('which your eyes have seen').
29.4: velo-natan YHWH lachem lev lada'at ve'einayim lir'ot ve'oznaim lishmo'a ad hayom hazeh: 'yet the LORD has not given you a heart to understand or eyes to see or ears to hear, to this very day' — the most anthropologically striking verse in Deuteronomy 29. Despite seeing the Exodus miracles (vv2-3), Israel lacks the divinely given perceptual apparatus to understand what they witnessed. Paul quotes this verse in Romans 11:8 (citing a combination of Deut 29:4 and Isa 29:10) to explain Israel's partial hardening. The natan (given) makes YHWH the agent of both revelation and non-understanding; the capacity to receive revelation is itself a divine gift.
29.5: The wilderness provision repeated in summary: forty years, clothing that did not wear out, sandals that did not wear out (na'alechem lo valu me'al ragleikem). The miracle of preservation is the covenant's educational program (lema'an tede'u ki Ani YHWH Eloheichem, 'so that you would know that I am the LORD your God'). The purpose of miracle is da'at YHWH — knowing YHWH — not abstract information but relational covenant knowledge.
29.6: lechem lo akhally veyayin ve-shechar lo shtitem: 'you ate no bread and drank no wine or strong drink' — the wilderness diet defined by what it lacked; Israel's food was manna, water from the rock, not normal agricultural produce. The absence of normal food and drink is itself a theological statement: Israel lived on divine provision alone, not on their own agricultural labor. This is the preparatory education for the land's abundance: dependence before possession.
29.7: The military history condensed: Sihon of Heshbon and Og of Bashan came out to fight and were defeated. The covenant-victories over Transjordanian kings are the concrete evidence of YHWH's covenant-fighting on Israel's behalf; they are cited here as the recent historical foundation of covenant confidence.
29.8: The distribution of Transjordanian territory: Reuben, Gad, and half-Manasseh. The giving of the land (veniten et-artzam lenachalah) as a covenant inheritance is already partially fulfilled east of the Jordan; it grounds the expectation of what lies ahead. Covenant promise becomes covenant history in stages.
29.9: u-shmartem et-divrei ha-berit hazot va'asitem otam lema'an taskilu et kol asher ta'asun: 'observe this covenant and do it so that you may be successful in everything you do' — taskilU from sakal (to be wise, to prosper): covenant observance produces sekhel (wisdom, prudent success). This echoes the promise of Joshua 1:8 (if the Book of the Law does not depart from your mouth, you will make your way prosperous and be successful). Covenant observance and practical wisdom are not separable categories.
29.10: atem nitzavim hayom kulkhem lifnei YHWH Eloheichem: 'you are all standing today before the LORD your God' — the covenant assembly; the full text of what 'all' means follows: leaders, elders, officials, all the men of Israel (v10-11). The spatial standing before YHWH (lifnei YHWH) is the covenant ceremony; the whole community becomes a legal party to the covenant.
29.11: tapkhem nesheichem ve-gercha asher bekerev machaneicha: 'your children, your wives, and the foreigners in your camp' — the covenant assembly extends to the marginalized: women, children, and the ger (resident alien). Even the wood-choppers and water-carriers (chotech etzeicha shov meimicha) are included; no functional level of Israelite society is excluded from covenant membership.
